## pqac-00000004

From text 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5'.

Hebi Li, Youbiao He, Qi Xiao, Jin Tian, and Forrest Sheng Bao. Bhdl: a lucid, expressive, and embedded programming language and system for pcb designs. 2021 58th ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC), pages 355-360, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086, doi:10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes BHDL as an embedded circuit DSL that uses structural/compositional syntax ("series and parallel syntax") and supports "in-place part creation" (anonymous components, loops and conditionals) so users can programmatically generate repeated circuitry. BHDL provides formal reduction semantics for nets and composition (e.g., "The nets(xi.R, xj.L) means a netlist consisting of two pins" and net unions like "y.nets ⇝N1 ∪N2"), indicating it derives netlists from circuit clauses. Layout is expressed with hierarchical "layout combinators" such as hstack/vstack and spatial operators ("at deﬁnes the offset (x, y) between two parts.") and example layouts appear as nested forms like "(hstack (vstack btn1 btn2 btn3) esp01)". BHDL embeds in a host language ("The BHDL circuit is a ﬁrst-class value inside the host language"), allowing parameterized variants (examples show different behavior for "Debug" vs "Release"). Part/footprint management uses a "footprint" clause and "import" to bind package names and pin orders to BHDL parts. For placement, the system applies a simple padding heuristic ("padding parameter that enlarge components’ footprints") and a detailed placer using simulated annealing ("We also implement a simulated annealing (SA) detailed placer"), implying outputs include netlists, mapped footprints/pads, and auto-placed layouts derived from code.

## pqac-00000005

From text 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-3'.

Hebi Li, Youbiao He, Qi Xiao, Jin Tian, and Forrest Sheng Bao. Bhdl: a lucid, expressive, and embedded programming language and system for pcb designs. 2021 58th ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC), pages 355-360, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086, doi:10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086. This article has 1 citations.

From the excerpt, BHDL is a domain-specific, embedded programming language for describing PCB circuits and their physical layouts. Users write BHDL code that declares parts (including anonymous in-place parts), pins, and connections using compositional wire/connection primitives: net (list-of-pins producing a conventional netlist), series (connect parts in series by left/right pins), parallel (connect parts in parallel), and bus (connect corresponding pins across parts, with placeholders supported). These syntactic constructs have formal semantics that generate the connectivity information (i.e., netlists) and can be nested to express complex topologies concisely. For physical placement, BHDL supplies relative layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) and a hierarchical layout model: sub-circuits can be laid out and then composed into larger layouts using spatial relations like “left of” and “above.” The language therefore takes as input textual BHDL descriptions (parts, pins, connection expressions, and layout combinators) and produces structured circuit connectivity (netlist/schematic representations) and hierarchical, auto-placed layouts derived from the layout combinators. The excerpt does not list exact exported file formats or downstream EDA artifacts.

## pqac-00000006

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 48-50'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes an HCL-based design flow (Polymorphic Circuit Blocks) where designers write system-architecture-level descriptions (block diagrams) in a hardware description language. The HCL uses subcircuit generator libraries: blocks can be defined as executable generators that translate high-level specifications into concrete implementations (for example computing resistor values from input voltage). The language supports polymorphism and ambiguity (generic parts vs. specific part numbers) so designs can be refined progressively. HCL designs are elaborated into a hierarchical block-graph, expanded with community libraries, and refined interactively in a GUI; parameters are automatically propagated and checked for consistency. The primary output described is a netlist file that can be exported and imported into conventional board layout tools. The system is presented as an end-to-end authoring approach but the excerpt notes the GUI refinement and export-to-netlist step rather than fully automated placement/routing; actual auto-placed PCB layouts are not explicitly described in this passage.

## pqac-00000007

From text 'ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 50-52'.

R Ramesh. Better embedded design tools with automated reasoning. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes a generator-style hardware description language (HDL) implemented as a Python-embedded domain-specific language that authors parameterized blocks (templates/classes) programmatically. It separates interface from implementation by translating high-level inputs into internal parameters: examples include an LED-resistor generator that computes a resistor value from input voltage and an RC filter block that computes R and C from cutoff frequency and impedance. The system includes an electronics model (ports, links, signal types) and a library of primitives and higher-level blocks derived from parts tables (E24 series, capacitors, inductors, etc.). Port parameters propagate through links to form aggregate net properties (e.g., total current, acceptable voltage range), and links can assert correctness constraints. Library blocks produced by the HDL provide design automation and can be dropped into system-level descriptions. From this, inputs to the HDL are high-level block parameters, class/port/link definitions, and design constraints; outputs are instantiated, parameterized block implementations and library components (with selected part values and propagated net parameters) that enable downstream artifacts such as schematic-level blocks and machine-readable component selections.

## pqac-00000008

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen as an LLM-based framework that translates natural-language PCB schematic specifications into SKiDL code. The authors choose SKiDL because Python-based hardware description languages provide high information density and align with LLM pretraining. The pipeline uses carefully designed prompts, a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) that encodes essential device rules and constraints (compressing typical ~16k-token datasheets down to ~300 tokens per component), and a multi-stage verification loop combining electrical design rules and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) topology checks. Pin semantics are abstracted via a labeling function mapping pins to one of 36 roles to decouple verification from vendor naming. Correctly generated SKiDL produces KiCAD schematic files and KiCAD can then generate PCB layout files ready for routing. The system provides verification feedback to the LLM for iterative refinement. The excerpt does not mention pcbGPT or describe its approach, so comparisons or pcbGPT-specific details are not present here.

This excerpt (Vassallo & Bajada, DATE 2024) describes an end-to-end RL approach to PCB component placement that frames placement as an MDP and trains agents (TD3 and SAC). On unseen circuits, their policies outperform state-of-the-art simulated annealing baselines, producing average post-routing wirelength reductions of 17% (TD3) and 21% (SAC). The authors report emergent collaborative/competitive behaviours and substantially faster placement convergence in some cases, “sometimes exceeding an order of magnitude.” Their state representation uses image-processing: grayscale layered images of the netlist (0.5 mm resolution) plus per-component masks for local overlap and line-of-sight detection. They provide code, datasets, and pre-generated results on GitHub. The excerpt contains no performance numbers, benchmarks, or success rates for HiePlace or Li’s double-layer placement algorithm; additional sources are needed to compare those methods and to obtain their reported metrics and benchmarks.

## pqac-00000022

From text 'vashisht2020placementinintegrated pages 5-7'.

Dhruv Vashisht, Harshit Rampal, Haiguang Liao, Yang Lu, Devika Shanbhag, Elias Fallon, and Levent Burak Kara. Placement in integrated circuits using cyclic reinforcement learning and simulated annealing. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.07577, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.07577. This article has 62 citations.

This excerpt reports results from a paper that combines deep reinforcement learning (RL) and simulated annealing (SA) in a cyclic framework for placement (Sequence Pair representation). Training/testing settings: ami49 was trained for 10 epochs with the agent taking 200 RL steps followed by 5000 SA steps; lattice experiments ran 15 epochs with 200 RL steps and 5000 SA steps per epoch. Runtime: each training epoch averages 17 seconds; after training, RL+SA takes ~17 seconds to run 5000 steps to find the final solution, while pure SA takes ~16 seconds for 5000 steps. Results (averages over 10 runs) show RL-initialized SA outperforms random initialization (pure SA) on lattice benchmarks: for 100 blocks RL init cost 3,386 µm vs random 3,532 µm; for 225 blocks 14,348 vs 14,844. On ami49 (10-run averages), RL init yields lower area costs: with fixed blocks 49 mm2 vs 53 mm2; without fixed blocks 43 mm2 vs 46 mm2. The paper reports smaller standard deviations for RL initialization, and concludes the cyclic RL+SA framework provides better initializations and lower-cost placements than pure SA.

Shanyi Li, Zhen Zhuang, Mingyu Liu, Weihua Sheng, Bei Yu, and Tsung-Yi Ho. Hieplace: efficient hierarchical pcb placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3580511, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3580511. This article has 2 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes HiePlace as an MP-based, hierarchical PCB placement framework targeting PCB-specific constraints and metrics (noting first-priority components and core chips). It introduces two acceleration techniques: DP for first-priority component placement and a relaxation algorithm that reduces Boolean variables and the solution space. Routability was compared against Floorplet using metrics such as estimated HPWL for first-priority components/core chips, misaligned regions (MC), and net crossings; the experiments reported use the first 10 PCB designs with feasible placements. Reported results include an average reduction of 16% in wirelength and 46% in misaligned regions versus the baseline, plus significant runtime improvements and lower costs. HiePlace also reportedly optimized PCB-specific design metrics. The paper notes remaining limitations: routability evaluation (currently treated as a superposition of indicators like net intersection and alignment) and lack of double-sided layout support, with plans to extend the model and include quick routing feasibility checks and interlayer cost handling in future work.

## pqac-00000024

This article has 2 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the HiePlace implementation and experimental setup. The implementation is in C++ and the MP-based subproblem is solved via Gurobi. The authors evaluate on 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industry, 4 academic), with density defined as sum of component areas over the irregular outline area; PCB8 (141 components) is used for convergence studies. Reported metrics include HPWLall (half-perimeter wirelength), Area Ratio (computed by a sweepline algorithm), Cost (defined by equation (17)), alignment-path count, and runtime. Key empirical findings: HiePlace converges rapidly (near-optimal within ~10 min), scales to large cases (most significant cases finish in <2 h), and outperforms prior MP-based and Quilter baselines. On average it reduces cost by 66% and 18% versus two baselines while using only 13% of their runtime. Versus Quilter on four academic benchmarks, cost reductions range 76.7%–91.3% (e.g., bm4 from 19.46×10^3 to 3.58×10^3) and runtime drops 99.4%–99.9% (Quilter: 34–75 min; HiePlace: a few seconds). Improvements in routability, fewer net crossings, and better alignment are also reported.

The HiePlace input description includes an irregular fixed outline, a set of modules M (containing chips U and components C), per-component pins P with fixed coordinates, a netlist N, preplaced components with fixed corner coordinates, obstacle blocks, and spacing constraints between components and the outline. The desired output is a detailed placement that places chips and components within the outline. Objectives are to minimize weighted wirelength and white-space ratio, reduce the number of misaligned areas (alignment constraint), and minimize net crossings for high-priority components; same-layer chip-to-component connections are preferred to reduce drilling and improve routability. Preprocessing handles irregular outlines ("To fill the irregular outline, we employ five blocks") and stacked/irregular components via a subdivision strategy and a cohesion constraint to keep subcomponents' relative coordinates. Methodologically, HiePlace adopts an MP-based approach converting constraints to mathematical formulas and introduces a novel grouping algorithm that clusters highly correlated components to improve alignment and reduce solution space/runtime. The excerpt reports qualitative improvements (better alignment, improved routability, minimal change to overall wirelength) and shows example results (Fig. 2(d)), but it does not provide numeric benchmarks or detailed quantitative results in the provided pages.

Grouping formulas (Chnew ≡ Chorigin × Vcnt + sp × (Vcnt −1)) and grouping/spacing rules are provided. The excerpt does not include reported results, performance metrics, or benchmark comparisons.

## pqac-00000028

From text 'li2026pcbbenchbenchmarkingllms pages 2-4'.

J Li, L Chen, B Yang, J Zhu, Y Wang, and Y Ma. Pcb-bench: benchmarking llms for printed circuit board placement and routing. Unknown journal, 2026.

PCB-Bench evaluates LLMs/MLLMs on three complementary PCB tasks and modalities: (1) large-scale text-to-text QA and choice questions (about 1,800 free-form QAs with corresponding CQs, ~3,700 questions total) to test generative and factual reasoning; (2) image-and-text multimodal reasoning (about 500 questions) pairing natural language prompts with PCB placement images; and (3) real-world PCB design comprehension using over 170 complete designs (schematic diagrams, design intents, component libraries, placement and routing files) for schematic-to-description tasks. The benchmark explicitly measures generative output with BERTScore and SBERT similarity and factual accuracy for choice questions (accuracy). The authors report that current LLMs struggle with real-world engineering constraints, producing errors in rule adherence, logical consistency, and component semantics, and that performance varies across models and task types. PCB-Bench also provides a curated dataset of over 170 real-world PCB designs and standardized protocols (task formats, metrics, prompt designs) to support reproducible evaluation of foundation models on PCB placement and routing.

GPT-5-nano scores highly on semantic similarity but is mid-tier in accuracy; Gemini-2.5-Pro attains top CBFB accuracy and perfect BWEI in the reported table. The study finds no single model dominates all dimensions and notes a clear trade-off between structured accuracy and semantic coherence. Overall leaders include Gemini-2.5, GPT-4o/5, Claude, and Qwen-VL-Max. The authors conclude that larger and domain-adapted models (or EDA integration) and a dedicated PCB evaluation system are needed since current LLMs remain insufficient for realistic open-ended PCB placement/routing.

## pqac-00000034

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench evaluates LLMs on board-level schematic design using a generative pipeline that mimics human engineering and breaks design into sub-tasks (e.g., module partitioning). The benchmark uses a three-tier verification hierarchy: static rule checks (topology/ pin-interface consistency), dynamic SPICE transient simulation driven via LTspice, and a global correctness metric requiring full system-level functionality. It also compiles generated netlists into SPICE scripts and applies electrical metric monitoring across the simulation lifecycle (probe voltages/currents must meet thresholds). The authors tested six LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.5, DeepSeek-v3, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-max, Grok-4) with standardized sampling (T=0.1, top-p=0.95). Results: static checks show an overall average pass rate of 71.84% (Claude Sonnet 4.5: 77.73%; DeepSeek-v3: 74.78%). Dynamic simulation pass rates are lower (Claude Sonnet 4.5: 67.92%; Qwen3-Max: 60.21%; Gemini 3.1 Pro: 60.00%). Global correctness (end-to-end functional designs) is very low: Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 8.15%, GPT-5.2 at 6.58%, others ~4–5%, indicating substantial gaps in physical reasoning and cross-module coherence.

## pqac-00000035

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes HWE-Bench evaluation tasks, metrics, and key results for LLMs applied to board-level schematic design. Tasks include static rule checking (topology and label-matching), dynamic behavior (SPICE-style) simulation, and an overall correct-design metric. Results are reported across multiple application domains (Aerospace, Educational, Automotive, Telecom, IoT, Consumer, Healthcare, Industrial). Aggregate metrics: static rule-checking average pass rate ≈ 71.84% (Table III), dynamic simulation average pass rate ≈ 58.65% (Table IV), and very low overall correct-design rates per model (Table V shows values like 5.12%, 8.15%, 6.58%, 5.16%, 4.4%, 5.03%). The paper notes models often score higher on “spice simulation pass rate” than on strict static checks because they create functionally equivalent connections that pass simulation but violate rigid physical labeling or exclusivity constraints. Identified failure modes include entity role confusion, label conflicts, and pin multiplexing mistakes (microcontroller pins assigned incompatible simultaneous roles). HWE-Bench applied signal-chain constraints via prompt engineering (an internal signal chain inference mechanism) to reduce label mapping errors and resolve role conflicts in case studies.

## pqac-00000036

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

The provided pages describe HWE-Bench's task set, pipeline, and evaluation approach. The benchmark contains 300 standardized circuit design tasks plus a library of 2,914 cleaned datasheets. Tasks are decomposed into submodules (component assignment, module-level local netlist generation, then system-level global netlist integration). The model is constrained to output structured JSON/ plain-text netlists; automated parsers validate formats and convert outputs. Verification is dual-stage: static rule checking (rule-based label matching/topological and protocol checks) and dynamic SPICE transient simulation using human-constructed ground-truth SPICE circuits wrapped in Python testbenches. The core metric is the aggregate pass rate across static and dynamic checkpoints. Success is defined as avoiding physical damage and keeping node voltages/currents within predefined safety thresholds under specified excitations. The excerpt details pipeline, data curation, and evaluation criteria but does not include numeric performance results or reported pass rates on these pages.

## pqac-00000037

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is an execution-driven benchmark for LLMs on board-level schematic design. The benchmark contains 300 board-level design tasks across 8 application domains and includes a knowledge base of 2,914 real IC datasheets. For each task, models must generate a schematic from scratch using only the provided functional requirements and component datasheets. Evaluation is two-stage: static electrical rule checking of the produced netlists, followed by dynamic verification via integration into an industry-standard LTspice simulator to check transient behaviors. The authors standardize tasks with machine-checkable goals and pre-parsed datasheet representations and provide a test pipeline designed to emulate human engineering workflows. They evaluated several state-of-the-art LLMs (DeepSeek-v3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Qwen3-Max, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok-4). Results show limited physical reasoning: while models show some engineering/documentation understanding, the top-performing model achieved an overall pass rate of only 8.15%, indicating substantial gaps in physical intuition and practical board-level design capability.

## pqac-00000042

From text 'luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 8-9'.

SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations This article has 0 citations.

The paper presents SchGen, a PCB schematic generation system that finetunes a 20B-parameter base LLM (GPT-oss-20b) using a semantic-grounded code representation called Code-L1. Code-L1 encodes schematics with features such as relative coordinates and pin-name connectivity; ablations (Code-L2 without relative coordinates and Code-L3 without relative coordinates and pin-name connectivity) isolate the contribution of these choices. Training uses example code, uniform prompts, and provided Python APIs; all models are evaluated in a single-run setting on identical test inputs. Outputs are generated schematic code/netlists that are measured by structural and functional metrics: valid circuit pass ratio, spatial violations (overlaps), netlist accuracy (Jaccard, precision, recall), symbol/pin connection errors, and expert functional verification. The authors avoid SPICE simulation due to mixed-domain system complexity. SchGen outperforms ablations and larger frontier models (GPT-5.2, GPT-o4mini, Grok-4) on the benchmark: e.g., SchGen achieves 82.00% valid circuits, netlist Jaccard 49.08, precision 54.87, recall 52.80, and 60.5% expert functional correctness. On an unseen GitHub dataset SchGen generalizes with 65.59% valid circuits and Jaccard 40.65. The results highlight the importance of the Code-L1 representation and chain-of-thought for generation quality.


---

## pqac-00000043

From text 'luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 1-3'.

SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations This article has 0 citations.

SchGen is a finetuned LLM (GPT-oss-20b) that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests by using a semantic-grounded code representation. The approach replaces verbose, geometry-heavy schematic files with a compact set of editing primitives (add symbol, connect pins, query pin locations) and APIs that encode spatial relations via local coordinates and relative offsets and connect nets by semantic pin names (e.g., VCC, TXD). Inputs are user textual functional requests; outputs are executable Python-like code representations of schematics that can be converted to netlists and downstream PCB layout/fabrication files. To address data scarcity, the authors build a large dataset via a human–agent pipeline that converts open-source PCB designs into their representation and synthesizes prompts. Evaluation covers design validity, spatial layout quality, netlist accuracy, and expert verification. On these benchmarks, their Code-L1 representation yields an 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness versus a 32% valid rate from raw KiCad file baselines.

Inputs include natural-language specifications and domain knowledge from IC datasheets (the KG), and outputs are executable SKiDL code and downstream KiCAD PCB layout files (netlist/layout-ready artifacts) using real-world IC packages. For evaluation, the authors curated a benchmark of 23 unique circuits spanning digital, analog, and power domains, each tied to real IC components. ## pqac-00000045

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-driven pipeline that translates natural-language PCB schematic descriptions into executable SKiDL code and downstream KiCAD schematic/layout files. Inputs are natural-language task descriptions (digital, analog, power, or mixed). SKiDL was chosen as the semantic-grounded code representation because Python-based hardware description languages provide high information density. The approach emphasizes prompt design to improve LLM comprehension, circuit connectivity awareness, and adaptability. For correctness, the system constructs an IC datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) encoding device rules and constraints, and uses a multi-stage verification stack including electrical design rules and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) topology checks; verification feedback is returned to the LLM for iterative refinement. The KG compresses typical datasheet token footprints (≈16k tokens) to about 300 tokens per component, a 30–70× context reduction while preserving functional/electrical constraints. The method also defines semantic pin roles (mapping pins to one of 36 roles) to decouple verification from vendor pin naming. The excerpt notes there is currently no scalable functional/electrical performance verifier, so datasheet-grounded structural verification is used as a practical abstraction. No numeric layout-generation accuracy benchmarks or end-to-end task metrics are provided in the excerpt.

## pqac-00000046

From text 'luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 3-4'.

SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations This article has 0 citations.

SchGen is an LLM system for PCB schematic generation that is finetuned from a 20B-parameter model (GPT-oss-20b). The approach centers on a semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) that encodes schematic editing primitives, relative placement, and pin-level connectivity instead of raw KiCad text or raster images. Inputs are natural-language user prompts; outputs are editable schematic code representations that can be materialized into KiCad schematics and netlists. The authors built a dataset (1390 schematic types) from open-source resources via a human–agent pipeline, using KiCad as the target format. Evaluation uses metrics for design validity, spatial layout quality, netlist accuracy, and expert verification. Results show the Code-L1 representation yields an 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness, versus only 32% valid circuits using raw KiCad baseline. SchGen matches or exceeds larger frontier LLMs (e.g., matching GPT-5.2 netlist accuracy) and achieves the highest performance despite its smaller 20B size, with demonstrated generalization to out-of-distribution GitHub projects.

## pqac-00000047

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 2 citations.

The paper proposes PCBSchemaGen, a training-free, constraint-guided pipeline for generating PCB schematics by combining LLM code proposals with formal verification. The approach uses an LLM as a proposal generator for SKiDL (Python-style) code via domain-specific prompts and reasoning techniques (CoT, ICL). Generated proposals are validated by a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) and a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) verifier that encodes pin-role semantics and topological/pin-package constraints derived from real IC datasheets. Inputs to the system include IC datasheets, constraint specifications/knowledge graph, and task prompts; outputs are SKiDL code representations of schematics (i.e., netlists/code that can be captured as schematics). The authors evaluate the system on an extensive benchmark of 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains. Results reported show that PCBSchemaGen "significantly improves design accuracy and computational efficiency." All code, KG, and benchmarks are open-sourced at the linked repository for reproducibility. This makes the method semantically grounded (datasheet/KG) and verifiable (SI-based checking) while avoiding large-scale model fine-tuning.

## pqac-00000048

From text 'luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 4-6'.

SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations This article has 0 citations.

SchGen represents PCB schematics as executable, semantic-grounded code rather than raw KiCad text or images. The approach abstracts schematic creation as a sequence of editing APIs (add_schematic_symbol, add_label, get_pin_location, connect_pins, write_out_all_wires) that capture placement, labeling, pin queries and connectivity. To reduce LLM difficulty with spatial reasoning, placements use local (relative) coordinates anchored to central symbols or pins rather than absolute coordinates. Executing the generated code produces valid KiCad schematic files. Dataset construction uses an LLM sketching + human correction pipeline from online open-source PCB designs and trains SchGen on user prompts paired with these code representations. Representations are benchmarked using Minimum Description Length (MDL), Lempel–Ziv complexity (LZ Norm), and validation loss (Val Loss). The proposed Code-L1 (with relative coordinates and pin-name-based connectivity) achieves the lowest MDL/LZ/Val Loss compared to variants that remove relative coordinates (Code-L2) or pin-name connectivity (Code-L3), with example reported values for Code-L1 around 2.19/2.31 (MDL), 1.36/1.42 (LZ Norm), and 1.29/0.25 (Val Loss), indicating a more structured, learnable representation and better generation-to-file validity.

pcbGPT: Automatic PCB Schematic Synthesis from Natural Language Requirements This article has 0 citations.

The paper frames PCB schematic generation as tool-grounded program synthesis in a Python-based circuit DSL that compiles into editable KiCad artefacts. The pipeline has three core parts: (1) a tool-augmented synthesis agent that accepts a natural-language request (examples: regulator stage, sensor interface, or complete embedded subsystem) and produces a circuit in the DSL, (2) component tools that ground the agent by searching local KiCad libraries and retrieving datasheet-derived component information (pin maps, footprints, integration rules), and (3) a validation stage that executes the DSL, checks syntactic correctness, and performs structural and semantic validation (rather than relying on full electrical simulation). These parts operate in an iterative generate–execute–repair loop: the agent issues tool calls to find symbols and component knowledge, assembles a candidate schematic, the validator returns concrete failure signals or critiques, and the agent revises the design. The excerpt documents inputs (natural-language requirements, KiCad libraries, datasheets), the output (KiCad-compatible schematic / Python DSL representation), grounding strategies, and the validation-driven iterative workflow. The excerpt does not include explicit benchmark descriptions or quantitative results.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000050

From text 'wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 7-12'.

Shikai Wang, Haodong Zhang, Shiji Zhou, Jun Sun, and Qi Shen. Chip floorplanning optimization using deep reinforcement learning. Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14, doi:10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14. This article has 18 citations.

The paper describes a DRL-based chip floorplanning approach that uses a graph neural network (GNN) for state representation. The GNN encodes node features (module dimensions, current position, pin count), edge features (connection strength, criticality), and global features (utilization, total length, timing information). The agent is trained with a composite reward R combining HPWL, area utilization, timing violations, and an exploration bonus: R_t = -α * HPWL_t - β * Area_t - γ * Timing_Violations_t + δ * Exploration_Bonus_t. To mitigate sparse rewards, an exploration bonus based on reconstruction error L_Rec = ||f(B̂ ) - f(B)||^2 is used. Training employs Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with curriculum learning and embedded expert heuristics (e.g., masking actions to place macros at margins). Experiments use ISPD 2005 benchmarks (circuit characteristics listed) and are run on a cluster with NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs using PyTorch 1.9.0 and PyTorch Geometric. Reported results include Table 4 per-circuit HPWL improvements (e.g., adaptec1: 84,905,888 vs DREAMPlace 128,927,038, 34.14% improvement) and Table 6 averages where "Our Method" shows lower Avg. HPWL (145,010,125) and moderate runtime (5.8 hours) versus baselines (DREAMPlace Avg. HPWL 211,470,939).


---

## pqac-00000051

From text 'wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 1-4'.

Shikai Wang, Haodong Zhang, Shiji Zhou, Jun Sun, and Qi Shen. Chip floorplanning optimization using deep reinforcement learning. Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14, doi:10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14. This article has 18 citations.

The paper proposes a chip floorplanning method that combines deep reinforcement learning (DRL) with graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach represents the chip netlist with GNNs to capture topological information and uses a three-head network (policy, cost, and reconstruction heads) to improve feature extraction and decision making. A multi-objective reward function balances wire-length minimization (HPWL), area utilization, and timing constraints; an exploration bonus derived from reconstruction error is added to mitigate sparse rewards. Training strategies aim to improve scalability and generalization. The method was evaluated on ISPD 2005 benchmarks and reported state-of-the-art performance: an average 31.4% reduction in half-perimeter wire length (HPWL) and a 34.2% reduction in breach time versus the best baseline. The authors also report experiments assessing scalability and robustness across different circuits and perturbations.


---

## pqac-00000052

From text 'wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 12-15'.

Shikai Wang, Haodong Zhang, Shiji Zhou, Jun Sun, and Qi Shen. Chip floorplanning optimization using deep reinforcement learning. Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14, doi:10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14. This article has 18 citations.

The paper proposes a DRL-based chip floorplanning method that uses graph neural networks (GNNs) for state representation and feature extraction. The agent architecture is a three-head network (policy, value, reconstruction) and uses a reward that combines wire-length minimization, area utilization, and timing constraint satisfaction; an exploration bonus based on reconstruction error is added to mitigate sparse rewards. Experiments are run on the ISPD 2005 benchmark suite with a case study on the adaptec3 circuit. Quantitative results (Table 6) show the proposed method achieving average HPWL = 145,010,125, density = 0.92, runtime ~5.8 hours, and timing violations = 127, outperforming baselines DREAMPlace, RePlAce, and DeepPlace (e.g., DREAMPlace HPWL = 211,470,939). The paper reports an average 31.4% HPWL reduction and 34.2% reduction in timing violations versus the best baseline. Scalability analysis (Table 7) gives runtimes and relative HPWL improvements across scales: Small (<100k modules) 1.2h / 28.5% improvement; Medium 4.7h / 23.7%; Large 8.9h / 19.2%; Very Large (>1M) 15.6h / 15.8%. Limitations include substantial training compute requirements and a fixed action space that may restrict placement flexibility.

## pqac-00000053

From text 'basso2026automatingthelayout pages 76-79'.

D Basso. Automating the layout of analog circuits: a machine learning-based approach. Unknown journal, 2026.

The paper proposes a chip floorplanning approach that combines a pre-trained relational GCN (R-GCN) encoder with a reinforcement learning (RL) policy (masked PPO). The R-GCN is trained in a supervised fashion to predict placement rewards from circuit graphs: it uses node features (area, transistor params, pin counts, functional one-hot), models multiple relation types (connectivity, alignment, symmetry), and the architecture consists of 4 R-GCN layers, node mean aggregation, then 5 FC layers. The R-GCN training dataset contains 21,600 floorplans and rewards generated by SA/GA/PSO optimizers; the supervised loss is MSE. The RL agent state combines the R-GCN graph embedding, 32-dimensional instance embeddings, and pixel-level CNN maps on a 32×32 grid (including reward masks fds, fw and positional masks fp ∈{0,1}3×32×32). Actions choose one of three precomputed shapes and a grid cell for the block's lower-left corner; rewards are negative increases in proxy wirelength/empty space and an end-of-episode negative weighted sum of area, half-perimeter wirelength, and aspect-ratio discrepancy. The trained pipeline is integrated into ANAGEN and is reported to outperform traditional metaheuristics and prior RL methods on area, proxy wirelength, speed and cost metrics, shortening design time while matching or surpassing manual layouts. Numeric benchmark values are not included in the excerpt.

## pqac-00000054

From text 'basso2026automatingthelayout pages 71-76'.

D Basso. Automating the layout of analog circuits: a machine learning-based approach. Unknown journal, 2026.

The paper describes a chip floorplanning method that combines a relational graph convolutional network (R-GCN) with deep reinforcement learning. The R-GCN is trained to predict placement rewards and to encode circuit, device, and geometric constraints; its output is used as an encoder for an RL agent. The RL agent additionally consumes spatial, pixel-level encodings from a CNN and learns an absolute-placement policy that selects component shapes and positions while enforcing no-overlap, symmetry, alignment, and other constraints. The approach improves generalization across different circuit topologies compared to earlier methods that required a separate model per component count. Evaluations were performed on six industrial circuits; the pipeline "surpassed established floorplanning techniques in terms of speed, area and half-perimeter wire length." Reported results include an overall layout time reduction of 67.3% and an 8.3% mean area reduction versus manual layout. In a case study (OTA), automated tooling reduced template generation and refinement times by ~99.9% and ~95.8% respectively, and produced 13.8% smaller layout area and 14.9% shorter wirelength than manual design.

## pqac-00000055

From text 'basso2026automatingthelayout pages 79-82'.

D Basso. Automating the layout of analog circuits: a machine learning-based approach. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt describes a chip floorplanning DRL approach that uses a trained R-GCN as a circuit encoder for the RL policy (removing final FC layers to reuse embeddings) and trains the agent with a masked PPO to enforce positional constraints. The layout is discretized to a 32×32 grid and the agent chooses a block lower-left cell plus one of 3 shapes, yielding an action space of size 3×32×32=3072; placements are ordered heuristically by decreasing block size. Positional masking uses three masks (one per shape) formed by combining occupancy and symmetry/alignment constraint matrices; additional grid-based masks include partial placement, wire-increase (fw), and a dead-space mask fds computed as the incremental empty-space increase. The 6 masks are concatenated into a 6×32×32 tensor and fed to a CNN (conv layers then an FC producing a 512-dimensional embedding), which is concatenated with R-GCN embeddings for the value and policy networks. The policy has an FC then 3 deconvolution layers to produce action probabilities. Reward shaping gives intermediate reward rt = −(∆ds + ∆HPWL) and a final reward combining area, HPWL and aspect-ratio error with weights α,β,γ=(1,5,5). The excerpt does not report benchmark datasets, numeric results, or comparisons.

From text 'wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 4-7'.

Shikai Wang, Haodong Zhang, Shiji Zhou, Jun Sun, and Qi Shen. Chip floorplanning optimization using deep reinforcement learning. Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14, doi:10.55524/ijircst.2024.12.5.14. This article has 18 citations.

The paper models chip floorplanning as a sequential decision process (an MDP (S,A,P,R)) where states encode current placements and metrics, actions place a module at (x,y), and transitions are deterministic. The DRL architecture uses a shared GNN encoder to process the chip netlist graph (nodes = modules, edges = connections), producing learned node representations that feed three heads: a policy network (π(a|s)) for action probabilities, a value network (V(s)), and a reconstruction head that recovers a visual placement representation. GNN input features include node features (module dimensions, current position, pin count), edge features (connection strength, criticality), and global features (utilization, total length, timing information). The reward is a composite function targeting wire-length minimization, area utilization, and timing constraint satisfaction. The excerpt describes the model design, state/action definitions, network architecture, and feature/reward design but does not include experimental benchmarks, datasets, or quantitative results in the provided pages.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000057

From text 'krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4'.

S Krishnamoorthy. Leveraging generative ai for platform hardware design automation-learnings and recommendations. Unknown journal, 2025.

The paper reports applied methods, findings, and recommendations for using generative AI (LLMs) in platform hardware/PCB schematic generation. Methods include controlling LLM sampling via the temperature parameter (trade-off: diversity vs. precision) and iterative LLM prompting—pointing out errors in regeneration reduces generation error. At the functional-to-component level, generated Verilog or high-level representations are mapped to vendor parts using BOM preferences; a modular mapping approach is recommended to enable reuse across platforms. For schematic placement, the workflow differs from layout: placement emphasizes visual understandability over pure wirelength minimization. The process relies on component dimension and pin-location data, begins by placing the most-connected component, aligns connected pins (with rotations/mirroring as needed), and uses offpage connectors when multiple pages are required. Generated designs are made editable for user corrections and can be exported/imported into PCB layout editors. Overall recommendations: tune temperature for desired diversity/precision, use iterative prompts to correct LLM outputs, map high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs, adopt modular reuse, provide accurate component/pin metadata, and produce editable outputs for downstream layout tools.


---

## pqac-00000058

From text 'krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2'.

S Krishnamoorthy. Leveraging generative ai for platform hardware design automation-learnings and recommendations. Unknown journal, 2025.

The paper reports that generative AI can produce structured artifacts (code, HDL) useful for platform hardware/PCB design, and that these techniques have already reduced design time and improved system optimization. Key methods discussed include structured-text generation with LLMs (zero-shot, few-shot, fine-tuning), industry uses of reinforcement learning and high-level command-driven generation, and the use of Verilog HDL as a practical representation for schematics. The authors found few-shot approaches effective when paired with domain descriptions and best-known methods (BKMs) to set context for LLMs. They note challenges specific to platform hardware: strict functional correctness, optimization needs, domain-specific constraints, and the necessity of verification and design reviews. Recommendations and directions include creating machine-consumable data and component libraries, using Verilog-level primitives to represent schematics, leveraging agentic/collaborative AI workflows for generation, critiquing and regeneration, and accounting for continual change requests to improve board designer productivity and reduce re-spins.


---

## pqac-00000059

From text 'king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24'.

pcbGPT: Automatic PCB Schematic Synthesis from Natural Language Requirements This article has 0 citations.

This pcbGPT study evaluates a generative-AI pipeline that translates natural-language requirements into KiCad-editable schematics using large code-capable models (e.g., gpt-5.3-codex). Empirically, the system attains high synthetic metrics (With gpt-5.3-codex, the system reaches an overall pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00) and can produce reviewable first-draft schematics that serve as useful starting points for development. Major failure modes are localized: supporting circuitry, values, interface details, and local topology. The authors position the tool as design acceleration rather than autonomous replacement, emphasizing an interactive web UI to keep humans in the loop for iterative inspection and refinement. Important system limitations include focus on schematic generation only (It does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation...), validation that ensures executability and plausibility but not electrical correctness, and exclusion of layout-dependent concerns (signal integrity, EMI, thermal behavior, etc.). Recommended next steps are model adaptation to improve electrical decision reliability, expanding the benchmark to larger and more diverse circuits, and studying interactive workflows and user-efficiency gains via user studies.


---

## pqac-00000060

From text 'luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 9-12'.

SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes SchGen, a 20B-parameter LLM designed for PCB schematic generation. Methodologically, the authors introduce a semantic-grounded code representation that converts schematics into a structured sequence of interpretable editing Python code, enabling the model to capture spatial reasoning and functional intent. They assembled training data via an agent–human collaborative pipeline and evaluated generalization on a held-out KiCad dataset of 988 unseen samples from 20 GitHub projects. Findings show SchGen substantially outperforms ablation variants and general-purpose models on valid circuit generation, spatial arrangement, and netlist accuracy, and its performance on unseen designs is reported as comparable to GPT-5.2 when GPT-5.2 is prompted with their code representations — despite SchGen being much smaller (20B params). Limitations and recommendations include: current focus on structured SparkFun schematics and lack of modeling for advanced PCB constraints; scaling to larger/complex PCBs is challenging due to dataset and model capability limits; and future work should target larger datasets, stronger models, more efficient pipelines, and maintaining human oversight to ensure robustness and safety.


---

## pqac-00000061

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses LLMs to generate SKiDL-format PCB schematics and a rule-based verification stage to enforce real-world IC package and pin constraints. Methodologically, it (1) frames schematic generation as LLM-driven code synthesis (proposal generator for SKiDL), (2) builds a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (KG) and uses Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) matching as a verifier to encode pin-role semantics and topological constraints, and (3) adopts a decoupled architecture so generation and verification are separate. The authors evaluate the system on 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains and report that PCBSchemaGen “significantly improves” design accuracy and computational efficiency. Recommended design choices implicit in the paper include leveraging domain-specific prompts, chain-of-thought and in-context learning for the LLM, using datasheet-derived KG constraints rather than purely simulation-based verification (e.g., SPICE), and open-sourcing code, KG, and benchmarks to support reproducibility. These points emphasize constraint-aware, interpretable verification and training-free LLM-assisted code generation for practical PCB schematic automation.


---

## pqac-00000062

From text 'cirstea2024digitalelectronicsystemonchip pages 16-19'.

Marcian Cirstea, Khaled Benkrid, Andrei Dinu, Romeo Ghiriti, and Dorin Petreus. Digital electronic system-on-chip design: methodologies, tools, evolution, and trends. Micromachines, 15:247, Feb 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/mi15020247, doi:10.3390/mi15020247. This article has 99 citations.

The excerpt discusses how AI/ML—and specifically generative AI—is increasingly integrated into electronic and SoC design flows, with applications relevant to platform hardware and PCB automation. It notes successful combinations of open-source EDA frameworks (e.g., OpenLane/OpenROAD) with ML to achieve earlier design optimization at RTL. Generative AI roles include architectural space exploration, optimized place-and-route, code optimization, autocompletion, and partial automatic code generation. ML techniques are used for combinational circuit generation, accurate power modelling, and pre-synthesis HDL quality verification. Practical enablers include FPGAs that can run full operating systems for edge platforms and the use of GPUs to accelerate ML workloads. From these points, useful methodological guidance for a study like Krishnamoorthy 2025 would be: integrate generative-AI modules into existing EDA flows (targeting RTL and place-and-route stages), leverage open-source toolchains (OpenLane/OpenROAD) for reproducibility, employ ML-based power and quality models to guide optimization, and use GPU-accelerated training/inference. Recommendations also include validating generated outputs via standard EDA verification (HDL checks, synthesis, power analysis), and considering system-level constraints (edge computing, FPGAs, Industry 4.0) when automating platform hardware/PCB designs.


---

## pqac-00000063

From text 'luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 1-3'.

SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations This article has 0 citations.

This paper introduces SchGen, an LLM system for generating editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests. It targets two key challenges: efficient schematic representation and data scarcity. The authors propose a semantic-grounded code representation that replaces verbose geometry-heavy formats with a compact set of editing primitives (add symbols, connect pins, query pin locations). Spatial reasoning is handled via local coordinate systems using relative offsets from anchor components, and wiring is specified by semantic pin names (e.g., VCC, TXD) rather than absolute coordinates. To create training data, they build an agentic pipeline that converts open-source PCB designs into editable KiCad schematics, synthesizes user prompts, and emits executable Python code. They finetune GPT-oss-20b on this dataset to produce SchGen. Empirically, the Code-L1 representation yields substantial gains: 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness versus 32% valid circuits from raw KiCad-file baselines, and the model outperforms alternative representations and larger general-purpose LLMs on connectivity and functional metrics. The work emphasizes that careful representation design is critical for applying generative models to complex hardware design tasks.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000064

From text 'shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 6-8'.

Yichen Shi, ZHUOFU TAO, YuHao Gao, Tianjia Zhou, Cheng Chang, Yaxin Wang, Bingyu Chen, Genhao Zhang, Alvin Liu, Zhiping Yu, Ting-Jung Lin, and Lei He. Amsnet-kg: a netlist dataset for llm-based ams circuit auto-design using knowledge graph rag. May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3736166, doi:10.1145/3736166. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

AMSnet-KG constructs a netlist dataset by extracting schematic images from textbooks and papers, then automatically detecting components, symbols, and nets to generate full SPICE netlists. The pipeline uses template matching to bootstrap labels, then trains deep-learning models in a semi-supervised loop to label remaining schematics. Orientation labels are recorded for each component orientation to infer connectivity and to order terminals correctly for SPICE (e.g., MOSFET drain/gate/source/body). Symbol pin functions are determined by marking pin numbers and using MLLM/GPT-4 for identification. Net detection assumes solid-line wires and that intersecting wires without a junction are not connected; grouping grows from component bounding-box pixels to neighboring wire pixels to form nets. For even-component intersections, a 2D convolution finds high-density wire pixels as intersection points which are added and the grouping repeated. The authors report 97% recall for component and symbol detection and 96% accuracy for net labeling. Exceptions and erroneous results are manually corrected to ensure data quality. The dataset supports LLM-based AMS circuit auto-design using knowledge-graph RAG.


---

## pqac-00000065

From text 'shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 1-2'.

Yichen Shi, ZHUOFU TAO, YuHao Gao, Tianjia Zhou, Cheng Chang, Yaxin Wang, Bingyu Chen, Genhao Zhang, Alvin Liu, Zhiping Yu, Ting-Jung Lin, and Lei He. Amsnet-kg: a netlist dataset for llm-based ams circuit auto-design using knowledge graph rag. May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3736166, doi:10.1145/3736166. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper presents AMSnet-KG, a curated dataset of AMS circuit schematics and SPICE netlists organized as a knowledge graph with detailed functional and performance annotations. The authors use this KG to enable a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workflow for LLM-based automated AMS circuit design. The proposed pipeline: (1) formulate a design strategy from specifications (architecture and component counts), (2) retrieve matched circuit components from the KG and assemble a complete topology, (3) perform transistor sizing via Bayesian optimization, and (4) simulate the resulting netlist and feed simulation results back to the LLM for topology refinement. The approach is motivated by a scarcity of public AMS netlist datasets and attendant LLM hallucination problems; AMSnet-KG aims to mitigate this by providing high-quality, annotated examples. The paper reports case studies on operational amplifier and comparator design that verify end-to-end automatic design from specifications to netlists with minimal human effort. The excerpt does not provide quantitative performance metrics in these pages; the dataset will be open-sourced after publication.


---

## pqac-00000066

From text 'shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 9-11'.

Yichen Shi, ZHUOFU TAO, YuHao Gao, Tianjia Zhou, Cheng Chang, Yaxin Wang, Bingyu Chen, Genhao Zhang, Alvin Liu, Zhiping Yu, Ting-Jung Lin, and Lei He. Amsnet-kg: a netlist dataset for llm-based ams circuit auto-design using knowledge graph rag. May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3736166, doi:10.1145/3736166. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper presents AMSnet-KG, a knowledge-graph-centered dataset built from schematics, netlists, and structured annotations (JSON) to enable LLM-driven analog/mixed-signal (AMS) circuit auto-design. AMSnet-KG models entities as strings or circuit objects (including schematic, netlist, image attributes, and local annotations) and uses string relations to link them. The dataset contains 894 circuits with schematics, component bounding boxes, and netlists, and a focused subset covering OPAMPs, comparators, bandgaps, LDOs, ADCs and related testbenches. The proposed AMSgen pipeline uses KG-RAG: LLMs (prompted using in-context learning and chain-of-thought) produce design strategies and analyses, which are formatted into relation triplets. Those triplets are used to query the AMSnet-KG (e.g., via Cypher/Neo4j) to retrieve matching circuits and testbenches; retrieved components are assembled and simulated. The pipeline covers topology selection, automated transistor sizing, and topology regeneration if sizing fails, enabling an end-to-end flow from performance specifications to fully sized transistor-level netlists.

## pqac-00000067

From text 'shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 2-4'.

Yichen Shi, ZHUOFU TAO, YuHao Gao, Tianjia Zhou, Cheng Chang, Yaxin Wang, Bingyu Chen, Genhao Zhang, Alvin Liu, Zhiping Yu, Ting-Jung Lin, and Lei He. Amsnet-kg: a netlist dataset for llm-based ams circuit auto-design using knowledge graph rag. May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3736166, doi:10.1145/3736166. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper presents AMSnet-KG, a knowledge-graph dataset of AMS netlists annotated with architectural descriptions, pin functions, and expert insights to support LLM-based analog/mixed-signal (AMS) auto-design. Motivation: LLMs can describe high-level design strategies but often fail at producing correct transistor-level netlists. The proposed AMSgen framework leverages LLMs to generate high-level architectures, then uses KG-based retrieval (KG-RAG) to find matching circuit components from AMSnet-KG and assemble complete topologies. Local annotations index pin functions and expert rules to guide automatic transistor sizing via Bayesian optimization, reducing the search space. If a generated design misses targets, AMSgen converts the design and measured performance into few-shot examples and iterates with the LLM to refine strategies. The reported application used 28nm technology and achieved various target performance specifications with minimal human effort. Key contributions are the AMSnet-KG dataset for retrieval and the AMSgen end-to-end flow combining LLM strategy generation, KG retrieval/assembly, automated sizing, and iterative LLM feedback.

## pqac-00000068

From text 'shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 17-19'.

Yichen Shi, ZHUOFU TAO, YuHao Gao, Tianjia Zhou, Cheng Chang, Yaxin Wang, Bingyu Chen, Genhao Zhang, Alvin Liu, Zhiping Yu, Ting-Jung Lin, and Lei He. Amsnet-kg: a netlist dataset for llm-based ams circuit auto-design using knowledge graph rag. May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3736166, doi:10.1145/3736166. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper introduces AMSnet-KG, a knowledge-graph-formatted netlist dataset for analog/mixed-signal (AMS) circuit design that includes schematics, netlists, and manual annotations. The proposed workflow couples large language models (LLMs) with knowledge-graph retrieval-augmented generation (KG-RAG). Given performance specifications, an LLM generates a design strategy (e.g., topology) which is converted into relation-query triplets to retrieve relevant components and testbenches from the KG. Retrieved components are assembled into a circuit and parameter sizing is performed via Bayesian optimization using a formulated FoM. If the assembled design does not meet specs or is too large, the strategy and topology are iteratively refined. Two case studies (OPAMP and a strong-arm latch comparator) demonstrate the approach; results show that local annotations in the KG materially improve optimization convergence and final performance. For the comparator case, Bayesian optimization over a reduced set of free parameters (22 parameters reduced to 10 by symmetry) produced substantially better FoM and metrics when local annotations were used (FoM 2.89 vs 1.61; offset 3.50×10−5 V vs 7.98×10−4 V; delay 1.19×10−11 s vs 5.81×10−10 s; power 8.84×10−5 W vs 3.42×10−4 W). The authors plan to expand AMSnet-KG with more parameters, topologies, and improved performance modeling/sizing methods.

Yichen Shi, ZHUOFU TAO, YuHao Gao, Tianjia Zhou, Cheng Chang, Yaxin Wang, Bingyu Chen, Genhao Zhang, Alvin Liu, Zhiping Yu, Ting-Jung Lin, and Lei He. Amsnet-kg: a netlist dataset for llm-based ams circuit auto-design using knowledge graph rag. May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3736166, doi:10.1145/3736166. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper demonstrates an LLM + knowledge-graph RAG workflow (AMSnet-KG) for AMS circuit auto-design. The LLM generates a design strategy (example: two-stage OPAMP with differential first stage, current-mirror load, common-source second stage, and Miller compensation). That strategy is converted into relation-query triplets which query AMSnet-KG to retrieve matching component netlists (e.g., differential pair, PMOS current mirror, common-source stage, bias, Miller RC). Local annotations on components are used to assemble the full netlist and to match testbench circuits. SPICE simulations (Cadence Spectre) are invoked programmatically to evaluate metrics. Sizing uses Bayesian optimization on a FoM combining normalized gain, CMRR, PSRR, log(GBW), and a phase-margin penalty around 60°; initial min/max values are taken from 100 simulations. Local annotations let symmetric transistors share (L,W), reducing free parameters from 19 to 15, which accelerates convergence and yields better final FoM and metrics (comparison table shows improved DM gain, GBW, CMRR, PSRR when using annotations). However, the optimized sizing for the initial topology still did not reach the target gain (66.21 dB vs 80 dB), motivating topology regeneration.

The AMSnet-KG work presents Amsnet-kg, a netlist dataset intended to enable LLM-based analog/mixed-signal (AMS) circuit auto-design using a knowledge-graph (KG) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach. The pipeline extracts relationships from detailed circuit descriptions and encodes them as triplets (e.g., component types, roles, locations, functions) to populate a KG that can be queried by LLMs. The paper shows few-shot prompt examples where performance specifications (28nm process constraints) are analyzed and mapped to topology design recommendations: an op-amp example (DC Gain > 80dB, GBW > 10MHz, Phase Margin > 60°, CMRR/PSRR > 80dB) and a comparator example (propagation delay <1ns, offset <100µV, 1GHz sampling, <100µW power). LLM responses produce structured design strategies (input stages, gain/latch stages, compensation), concrete circuit-structure descriptions, and triplet outputs for KG ingestion. AMSnet-KG constructs a netlist knowledge graph for LLM-based AMS circuit auto-design using a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) style pipeline. The authors extract relation triplets (standardized by few-shot examples) via an LLM and perform Cypher queries in Neo4j to retrieve circuit components. Retrieved components are assembled into full netlists by matching preserved net functions and net names (e.g., Vout to Vin). Sizing is formulated as a constrained black-box optimization solved with Bayesian optimization (BO), where local annotations in AMSnet-KG provide equality/ratio constraints to reduce the parameter search space (Algorithm 1 describes constraint-augmented sizing). If sizing cannot meet specs, topology regeneration uses the LLM again, now seeded with previous design performance as quantitative context to propose new topologies. Implementation details: YOLO-V8 for object detection during dataset construction, Neo4j for the KG, GPT-4 for queries, experiments on SMIC 28nm (device limits given), Optuna for BO, and a simulation budget limited to 2000. A case study: for an OPAMP spec the LLM proposed a two-stage opamp (differential first stage with current mirror load, CS second stage, Miller compensation) and the strategy was converted to query triplets to retrieve circuits. The provided excerpt documents the approach and experimental setup but does not include final numeric performance results within these pages.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000072

From text 'li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 1-2'.

Hangyuan Li, Zhaoyang Yang, Haotian Pang, Ning Xu, and Yu Chen. A double-layer placement algorithm for integrated circuit-based modules on printed circuit board. ArXiv, Feb 2502. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012. This article has 0 citations.

The paper proposes PA-ICM, an automatic double-layer placement algorithm for IC-based functional modules on PCB. The placement is modeled as a mixed-variable optimization problem with a top-layer centralized placement subproblem and a bottom-layer pin-oriented placement subproblem. The approach is two-stage: a global-placement stage that permits partial overlap and concurrently optimizes module rotation angles and positions via a hybrid algorithm (DEA-PPM + CSA), and a legalization stage that eliminates overlaps and enforces diverse space rules. The method uses centralized strategies for top-layer placement and a pin-oriented global placement based on bin clustering for bottom-layer optimization. A cluster-based optimization strategy (cluster-wise initialization, batch optimization, and corresponding legalization) further improves performance. Numerical comparisons report that the proposed mixed-variable optimization scheme achieves optimized total wirelength. Experimental results on several industrial PCB cases are reported (details not provided in the excerpt).


---

## pqac-00000073

From text 'li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 6-7'.

Shanyi Li, Zhen Zhuang, Mingyu Liu, Weihua Sheng, Bei Yu, and Tsung-Yi Ho. Hieplace: efficient hierarchical pcb placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3580511, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3580511. This article has 2 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The Hieplace approach is a hierarchical PCB placement pipeline that first preprocesses input to handle PCB irregularities and reduce solution space. It converts the 2-D chip outline into a 1-D segment for placing first-priority components, then applies a reordering (Algorithm 1) that prioritizes minimizing net crossings (considered more important than wirelength) and secondarily reduces HPWL. Components are selected for a constrained first-row layout by formulating selection as a 0/1 knapsack problem; component value is computed as val(i) = hic × 1 −wlic/lenchip, favoring larger components with shorter HPWL. Algorithm 2 performs DP-based selection; unselected components are interpolated with the x-coordinate of their succeeding ordered component. After priority placement, a relaxation/position-solving stage places grouped modules under boundary constraints, solving an objective (minimizing inter-module connection cost) with SLSQP. Reported qualitative outcomes include improved routability, reduced wirelength and crossings, better alignment for irregular boards, and runtime benefits from dimensional reduction and staged processing. Quantitative metrics are not provided in the excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000074

From text 'li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 6-7'.

Hangyuan Li, Zhaoyang Yang, Haotian Pang, Ning Xu, and Yu Chen. A double-layer placement algorithm for integrated circuit-based modules on printed circuit board. ArXiv, Feb 2502. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012. This article has 0 citations.

The paper presents a double-layer PCB placement algorithm for IC-based modules that combines a mixed-variable model with a flexible legalization procedure. During legalization the algorithm removes overlaps by iteratively moving components (up/down/left/right) using an intermediate “middle” component and traversing neighboring components to eliminate overlaps. Top-layer legalization uses a partition-based approach; the basic idea is consistent with bottom-layer legalization but the top-layer is legalized based on region partitions and directional elimination within each partition. The authors propose two different legalization strategies to handle the differing placement principles of the two layers. Experimental validation used four industrial PCB cases (single- and two-layer instances) and fixed-outline floorplanning benchmarks; implementations were in C++ on an AMD Ryzen 7 5800H laptop. Numerically, the proposed method generally runs faster than Parquet 4.5 and IARFP and performs well on medium-to-large circuits (ami49, n100, n200, n300), though it underperforms the compared methods on the small-scale circuit ami33.


---

## pqac-00000075

From text 'li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 4-5'.

Hangyuan Li, Zhaoyang Yang, Haotian Pang, Ning Xu, and Yu Chen. A double-layer placement algorithm for integrated circuit-based modules on printed circuit board. ArXiv, Feb 2502. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes the approach of Li et al.'s double-layer PCB placement algorithm rather than presenting numerical results. The method treats top- and bottom-layer components differently. For the top layer it enforces a centralized layout around an IC: an artificial external contour is built, netlist connections to IC pins are extracted, each component is attached to its nearest associated IC-pin (with special handling for 'close-to-pin' nets which are initialized directly), and coordinates are legalized so components lie outside the IC. For the bottom layer the algorithm clusters IC pins using DBSCAN to form groups; components are assigned to pins (randomly for initial attachment), noise pin points are handled by attaching nearby components or placing some components directly on those pins, and components bound to close-to-pin nets are excluded from subsequent global placement. Component clustering is performed in initial placement, then global placement and legalization run per-cluster independently and are finally integrated; legalization aims to eliminate overlaps and reduce wirelength and constraint violations. The excerpt does not include quantitative experimental results or performance metrics.


---

## pqac-00000076

From text 'li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 7-8'.

Hangyuan Li, Zhaoyang Yang, Haotian Pang, Ning Xu, and Yu Chen. A double-layer placement algorithm for integrated circuit-based modules on printed circuit board. ArXiv, Feb 2502. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012. This article has 0 citations.

The paper formulates the two-layer (top and bottom) PCB module placement as a mixed-variable optimization problem and develops tailored strategies for global optimization and legalization for both layers. The method yields legal placements for top- and bottom-layer components: centrally located parts keep small offsets and relative positions to neighbors, close-to-pin components remain adjacent to their assigned pins, and bottom-layer parts are placed within feasible regions restricted by IC outlines. The algorithm handles unbalanced component distributions and produces legal placements with various component-space settings; it also produces few crossed jumper wires for top-layer connections. Experiments (ten independent runs) report HPWL and runtime statistics (examples: Case1 bottom: 9267.355 HPWL, Case2 top: 4667.297 HPWL) and show the algorithm achieves automatic placement in a couple of minutes with small standard deviations, indicating stability. Numerical comparisons claim the mixed-variable optimization outperforms metaheuristic methods based on representative structure codes. A noted limitation is that the density-based clustering step can concentrate IC-pin points into one cluster, making global placement more challenging; future work targets large-scale cases and integration into multi-module PCB design.


---

## pqac-00000077

From text 'li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 3-4'.

Hangyuan Li, Zhaoyang Yang, Haotian Pang, Ning Xu, and Yu Chen. A double-layer placement algorithm for integrated circuit-based modules on printed circuit board. ArXiv, Feb 2502. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2502.14012. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes Li et al.'s two-layer PCB placement approach: they transform constrained placement into unconstrained weighted objectives combining HPWL (wirelength), overlap penalty (square root of overlapping area) and boundary-violation penalty for the top layer (min f = α1 W + β1 sqrt(D) + γ1 B) and a simpler objective for the bottom layer (min f = α2 W + β2 sqrt(D)). Wirelength uses HPWL formula; overlaps are computed from per-axis pairwise overlap terms Oi,j(x,r) and Oi,j(y,r) with component effective widths/heights ˆwi,ˆhi depending on orientation. Optimization uses two main methods: (1) a Conjugate Sub-Gradient Algorithm (CSA) to handle analytic non-smooth optimization of positions, and (2) a Distributional Evolutionary Algorithm based on a Population of Probability Model (DEA-PPM) to search orientation combinations. An initial placement phase (Algorithm 3 TopInitialization) computes top-layer area, approximates the contour, and seeds component coordinates before global placement refinement. The excerpt does not include numerical experiments, benchmarks, or performance results, so no quantitative results or comparisons are available in these pages.


---

## pqac-00000078

From text 'li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 1-2'.

Shanyi Li, Zhen Zhuang, Mingyu Liu, Weihua Sheng, Bei Yu, and Tsung-Yi Ho. Hieplace: efficient hierarchical pcb placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3580511, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3580511. This article has 2 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes HiePlace, an MP-based hierarchical PCB placement framework by Li et al. tailored to PCB-specific constraints (irregular outlines, priorities, alignment). To reduce the heavy computation of direct MP solutions, HiePlace applies two acceleration techniques: (1) a dynamic programming stage that prioritizes placement of core (large) components to reduce the solution space and improve placement quality; and (2) a relaxation algorithm that reduces the number of boolean variables, further narrowing the search space. The approach explicitly considers PCB-specific features such as irregular components, component priorities, and alignment. Reported experimental results for HiePlace show substantial improvements: a 7.7× speedup and a 66% reduction in cost (objective) compared to baseline methods. The work emphasizes that conventional VLSI placers do not match PCB needs, motivating this MP-based hierarchical approach with specialized accelerations.
