function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive comparison table of commercial AI-native PCB design platforms and tools that bridge topology/block diagram to schematic+floorplan. Include columns: Tool/Platform, Input (what it accepts), Output (schematic/netlist/footprints/BOM/outline/floorplan), ECAD Export (KiCad etc), Scriptability/API, Pricing/Licensing, Maturity. Include: CELUS, Flux.ai, JITX, Circuit Mind, Cofactr/Diadem, Quilter, DeepPCB/InstaDeep. Also include the newer LLM-based research tools: pcbGPT, PCBSchemaGen, SchGen."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Tool/Platform | Input (what it accepts) | Output (schematic/netlist/footprints/BOM/outline/floorplan) | ECAD Export | Scriptability/API | Pricing/Licensing | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CELUS Design Platform / CUBOs / Design Assistant | High-level functional requirements, block-diagram-style intent, reference-design/library-driven selections; strongest when mapping requirements to known solution blocks rather than arbitrary bare topologies. Academic references describe automated reference-design processing and schematic generation around CELUS libraries. (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) | Claimed/marketed: generated schematic drafts, component selection/BOM, design documentation; practical floorplanning/outline generation appears limited and typically downstream in partner ECAD. Best viewed as requirements/block intent -> schematic/BOM accelerator, not a full autonomous starter-board generator. (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) | Altium-centric in practice; no strong public evidence of native KiCad export in the cited research context. | Primarily SaaS GUI/workspace; public headless API/CLI not prominent in available evidence. | Commercial enterprise SaaS; pricing not generally public. | Commercial and deployed, but evidence suggests strongest maturity in requirements-to-reference-design synthesis rather than headless topology->KiCad starter-board export. (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) |
| Flux.ai | Usually starts from human-authored schematic/PCB or copilot-assisted editing in a browser ECAD; accepts natural-language assistance, component/library queries, and collaborative schematic edits more than pure block diagrams. | Browser-native schematic + PCB editing, shared libraries/BOM workflows; can help assign footprints and prepare PCB projects, but public evidence for fully automatic block-diagram -> complete starter board is limited. | Flux native; interoperability with standard ECAD formats exists, but KiCad-native round-trip/headless export is not its core story. | Web product; some integrations, but not a broadly documented public CLI-first workflow. | Commercial SaaS with team/business tiers; pricing changes over time. | Mature collaborative ECAD with AI copilot features, but not the most credible headless bridge for topology+BOM -> KiCad starter board. |
| JITX | Code/programmatic hardware specification using Stanza-based generators; best input is parameterized circuit/programmatic intent, not an informal block diagram. Related academic systems describe high-level HDL/generator flows that elaborate to concrete circuits and footprints. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000019) | Concrete circuit implementations, netlists, selected parts/footprints, manufacturable design artifacts; can automate repetitive schematic capture and some placement/floorplan logic through generators. | Exports to mainstream PCB CAD/manufacturing flows; KiCad is not the primary native target. | Strong scriptability/programmatic interface; this is the main value proposition. | Commercial proprietary. | One of the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, especially for reusable generator-based designs; credible for code-first starter-board generation, weaker for raw natural-language topology ingestion. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) |
| Circuit Mind | Natural-language / requirement-driven electronics design assistant focused on suggesting architectures and schematics; best for early concept exploration. | Schematic concepts, component suggestions, documentation; public evidence for complete footprinted ECAD projects with board outline/floorplan is limited. | Limited public evidence of native KiCad starter-project export. | Mostly GUI/web workflow; public CLI/API not clearly established. | Commercial; pricing typically by demo/contact. | Emerging commercial AI assistant; useful upstream, but not clearly proven as a full topology+BOM -> footprinted starter-board bridge. |
| Cofactr / Diadem | Supply-chain/BOM/procurement-centric inputs: AML/AVL, sourcing constraints, approved parts, lifecycle, inventory; not primarily a topology-to-schematic authoring tool. | Strong on vetted manufacturer parts, BOM enrichment, sourcing, compliance, lifecycle/risk data; does not itself appear to generate a complete captured schematic + floorplan. | Integrates with enterprise PLM/EDA data rather than acting as a KiCad project generator. | API/integration oriented on supply-chain data more than ECAD synthesis. | Commercial enterprise software. | Mature for BOM intelligence and part selection; useful as a component-grounding layer, not a complete starter-board synthesizer. |
| Quilter | Input is already a PCB project with netlist, footprints, board outline, constraints, and usually pre-placement context; cited PCB-placement literature uses Quilter as a placement/routing baseline, not as a block-diagram-to-schematic tool. HiePlace compares against Quilter on academic benchmarks. (pqac-00000024) | Autonomous placement/routing assistance on an existing board design; does **not** fill the topology+BOM -> starter-board gap itself. (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000078) | Works on existing PCB CAD data; not presented in cited evidence as a schematic capture/export platform. | Commercial tool workflow; public general-purpose CLI/API not the main story in available evidence. | Commercial. | Strong downstream physical-design tool; explicitly depends on upstream starter-board artifacts already existing. (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025) |
| DeepPCB / InstaDeep | Similar to Quilter: requires board-level physical-design inputs (placed/unplaced components with footprints, nets, constraints, board geometry). Research and benchmark context treats it as a placement/routing-stage method. | Placement/routing optimization, not schematic/BOM capture. | Consumes existing PCB design representations; not a schematic-capture export system. | Research/commercial-access dependent; not a public general-purpose CLI-first topology compiler. | Commercial/research-collaboration oriented. | Important downstream autonomous-layout reference, but not a solution to the intermediate topology->starter-board step. |
| pcbGPT | Natural-language hardware requirements; grounded by local KiCad libraries, exact component names, pin maps, footprint candidates, and datasheet-derived rules. Uses a Python circuit DSL instead of raw SKiDL. (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000049) | Editable KiCad project drafts, schematic artifacts, netlists, JSON/graph exports; focuses on schematic generation and draft project creation, not reliable autonomous placement/routing/manufacturing prep. Reported pass@1 0.90, pass@5 1.00 on its benchmark, but authors state it does not automate placement/routing. (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059) | KiCad-compatible projects (.kicad_sch/.kicad_pcb/.kicad_pro) and netlists. (pqac-00000015) | Research prototype with tool-augmented execution/validation loop; not yet a production public API/CLI platform. | Research code/prototype. | Among the most credible research systems for NL -> KiCad schematic drafts, but still weak on floorplan/outline and not production-ready. (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000059) |
| PCBSchemaGen | Natural-language specifications plus datasheet-derived knowledge graph constraints; generates executable SKiDL code with verifier feedback. Covers digital/analog/power tasks. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047) | Verified SKiDL code, KiCad schematics, and layout-ready KiCad artifacts; emphasizes structural correctness via KG + subgraph-isomorphism verification. Public evidence is stronger for schematic/netlist correctness than for automatic board outline/floorplan. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000045) | SKiDL -> KiCad workflow; explicitly aims at KiCad-ready files. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009) | Research pipeline; open-sourced according to the paper, but not yet a polished commercial API product. (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000047) | Research/open prototype. | Very promising as a topology/requirements -> verified KiCad schematic bridge; less mature on physical floorplanning and production workflow hardening. |
| SchGen | Natural-language user requests; finetuned 20B LLM generates semantic-grounded schematic-editing code rather than raw KiCad text. (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000063) | Editable schematic code that executes to valid KiCad schematic files/netlists; benchmarked at 82% valid circuits and 60.5% expert functional correctness. Focus is schematic generation, not board-outline/floorplan automation. (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046) | KiCad target format via generated executable editing code. (pqac-00000043, pqac-00000048) | Research model/code pipeline; not a commercial headless PCB compiler product. | Research/open prototype. | Strong research result on NL -> editable KiCad schematics; not yet a complete starter-board generator for autonomous routers. |
| Bottom line for this gap | Bare topology/block diagram alone is still rarely enough for robust automation. The most credible current bridges are: **commercial** code/reference-design systems like JITX/CELUS for constrained domains, and **research** systems like pcbGPT/PCBSchemaGen/SchGen for KiCad schematic generation. Quilter/DeepPCB remain downstream consumers, not gap-fillers. (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000057, pqac-00000059) | A usable “starter board” for autonomous routers still usually needs human decisions on exact parts, footprints, power-tree safety, board outline, placement regions, keepouts, analog partitioning, and connector/mechanical details. HWE-Bench’s best full-correct rate of only 8.15% underscores the current gap. (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037) | KiCad-centered research is improving rapidly; production-grade end-to-end automation is not yet solved. | Headless/scriptable today: strongest in code-first/open flows, weaker in commercial GUI-first platforms. | Mixed commercial + research landscape. | As of 2025, the topology+BOM -> KiCad-starter-board gap is **partially fillable**, not fully solved. (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000059) |


*Table: This table compares commercial platforms and recent research systems on the specific intermediate PCB-design gap: turning high-level requirements or topology into a schematic/netlist/BOM/footprinted starter board. It highlights which tools are real candidates for feeding downstream autonomous placement/routing engines and where the major limitations still are.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038, pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000047, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000049, pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055, pqac-00000056, pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058, pqac-00000059, pqac-00000060, pqac-00000061, pqac-00000062, pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064, pqac-00000065, pqac-00000066, pqac-00000067, pqac-00000068, pqac-00000069, pqac-00000070, pqac-00000071, pqac-00000072, pqac-00000073, pqac-00000074, pqac-00000075, pqac-00000076, pqac-00000077, pqac-00000078]

Include: atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, BHDL, Polymorphic Blocks/EDG, KiCad Python IPC API + kicad-cli, PCBmodE, Horizon EDA."}

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Tool/Framework | Language | Input Description | Output (netlist/schematic/footprint/BOM/board outline/floorplan) | KiCad Export? | Auto-Placement/Floorplan? | Scriptable/CI-Ready? | License | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| atopile | atopile DSL + Python ecosystem | Textual hardware-description source capturing modules, constraints, interfaces, and component intent at higher abstraction than hand-drawn schematics; generally code-first rather than free-form block-diagram-first | Generates netlists, schematic/PCB project artifacts, footprints via libraries, BOMs; board outline/floorplan support exists but is limited compared with schematic/netlist generation and often still needs explicit designer intent | Yes, KiCad-centered in practice | Partial: can encode placement intent/constraints, but not a mature autonomous floorplanner; outline/floorplan usually still semi-manual | Yes; strongly oriented to Git/version control and automation workflows | Open source | Fast-moving open-source project; promising for headless starter-board generation, but less mature than KiCad itself |
| tscircuit | TypeScript/JavaScript | Programmatic circuit and PCB descriptions in TS/JS; strongest for software-engineer-friendly code-first designs and reusable generators/components | Can emit schematic/PCB data structures, footprints, BOM-style outputs, and web-native rendered PCB artifacts; KiCad interoperability is improving but not yet the canonical native target | Partial / indirect | Limited native auto-floorplanning; mainly parametric geometry and explicit placement authored in code | Yes; highly scriptable and CI-friendly | Open source | Emerging and developer-friendly, but still earlier-stage for production mixed-signal starter boards |
| SKiDL | Python | Python code describing parts, nets, buses, hierarchy, and parameters; ideal as an intermediate representation from higher-level generators or LLMs | Netlists directly; via downstream tools can generate/update KiCad schematics; supports real parts and footprints if assigned; BOM possible through Python processing; board outline/floorplan not native | Yes, through KiCad-oriented netlisting/workflows; heavily used in KiCad-centric research pipelines (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047) | No native floorplanning; leaves board outline and placement mostly manual/downstream | Yes; excellent for headless generation in CI | Open source | Mature and widely used as the most practical code-first schematic/netlist layer for KiCad automation |
| BHDL | Racket (embedded DSL) + Julia placer backend | Declarative PCB code with pin/part/wire/layout clauses; supports hierarchical composition, loops, parameterization, and layout combinators | Produces netlists, schematic representations, mapped footprints, BOM, KiCad PCB export, and auto-placed layouts using analytical + SA placers; can route via FreeRouting downstream (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005) | Yes, explicit KiCad PCB export (pqac-00000002) | Yes, unusually strong among research HDLs: includes relative layout combinators plus auto-placement (RePlace-style global placer + detailed SA) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) | Yes, code-first and notebook/REPL friendly; more research-prototype than turnkey CI product | Open source (pqac-00000000) | Research prototype but one of the strongest demonstrations of code->KiCad PCB + placement |
| Polymorphic Blocks / EDG | Python-embedded HDL | High-level block diagrams / abstract typed blocks refined into concrete implementations with propagated electrical constraints and generator libraries | Generates refined hierarchical circuit models, netlists, footprint annotations, and exports netlists for import into KiCad/layout tools; board layout and floorplan are mostly downstream/manual (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Yes, via netlist export/import workflow rather than native .kicad_pcb emission (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Limited: can reach layout-ready circuits conceptually, but not a full automatic board floorplanner in cited evidence (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000018) | Partly: underlying HDL/compiler is scriptable; user-facing refinement has GUI elements (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000019) | Research / open academic tooling | Important conceptual predecessor for high-level circuit synthesis; not a polished production KiCad compiler |
| KiCad Python / IPC API + kicad-cli | Python + CLI | Existing netlists/projects/scripts, custom generators, library queries, PCB edits, DRC/plot/export jobs; best used as a backend rather than a high-level language itself | Native KiCad projects, schematics/PCB edits, footprint assignment, plotting, ERC/DRC, BOM/plugin-driven outputs; board outlines and placement can be scripted but require user-authored logic | Native yes | Only what you script yourself; no built-in intelligent floorplanner | Yes; best native headless path for CI around KiCad | Open source | Production-grade backend automation layer, but not itself a high-level topology compiler |
| PCBmodE | Python + SVG | Geometry/code-driven PCB artwork and board-shape descriptions, usually emphasizing board form and traces over traditional schematic-first capture | Board outlines, copper geometry, stylized PCB layouts; not a full schematic/netlist/BOM/footprint capture system | No native full KiCad-project generation; more fabrication-geometry oriented | Can automate geometry/artistic layout, but not electrical floorplanning from topology | Yes | Open source | Niche/specialized; useful for geometry-first boards, not for general starter-board generation |
| Horizon EDA | Native application with code-friendly data model | Traditional schematic/PCB entry with scriptable/project-file friendliness; higher-level design capture is weaker than dedicated HDLs | Schematic, PCB, footprints, libraries, manufacturing outputs; can handle real parts and footprints natively; board outline manual; no known autonomous floorplanning focus | Not primarily KiCad-native; interoperability exists through standard EDA exchange rather than first-class KiCad export | Minimal built-in auto-floorplanning | Partial: data model is automation-friendly, but headless CI support is not as strong as SKiDL/KiCad CLI | Open source | Serious open-source EDA suite, but not a topology->starter-board compiler |
| Practical takeaway | Mixed | For fully headless, version-controlled generation of a footprinted KiCad starter board, the strongest stack today is usually **SKiDL or atopile as the source language**, plus **KiCad CLI/Python** as the execution/export backend; **BHDL** is the strongest research example of adding automated placement to that stack (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009) | Netlist/schematic/BOM generation is much more automated than board outline/floorplan generation; the latter usually still needs explicit constraints or human intent (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Yes, with the right stack | Partial overall | Yes, especially SKiDL/atopile + KiCad CLI | Mixed | As of 2025, code-first generation of a KiCad-ingestible starter board is feasible, but reliable automatic floorplanning remains the weak link |


*Table: This table compares the main design-as-code and scriptable PCB frameworks relevant to generating footprinted, netlisted KiCad starter projects from higher-level descriptions. It highlights where automation is already strong—schematic/netlist/BOM generation—and where it remains weak, especially board outline and floorplanning.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00, artifact-01]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038, pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000047, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000049, pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055, pqac-00000056, pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058, pqac-00000059, pqac-00000060, pqac-00000061, pqac-00000062, pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064, pqac-00000065, pqac-00000066, pqac-00000067, pqac-00000068, pqac-00000069, pqac-00000070, pqac-00000071, pqac-00000072, pqac-00000073, pqac-00000074, pqac-00000075, pqac-00000076, pqac-00000077, pqac-00000078]

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table of recent (2020-2025) peer-reviewed/arXiv papers on automatically going from netlist/topology to initial placement/floorplan/board outline, including LLM-driven schematic-to-layout work. Columns: Paper (authors, year), Approach, Domain (PCB/IC/Analog), Input, Output, Key Results/Metrics, Benchmark. Include approximately 14 papers covering: HiePlace (Li 2025), Vassallo & Bajada (DATE 2024), Vashisht et al. (2020), Wang et al. (2024 DRL+GNN floorplanning), Li et al. (2025 double-layer), Lim (2025 component-centric), Basso (2026 analog RL+R-GCN), PCBSchemaGen (Zou 2025), pcbGPT (King 2026), SchGen (Luo 2025), HWE-Bench (Qiu 2025), PCB-Bench (Li 2026), AMSnet-KG (Shi 2025), Krishnamoorthy (2025)."}

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-02)

| Paper (authors, year) | Approach | Domain | Input | Output | Key Results / Metrics | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Li et al., **HiePlace** (2025) | Hierarchical mathematical-programming PCB placement with dynamic programming for first-priority parts and relaxation to reduce Boolean variables | PCB placement | Irregular board outline, components/modules, pins, netlist, preplaced parts, obstacles, spacing constraints | Detailed component placement | Avg. **66% cost reduction** and **7.7× speedup** vs baselines; on four academic benchmarks **76.7%–91.3%** cost reduction vs Quilter, with **99.4%–99.9%** runtime reduction; also ~**16% wirelength** and **46% misaligned-region** reduction in feasible cases (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000078) | 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industry, 4 academic); Quilter comparison on 4 academic boards (pqac-00000024) |
| Vassallo & Bajada (DATE 2024) | End-to-end RL for PCB placement using TD3/SAC with adaptive rewards | PCB placement | Unplaced circuit/netlist represented as layered images plus per-component masks | Placed PCB components | On unseen circuits, **17% (TD3)** and **21% (SAC)** average reduction in post-routing wirelength vs simulated annealing; faster convergence, sometimes >10× (pqac-00000020) | Unseen PCB circuits; GitHub-released datasets/results (pqac-00000020) |
| Vashisht et al. (2020) | Cyclic RL + simulated annealing using sequence-pair representation | IC / floorplanning | Block/netlist placement instances | Better initial floorplans / placements | RL-initialized SA beats random-init SA: e.g. lattice **100 blocks 3386 vs 3532 µm**, **225 blocks 14348 vs 14844**; ami49 area **49 vs 53 mm²** with fixed blocks, **43 vs 46 mm²** without (pqac-00000022) | Lattice benchmarks and ami49 (pqac-00000022) |
| Wang et al. (2024) | DRL + GNN chip floorplanning with PPO, policy/value/reconstruction heads, multi-objective reward | IC floorplanning | Chip netlist graph with module, edge, and global features | Floorplans / macro placement | Avg. **31.4% HPWL reduction** and **34.2% timing-violation reduction** vs best baseline; avg HPWL **145,010,125** vs DREAMPlace **211,470,939** (pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052) | ISPD 2005 benchmarks (pqac-00000050, pqac-00000052) |
| Li et al. (2025), **PA-ICM double-layer placement** | Mixed-variable optimization for top-layer centralized + bottom-layer pin-oriented PCB module placement; DEA-PPM + CSA + legalization | PCB module placement | IC-based modules, top/bottom-layer components, pin assignments, fixed outline | Legal top/bottom-layer placements | Automatic placement in **minutes** with small std. dev.; generally faster than Parquet 4.5 and IARFP on medium/large cases; stable HPWL examples reported (e.g. Case1 bottom **9267.355**, Case2 top **4667.297**) (pqac-00000072, pqac-00000074, pqac-00000076, pqac-00000077) | 4 industrial PCB cases + fixed-outline floorplanning benchmarks (pqac-00000074, pqac-00000076) |
| Lim (2025), **Component Centric Placement Using DRL** | RL for component-centric PCB placement; compares A2C, DQN, DQNnet | PCB placement | PCB placement states with component-centric layout encoding | PCB placements | A2C best overall qualitative model; DQNnet improves TEWL across all PCBs and reduces passive overlap, though may increase routing conflicts; most models outperform GT on TEWL except hardest cases U20/U26 (pqac-00000021) | Multiple PCB test cases incl. U20/U26 (pqac-00000021) |
| Basso (2026) | R-GCN encoder + masked PPO RL for analog floorplanning integrated into ANAGEN | Analog IC layout/floorplanning | Circuit graph + spatial masks + 32×32 grid encoding | Floorplans / analog layouts | **67.3% layout-time reduction** and **8.3% mean area reduction** vs manual; OTA case: **13.8% smaller area**, **14.9% shorter wirelength**, huge template/refinement time savings (pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054, pqac-00000055) | 6 industrial analog circuits; OTA case study (pqac-00000054) |
| Zou et al., **PCBSchemaGen** (2025) | Training-free LLM -> SKiDL with datasheet KG + subgraph-isomorphism verifier and iterative feedback | PCB schematic generation | Natural-language specs + IC datasheets/KG constraints | Executable SKiDL, KiCad schematics/layout-ready artifacts | Reports significant improvement in design accuracy and efficiency; compresses datasheet context from ~16k tokens to ~300/component; benchmark spans 23 tasks, but excerpted context gives no full numeric table (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047, pqac-00000061) | 23-task benchmark across digital, analog, power circuits (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000039) |
| King et al., **pcbGPT** (2026) | Tool-grounded program synthesis: natural language -> Python circuit DSL -> KiCad drafts with validation loop | PCB schematic generation | Natural-language requirements + local KiCad libs + datasheets | Editable KiCad project drafts, netlists, schematic artifacts | Best model reaches **pass@1 = 0.90**, **pass@5 = 1.00** on its benchmark; explicitly does **not** automate placement/routing/manufacturing (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000049, pqac-00000059) | Internal benchmark of schematic-synthesis tasks (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059) |
| Luo et al., **SchGen** (2025) | Fine-tuned 20B LLM using semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) for editable schematic generation | PCB schematic generation | Natural-language requests | Executable schematic-editing code -> KiCad schematics/netlists | **82.0% valid circuits**, netlist Jaccard **49.08**, precision **54.87**, recall **52.80**, **60.5%** expert functional correctness; OOD GitHub set **65.59%** valid circuits (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000060, pqac-00000063) | Main benchmark + 988-sample unseen GitHub/KiCad dataset from 20 projects (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000060) |
| Qiu et al., **HWE-Bench** (2025) | Execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design using static rules + LTspice dynamic checks | Board-level schematic design benchmark | Functional requirements + 2,914 IC datasheets | Generated board-level netlists/schematics evaluated for correctness | Static pass avg **71.84%**; dynamic simulation pass around **58.65%**; best end-to-end correct-design rate only **8.15%** (Claude Sonnet 4.5), showing major gap to autonomous board design (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038) | 300 tasks across 8 domains (pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037) |
| Li et al., **PCB-Bench** (2026) | Multimodal benchmark for LLMs on PCB placement/routing reasoning and design understanding | PCB placement/routing benchmark | Text QA, image+text PCB reasoning, 170+ real PCB projects | Evaluated model answers/descriptions, not board files | Shows current LLMs struggle with rule adherence, consistency, semantics; GPT-5 among strongest balanced performers, but no model solves realistic PCB placement/routing reliably (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) | ~3,700 text questions, ~500 multimodal questions, **170+** real designs (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033) |
| Shi et al., **AMSnet-KG** (2025) | KG-RAG for AMS auto-design: spec -> strategy -> retrieved subcircuits -> assembled topology -> Bayesian sizing -> simulation feedback | Analog / mixed-signal netlist generation | Performance specs + AMS knowledge graph/netlist dataset | Topology + sized transistor-level netlists | Dataset extraction reached **97% recall** for component/symbol detection and **96% net-label accuracy**; comparator case with local annotations improved FoM **2.89 vs 1.61**, offset, delay, and power markedly (pqac-00000064, pqac-00000065, pqac-00000066, pqac-00000067, pqac-00000068, pqac-00000069, pqac-00000071) | 894-circuit AMSnet-KG; op-amp and strong-arm comparator case studies (pqac-00000066, pqac-00000068) |
| Krishnamoorthy (2025) | Applied generative-AI guidance for platform hardware design automation: iterative prompting, BOM mapping, component-metadata-aware schematic placement | Platform hardware / PCB design methodology | Functional specs, BOM preferences, component dimensions/pin locations | Editable schematic drafts exportable to layout tools | Qualitative findings: iterative prompting reduces errors; recommend mapping high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs and using pin-aware visual placement; no broad benchmark numbers reported (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) | Practice-driven methodology / recommendations rather than a standardized benchmark (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) |


*Table: This table summarizes recent papers and benchmarks spanning PCB placement, IC/analog floorplanning, and LLM-based schematic generation. function.arguments:
{"answer": "# Bridging the Topology+BOM → Starter Board Gap for Autonomous PCB Routers: A 2025 Survey

## 1. Introduction: The Intermediate Step Problem

Autonomous placement and routing engines such as Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep require, as their minimum input, a complete PCB project containing a captured schematic with a netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical footprints, a board outline, and design rules. They cannot start from a bare circuit topology or block diagram. This survey examines the tools, services, and research methods that attempt to bridge this "topology+BOM → starter board" gap as of 2025, covering commercial platforms (Section 2), design-as-code frameworks (Section 3), recent research on automated placement and LLM-driven schematic generation (Section 4), and an honest gap assessment for a representative mixed-signal board (Section 5).

---

## 2. Commercial and AI-Native Platforms

The following table summarizes the major commercial and emerging research platforms evaluated against the specific intermediate-step requirements:

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 2.1 CELUS Design Platform (CUBOs, Design Assistant)

CELUS operates primarily as a requirements-to-reference-design accelerator, mapping high-level functional requirements to pre-validated solution blocks ("CUBOs") drawn from its component library. Generative AI is applied to reference-design processing for embedded systems, where the platform classifies schematic elements and assembles designs from vetted building blocks (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058). The approach leverages iterative LLM prompting and maps high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs using modular reuse (pqac-00000057). CELUS is strongest in the Altium ecosystem and does not prominently feature headless KiCad export or a public CLI. Pricing is enterprise SaaS. For the topology→starter-board gap, CELUS is most useful when the design maps to its known reference designs; it is weaker for novel or highly custom topologies.

### 2.2 Flux.ai

Flux.ai provides a browser-native ECAD environment with AI copilot features for schematic and PCB editing. It is best characterized as a collaborative design tool with AI assistance rather than a topology-to-board compiler. It does not prominently offer headless/CLI access or native KiCad round-trip export, making it less suitable for a version-controlled CI workflow.

### 2.3 JITX

JITX is among the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, using a Stanza-based hardware description language where generators can elaborate high-level parametric specifications into concrete circuits with selected parts and footprints. Related academic work on Polymorphic Blocks demonstrates how HDL-based systems can support block-level polymorphism (abstract-typed blocks refined into concrete implementations), electronics-model constraint propagation, and netlist export to layout tools (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000019). JITX is strongly scriptable and code-first, making it one of the most credible options for automated starter-board generation. However, it is commercial/proprietary, and KiCad is not its primary export target.

### 2.4 Circuit Mind and Cofactr/Diadem

Circuit Mind provides AI-assisted architecture suggestions and schematic concepts but lacks strong public evidence for complete KiCad starter-project generation. Cofactr/Diadem is supply-chain and BOM-intelligence focused—valuable for grounding component selection in availability and lifecycle data but not itself a schematic-capture or floorplanning tool.

### 2.5 Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep: Input Requirements

HiePlace benchmarks explicitly compare against Quilter on academic PCB placement tasks, demonstrating that Quilter operates as a downstream placement/routing engine that consumes an already-prepared board design with components, footprints, nets, and board outline (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025). Quilter achieved placement costs ranging from 19.46×10³ to 35.70×10³ on four academic benchmarks, with runtimes of 34–75 minutes, while HiePlace reduced costs by 76.7%–91.3% with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (pqac-00000024). DeepPCB/InstaDeep similarly requires board-level physical-design inputs. Neither tool fills the topology→starter-board gap; they are explicitly consumers of the output that this gap analysis seeks to produce.

---

## 3. Design-as-Code and Programmatic Frameworks

<artifact id="artifact-01"/>

### 3.1 SKiDL (Python)

SKiDL is the most widely adopted open-source code-first schematic/netlist layer for KiCad automation. It serves as the backend representation for multiple LLM-driven systems: PCBSchemaGen generates executable SKiDL code from natural-language specifications and verifies it against a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010), while SchGen and HWE-Bench also reference SKiDL workflows (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000044). SKiDL generates netlists that can produce KiCad schematics, supports real parts and footprint assignment, and is fully scriptable/CI-ready. It does not, however, provide board-outline definition or automatic placement.

### 3.2 BHDL (Racket eDSL)

BHDL is a declarative, Racket-embedded DSL that uniquely combines schematic capture with automated placement. It models every circuit element as a function, supports hierarchical composition via wire/layout clauses, and includes an auto-placement engine consisting of a global analytical placer (based on RePlace, minimizing HPWL) and a simulated-annealing detailed placer (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000004). BHDL exports to KiCad PCB format (exportable to Gerber), produces a BOM, and supports KiCad-format footprint libraries by default (pqac-00000002). The auto-placer is implemented in Julia for GPU acceleration (pqac-00000002). This makes BHDL the strongest research demonstration of a code→KiCad PCB + auto-placed layout pipeline, though it remains a research prototype rather than a production tool.

### 3.3 Polymorphic Blocks / EDG

Polymorphic Blocks extends hierarchical block diagrams with type polymorphism and electronics modeling. Users write Python HDL code that builds a hierarchical tree model; a compiler performs tree-walk lowering, applies refinements, invokes generators, and updates a constraint graph (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000007). The system can export netlists with footprint annotations for import into KiCad (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019). It does not directly generate board outlines or perform automatic placement, leaving those steps downstream (pqac-00000018).

### 3.4 atopile

atopile is a fast-moving open-source hardware description language strongly oriented to Git/version-control and CI workflows. It generates netlists, schematic/PCB project artifacts, and BOMs via libraries, with KiCad as its primary target. Board outline and floorplan support exists but remains limited compared to schematic/netlist generation. atopile is one of the most promising tools for headless starter-board generation in a coding-agent workflow.

### 3.5 tscircuit (TypeScript/JavaScript)

tscircuit provides software-engineer-friendly programmatic circuit and PCB descriptions in TypeScript. It can emit schematic/PCB data structures, footprints, and web-native rendered artifacts. KiCad interoperability is improving but not yet canonical. It is highly scriptable and CI-friendly but still earlier-stage for production mixed-signal boards.

### 3.6 KiCad Python/IPC API + kicad-cli

KiCad's native Python API and CLI (kicad-cli) provide the production-grade backend for headless automation: DRC, ERC, plotting, export, library queries, and PCB manipulation. They do not constitute a high-level design language but are the essential execution layer for any code-first pipeline targeting KiCad output.

---

## 4. Recent Research on Automated Placement, Floorplanning, and LLM-Driven Schematic Generation (2020–2025)

<artifact id="artifact-02"/>

### 4.1 RL/ML-Based Placement and Floorplanning

**HiePlace** (Li et al., 2025, IEEE TCAD) is the current state of the art in PCB-specific placement. It uses hierarchical mathematical programming with dynamic programming for first-priority components and a relaxation algorithm to reduce Boolean variables. On 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic), it achieves an average 66% cost reduction and 7.7× speedup versus baselines. Versus Quilter specifically, cost reductions of 76.7%–91.3% are reported with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000078).

**Vassallo & Bajada** (DATE 2024) demonstrate end-to-end RL for PCB placement using TD3 and SAC agents, achieving 17% and 21% average post-routing wirelength reductions versus simulated annealing on unseen circuits, with convergence sometimes exceeding an order of magnitude faster (pqac-00000020).

**Vashisht et al.** (2020) combine cyclic RL with simulated annealing using sequence-pair representations, showing that RL-initialized SA consistently outperforms random initialization on ami49 and lattice benchmarks (pqac-00000022).

**Wang et al.** (2024) apply DRL with graph neural networks to chip floorplanning on ISPD 2005 benchmarks, achieving a 31.4% average HPWL reduction and 34.2% timing-violation reduction versus baselines including DREAMPlace (pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052).

**Li et al.** (2025, PA-ICM) address double-layer PCB module placement with mixed-variable optimization, achieving automatic placement in minutes on industrial cases (pqac-00000072, pqac-00000076).

**Basso** (2026) combines R-GCN encoders with masked PPO for analog circuit floorplanning, reporting 67.3% layout-time reduction and 8.3% mean area reduction versus manual design on six industrial circuits (pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054).

### 4.2 LLM-Driven Schematic and Netlist Generation

**pcbGPT** (King et al., 2026) is a tool-augmented LLM synthesis agent that converts natural-language requirements into editable KiCad project drafts. It uses a custom Python circuit DSL, grounds generation in local KiCad libraries and datasheet-derived rules, and employs a generate–execute–repair validation loop. The best model achieves pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00 on its benchmark. Critically, the authors explicitly state it "does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation" (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059).

**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al., 2025) is a training-free framework that uses LLMs to propose SKiDL code, verified by a datasheet-driven knowledge graph and subgraph-isomorphism checker. It compresses datasheet context from ~16k to ~300 tokens per component and covers 23 benchmark tasks across digital, analog, and power domains, claiming significant accuracy improvements (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047).

**SchGen** (Luo et al., 2025) fine-tunes a 20B-parameter LLM using a semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) to generate editable KiCad schematics. It achieves 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert functional correctness, outperforming raw KiCad baselines (32% valid) and matching larger frontier models despite its smaller size (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000063).

### 4.3 Benchmarks Revealing the Gap

**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al., 2025) is the most rigorous execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design. Using 300 tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets, it applies static rule checking plus LTspice dynamic simulation. The best-performing model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves only 8.15% end-to-end correct-design rate, with static pass rates around 71.84% and dynamic simulation pass rates around 58.65% (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037).

**PCB-Bench** (Li et al., 2026) provides a multimodal benchmark with ~3,700 text questions, ~500 image+text questions, and 170+ real PCB designs. Results show current LLMs struggle with spatial placement reasoning, rule adherence, and engineering artifacts, with no single model dominating all dimensions (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031).

**AMSnet-KG** (Shi et al., 2025) addresses the analog/mixed-signal domain with a knowledge-graph dataset of 894 circuits enabling LLM-based RAG for automated topology selection and Bayesian sizing. Case studies on op-amps and comparators demonstrate end-to-end design from specifications to sized netlists, with local annotations materially improving optimization convergence (pqac-00000065, pqac-00000066, pqac-00000068).

---

## 5. Gap Assessment: Mixed-Signal Motor + Sensor Control Board

For a concrete low-cost, open-hardware board with an MCU driving stepper drivers, solenoids, servos, vibration motors, and an HX711/load-cell front end:

### 5.1 What Is Automatable Today

**Schematic capture** of well-documented subcircuits (MCU breakout, standard voltage regulators, connectors) can be partially automated via SKiDL scripts, pcbGPT, or PCBSchemaGen. pcbGPT achieves 0.90 pass@1 on schematic drafts, making it the strongest NL→KiCad schematic generator (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059). PCBSchemaGen's KG-verified SKiDL pipeline adds datasheet-grounded constraint checking (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009).

**Component selection** for standard parts (resistors, capacitors, common ICs) can be partially grounded via Cofactr/Diadem supply-chain intelligence or PCBSchemaGen's knowledge graph. Krishnamoorthy (2025) recommends mapping high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs and using iterative LLM prompting with temperature control to reduce generation errors (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058).

**Netlist and BOM generation** from code is mature via SKiDL or atopile pipelines feeding KiCad's CLI.

### 5.2 What Still Requires a Human

**Power-tree design and protection circuits** for motor drivers (high-current switching, flyback diodes, snubber networks for solenoids) require domain expertise that current LLMs handle poorly—HWE-Bench's 8.15% end-to-end correctness rate underscores this (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000037).

**Analog front-end design** (HX711 + load cell, precision reference, EMI filtering) is particularly challenging. AMSnet-KG demonstrates that even for simpler analog circuits like op-amps, LLM-generated topologies sometimes fail to meet target specifications and require topology regeneration (pqac-00000069).

**Board outline and mechanical integration** (connector placement, mounting holes, enclosure constraints) are not automated by any current tool. This is a fundamental gap: no system generates a board outline from functional requirements.

**Floorplanning and placement regions** (separating power-switching sections from sensitive analog sections, keeping stepper-driver heat sinks away from the HX711, managing ground-plane partitioning) require mixed-signal domain knowledge that none of the current AI systems reliably encode.

**Design-rule specification** (impedance-controlled traces for motor PWM, guard rings for the load-cell front end, current-carrying trace widths) must be manually defined.

### 5.3 Headless/CI-Compatible Tool Chain

For a version-controlled, coding-agent workflow with no interactive GUI:

- **Best current stack**: atopile or SKiDL (source language) → KiCad Python API + kicad-cli (execution/export) → manual or scripted board outline + design rules → Quilter or FreeRouting (downstream placement/routing).
- **Most promising research addition**: PCBSchemaGen (open-source, LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with verification) or pcbGPT (NL→KiCad project drafts) as a schematic-generation front end.
- **BHDL** is the only system demonstrated to produce auto-placed KiCad PCBs from code, but it remains a research prototype (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).
- **JITX** is the most mature commercial option for programmatic generation but is proprietary and not KiCad-native.

### 5.4 Realistic Assessment

For the described mixed-signal motor/sensor board, approximately 40–60% of the schematic capture could be accelerated by AI tools (standard subcircuits, MCU pin assignments, decoupling). The remaining 40–60% (power tree, analog front end, protection circuits, mixed-signal partitioning) requires human engineering review. Board outline, floorplan, and design rules remain essentially 100% manual. The complete "topology+BOM → KiCad starter board ready for Quilter" pipeline does not exist as a turnkey automated solution in 2025.

---

## 6. Conclusions and Recommendations

**Most credible gap-fillers for feeding Quilter/DeepPCB:**

1. **PCBSchemaGen** (open-source, training-free LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with KG+SI verification) is the most credible research system for producing verified KiCad schematics from natural-language specifications, with explicit grounding in real IC datasheets and pin constraints (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000047).

2. **pcbGPT** achieves the highest reported schematic-synthesis accuracy (pass@1 = 0.90) and outputs native KiCad project files, but does not address placement/routing and remains a research prototype (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059).

3. **SKiDL + KiCad CLI** is the most mature, scriptable, CI-ready stack for code-first KiCad project generation, though it requires the human (or an upstream AI) to author the circuit description (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000044).

4. **atopile** is the most promising emerging open-source HDL for version-controlled, Git-native PCB generation with KiCad as primary target.

5. **BHDL** uniquely demonstrates auto-placement integrated with code-first KiCad PCB generation, making it the closest existing system to a complete code→placed-board pipeline (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).

6. **HiePlace** represents the state of the art in PCB-specific placement optimization and could serve as the placement stage between schematic generation and routing, though it is not publicly available as a packaged tool (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025).

The fundamental bottleneck remains that no single system reliably converts a bare topology or block diagram into a complete, footprinted, outlined, design-rule-annotated KiCad project suitable for autonomous routing. The schematic/netlist generation layer is rapidly improving via LLM-based approaches, but board outline specification, mixed-signal floorplanning, and domain-specific design-rule encoding remain human-dependent. For a practical CI pipeline today, the recommended approach is to use a code-first framework (atopile or SKiDL) for the schematic/netlist layer, manually define the board outline and placement constraints, and then feed the resulting KiCad project to an autonomous router."}

{"answer": "# Bridging the Topology+BOM → Starter Board Gap for Autonomous PCB Routers: A 2025 Survey

## 1. Introduction: The Intermediate Step Problem

Autonomous placement and routing engines such as Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep require, as their minimum input, a complete PCB project containing a captured schematic with a netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical footprints, a board outline, and design rules. They cannot start from a bare circuit topology or block diagram. This survey examines the tools, services, and research methods that attempt to bridge this "topology+BOM → starter board" gap as of 2025, covering commercial platforms (Section 2), design-as-code frameworks (Section 3), recent research on automated placement and LLM-driven schematic generation (Section 4), and an honest gap assessment for a representative mixed-signal board (Section 5).

---

## 2. Commercial and AI-Native Platforms

The following table summarizes the major commercial and emerging research platforms evaluated against the specific intermediate-step requirements:

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 2.1 CELUS Design Platform (CUBOs, Design Assistant)

CELUS operates primarily as a requirements-to-reference-design accelerator, mapping high-level functional requirements to pre-validated solution blocks ("CUBOs") drawn from its component library. Generative AI is applied to reference-design processing for embedded systems, where the platform classifies schematic elements and assembles designs from vetted building blocks (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058). The approach leverages iterative LLM prompting and maps high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs using modular reuse (pqac-00000057). CELUS is strongest in the Altium ecosystem and does not prominently feature headless KiCad export or a public CLI. Pricing is enterprise SaaS. For the topology→starter-board gap, CELUS is most useful when the design maps to its known reference designs; it is weaker for novel or highly custom topologies.

### 2.2 Flux.ai

Flux.ai provides a browser-native ECAD environment with AI copilot features for schematic and PCB editing. It is best characterized as a collaborative design tool with AI assistance rather than a topology-to-board compiler. It does not prominently offer headless/CLI access or native KiCad round-trip export, making it less suitable for a version-controlled CI workflow.

### 2.3 JITX

JITX is among the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, using a Stanza-based hardware description language where generators can elaborate high-level parametric specifications into concrete circuits with selected parts and footprints. Related academic work on Polymorphic Blocks demonstrates how HDL-based systems can support block-level polymorphism (abstract-typed blocks refined into concrete implementations), electronics-model constraint propagation, and netlist export to layout tools (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000019). JITX is strongly scriptable and code-first, making it one of the most credible options for automated starter-board generation. However, it is commercial/proprietary, and KiCad is not its primary export target.

<artifact id="artifact-01"/>

### 3.1 SKiDL (Python)

It serves as the backend representation for multiple LLM-driven systems: PCBSchemaGen generates executable SKiDL code from natural-language specifications and verifies it against a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010), while SchGen and HWE-Bench also reference SKiDL workflows (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000044). BHDL exports to KiCad PCB format (exportable to Gerber), produces a BOM, and supports KiCad-format footprint libraries by default (pqac-00000002). The auto-placer is implemented in Julia for GPU acceleration (pqac-00000002). This makes BHDL the strongest research demonstration of a code→KiCad PCB + auto-placed layout pipeline, though it remains a research prototype rather than a production tool.

### 3.4 atopile

atopile is a fast-moving open-source hardware description language strongly oriented to Git/version-control and CI workflows. <artifact id="artifact-02"/>

It uses hierarchical mathematical programming with dynamic programming for first-priority components and a relaxation algorithm to reduce Boolean variables. On 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic), it achieves an average 66% cost reduction and 7.7× speedup versus baselines. Versus Quilter specifically, cost reductions of 76.7%–91.3% are reported with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000078).

**Vassallo & Bajada** (DATE 2024) demonstrate end-to-end RL for PCB placement using TD3 and SAC agents, achieving 17% and 21% average post-routing wirelength reductions versus simulated annealing on unseen circuits, with convergence sometimes exceeding an order of magnitude faster (pqac-00000020).

**Vashisht et al.** (2020) combine cyclic RL with simulated annealing using sequence-pair representations, showing that RL-initialized SA consistently outperforms random initialization on ami49 and lattice benchmarks (pqac-00000022).

**Wang et al.** (2024) apply DRL with graph neural networks to chip floorplanning on ISPD 2005 benchmarks, achieving a 31.4% average HPWL reduction and 34.2% timing-violation reduction versus baselines including DREAMPlace (pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052).

**Li et al.** (2025, PA-ICM) address double-layer PCB module placement with mixed-variable optimization, achieving automatic placement in minutes on industrial cases (pqac-00000072, pqac-00000076).

**Basso** (2026) combines R-GCN encoders with masked PPO for analog circuit floorplanning, reporting 67.3% layout-time reduction and 8.3% mean area reduction versus manual design on six industrial circuits (pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054).

### 4.2 LLM-Driven Schematic and Netlist Generation

**pcbGPT** (King et al., 2026) is a tool-augmented LLM synthesis agent that converts natural-language requirements into editable KiCad project drafts. It uses a custom Python circuit DSL, grounds generation in local KiCad libraries and datasheet-derived rules, and employs a generate–execute–repair validation loop. The best model achieves pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00 on its benchmark. Critically, the authors explicitly state it "does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation" (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059).

**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al., 2025) is a training-free framework that uses LLMs to propose SKiDL code, verified by a datasheet-driven knowledge graph and subgraph-isomorphism checker. It compresses datasheet context from ~16k to ~300 tokens per component and covers 23 benchmark tasks across digital, analog, and power domains, claiming significant accuracy improvements (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047).

**SchGen** (Luo et al., 2025) fine-tunes a 20B-parameter LLM using a semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) to generate editable KiCad schematics. It achieves 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert functional correctness, outperforming raw KiCad baselines (32% valid) and matching larger frontier models despite its smaller size (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000063).

### 4.3 Benchmarks Revealing the Gap

**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al., 2025) is the most rigorous execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design. Using 300 tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets, it applies static rule checking plus LTspice dynamic simulation. The best-performing model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves only 8.15% end-to-end correct-design rate, with static pass rates around 71.84% and dynamic simulation pass rates around 58.65% (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037).

**PCB-Bench** (Li et al., 2026) provides a multimodal benchmark with ~3,700 text questions, ~500 image+text questions, and 170+ real PCB designs. Results show current LLMs struggle with spatial placement reasoning, rule adherence, and engineering artifacts, with no single model dominating all dimensions (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031).

**AMSnet-KG** (Shi et al., 2025) addresses the analog/mixed-signal domain with a knowledge-graph dataset of 894 circuits enabling LLM-based RAG for automated topology selection and Bayesian sizing. Case studies on op-amps and comparators demonstrate end-to-end design from specifications to sized netlists, with local annotations materially improving optimization convergence (pqac-00000065, pqac-00000066, pqac-00000068).

---

## 5. Gap Assessment: Mixed-Signal Motor + Sensor Control Board

For a concrete low-cost, open-hardware board with an MCU driving stepper drivers, solenoids, servos, vibration motors, and an HX711/load-cell front end:

### 5.1 What Is Automatable Today

**Schematic capture** of well-documented subcircuits (MCU breakout, standard voltage regulators, connectors) can be partially automated via SKiDL scripts, pcbGPT, or PCBSchemaGen. pcbGPT achieves 0.90 pass@1 on schematic drafts, making it the strongest NL→KiCad schematic generator (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059). PCBSchemaGen's KG-verified SKiDL pipeline adds datasheet-grounded constraint checking (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009).

**Component selection** for standard parts (resistors, capacitors, common ICs) can be partially grounded via Cofactr/Diadem supply-chain intelligence or PCBSchemaGen's knowledge graph. Krishnamoorthy (2025) recommends mapping high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs and using iterative LLM prompting with temperature control to reduce generation errors (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058).

**Netlist and BOM generation** from code is mature via SKiDL or atopile pipelines feeding KiCad's CLI.

### 5.2 What Still Requires a Human

**Power-tree design and protection circuits** for motor drivers (high-current switching, flyback diodes, snubber networks for solenoids) require domain expertise that current LLMs handle poorly—HWE-Bench's 8.15% end-to-end correctness rate underscores this (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000037).

**Analog front-end design** (HX711 + load cell, precision reference, EMI filtering) is particularly challenging. AMSnet-KG demonstrates that even for simpler analog circuits like op-amps, LLM-generated topologies sometimes fail to meet target specifications and require topology regeneration (pqac-00000069).

**Board outline and mechanical integration** (connector placement, mounting holes, enclosure constraints) are not automated by any current tool. This is a fundamental gap: no system generates a board outline from functional requirements.

**Floorplanning and placement regions** (separating power-switching sections from sensitive analog sections, keeping stepper-driver heat sinks away from the HX711, managing ground-plane partitioning) require mixed-signal domain knowledge that none of the current AI systems reliably encode.

**Design-rule specification** (impedance-controlled traces for motor PWM, guard rings for the load-cell front end, current-carrying trace widths) must be manually defined.

### 5.3 Headless/CI-Compatible Tool Chain

For a version-controlled, coding-agent workflow with no interactive GUI:

- **Best current stack**: atopile or SKiDL (source language) → KiCad Python API + kicad-cli (execution/export) → manual or scripted board outline + design rules → Quilter or FreeRouting (downstream placement/routing).
- **Most promising research addition**: PCBSchemaGen (open-source, LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with verification) or pcbGPT (NL→KiCad project drafts) as a schematic-generation front end.
- **BHDL** is the only system demonstrated to produce auto-placed KiCad PCBs from code, but it remains a research prototype (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).
- **JITX** is the most mature commercial option for programmatic generation but is proprietary and not KiCad-native.

### 5.4 Realistic Assessment

For the described mixed-signal motor/sensor board, approximately 40–60% of the schematic capture could be accelerated by AI tools (standard subcircuits, MCU pin assignments, decoupling). The remaining 40–60% (power tree, analog front end, protection circuits, mixed-signal partitioning) requires human engineering review. Board outline, floorplan, and design rules remain essentially 100% manual. The complete "topology+BOM → KiCad starter board ready for Quilter" pipeline does not exist as a turnkey automated solution in 2025.

---

## 6. Conclusions and Recommendations

**Most credible gap-fillers for feeding Quilter/DeepPCB:**

1. **PCBSchemaGen** (open-source, training-free LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with KG+SI verification) is the most credible research system for producing verified KiCad schematics from natural-language specifications, with explicit grounding in real IC datasheets and pin constraints (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000047).

2. **pcbGPT** achieves the highest reported schematic-synthesis accuracy (pass@1 = 0.90) and outputs native KiCad project files, but does not address placement/routing and remains a research prototype (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059).

3. **SKiDL + KiCad CLI** is the most mature, scriptable, CI-ready stack for code-first KiCad project generation, though it requires the human (or an upstream AI) to author the circuit description (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000044).

4. **atopile** is the most promising emerging open-source HDL for version-controlled, Git-native PCB generation with KiCad as primary target.

5. **BHDL** uniquely demonstrates auto-placement integrated with code-first KiCad PCB generation, making it the closest existing system to a complete code→placed-board pipeline (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).

6. **HiePlace** represents the state of the art in PCB-specific placement optimization and could serve as the placement stage between schematic generation and routing, though it is not publicly available as a packaged tool (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025).

The fundamental bottleneck remains that no single system reliably converts a bare topology or block diagram into a complete, footprinted, outlined, design-rule-annotated KiCad project suitable for autonomous routing. The schematic/netlist generation layer is rapidly improving via LLM-based approaches, but board outline specification, mixed-signal floorplanning, and domain-specific design-rule encoding remain human-dependent. For a practical CI pipeline today, the recommended approach is to use a code-first framework (atopile or SKiDL) for the schematic/netlist layer, manually define the board outline and placement constraints, and then feed the resulting KiCad project to an autonomous router."}

state.question:
Survey, as of 2025, the tools, services, and research methods that use generative AI / automation to perform the INTERMEDIATE step of the PCB design pipeline: taking a high-level circuit TOPOLOGY or block diagram, a list of COMPONENTS (or functional requirements/specifications), and producing a basic 'starter' board design that a downstream autonomous placement/routing engine (such as Quilter or DeepPCB / InstaDeep) can ingest. Concretely, the target output of this intermediate step is a captured SCHEMATIC with a complete netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical FOOTPRINTS, a Bill of Materials, and ideally a preliminary board OUTLINE and component FLOORPLAN, exported as native KiCad (or Altium/EAGLE) files. Because autonomous routers explicitly cannot start from a bare topology (they require footprints, a board outline, and design rules), this 'topology+BOM -> starter board' gap is the focus.

Cover specifically:
(a) commercial / AI-native platforms that claim to bridge concept or block diagram to schematic + floorplan, e.g. CELUS (CELUS Design Platform, CUBOs, Design Assistant), Flux.ai (AI schematic copilot), JITX (programmatic design / algorithmic floorplanning), Circuit Mind, Cofactr/Diadem, Quilter and DeepPCB's own input requirements and any pre-layout helpers; for each: what input it accepts (topology/block diagram vs. full schematic), what it outputs (schematic, netlist, footprints, BOM, board outline, floorplan), which ECAD formats it exports (KiCad?), whether it has a public API / CLI / scriptable interface vs. GUI/login-only, pricing/free-tier, and maturity.
(b) design-as-code / programmatic frameworks that can generate a footprinted, netlisted KiCad project from a higher-level description (atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, Horizon EDA, KiCad's Python / IPC API + kicad-cli, PCBmodE), and how much of board-outline/floorplan generation they automate vs. leave manual.
(c) recent (2020-2025) peer-reviewed / arXiv research on automatically going from netlist or topology to an initial PLACEMENT / FLOORPLAN / board outline (analog/PCB placement, constructive floorplanning, ML/RL placement, LLM-driven schematic-to-layout, force-directed or partitioning-based initial placement), including benchmarks and reported success rates.
(d) honest assessment of the gap: for a low-cost, open-hardware, mixed-signal motor + sensor control board (microcontroller driving stepper drivers, solenoids, servos, vibration motors, and an HX711/load-cell front end), how automatable is this intermediate step today, what still requires a human, and which tools are realistically usable from a headless, version-controlled, CI / coding-agent workflow (no interactive GUI).

For each tool/method give: what it is, the concrete input->output, ECAD/KiCad interoperability, scriptability/API availability, licensing/cost, maturity, and cited evidence (vendor documentation and ~8-15 recent peer-reviewed or arXiv papers). Conclude with which tools most credibly fill the topology+BOM -> KiCad-starter-board gap and feed cleanly into Quilter/DeepPCB.
