answer.answer:
# Bridging the Topology+BOM → Starter Board Gap for Autonomous PCB Routers: A 2025 Survey

## 1. Introduction: The Intermediate Step Problem

Autonomous placement and routing engines such as Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep require, as their minimum input, a complete PCB project containing a captured schematic with a netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical footprints, a board outline, and design rules. They cannot start from a bare circuit topology or block diagram. This survey examines the tools, services, and research methods that attempt to bridge this "topology+BOM → starter board" gap as of 2025, covering commercial platforms (Section 2), design-as-code frameworks (Section 3), recent research on automated placement and LLM-driven schematic generation (Section 4), and an honest gap assessment for a representative mixed-signal board (Section 5).

---

## 2. Commercial and AI-Native Platforms

The following table summarizes the major commercial and emerging research platforms evaluated against the specific intermediate-step requirements:

| Tool/Platform | Input (what it accepts) | Output (schematic/netlist/footprints/BOM/outline/floorplan) | ECAD Export | Scriptability/API | Pricing/Licensing | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CELUS Design Platform / CUBOs / Design Assistant | High-level functional requirements, block-diagram-style intent, reference-design/library-driven selections; strongest when mapping requirements to known solution blocks rather than arbitrary bare topologies. Academic references describe automated reference-design processing and schematic generation around CELUS libraries. (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2) | Claimed/marketed: generated schematic drafts, component selection/BOM, design documentation; practical floorplanning/outline generation appears limited and typically downstream in partner ECAD. Best viewed as requirements/block intent -> schematic/BOM accelerator, not a full autonomous starter-board generator. (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2) | Altium-centric in practice; no strong public evidence of native KiCad export in the cited research context. | Primarily SaaS GUI/workspace; public headless API/CLI not prominent in available evidence. | Commercial enterprise SaaS; pricing not generally public. | Commercial and deployed, but evidence suggests strongest maturity in requirements-to-reference-design synthesis rather than headless topology->KiCad starter-board export. (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2) |
| Flux.ai | Usually starts from human-authored schematic/PCB or copilot-assisted editing in a browser ECAD; accepts natural-language assistance, component/library queries, and collaborative schematic edits more than pure block diagrams. | Browser-native schematic + PCB editing, shared libraries/BOM workflows; can help assign footprints and prepare PCB projects, but public evidence for fully automatic block-diagram -> complete starter board is limited. | Flux native; interoperability with standard ECAD formats exists, but KiCad-native round-trip/headless export is not its core story. | Web product; some integrations, but not a broadly documented public CLI-first workflow. | Commercial SaaS with team/business tiers; pricing changes over time. | Mature collaborative ECAD with AI copilot features, but not the most credible headless bridge for topology+BOM -> KiCad starter board. |
| JITX | Code/programmatic hardware specification using Stanza-based generators; best input is parameterized circuit/programmatic intent, not an informal block diagram. Related academic systems describe high-level HDL/generator flows that elaborate to concrete circuits and footprints. (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 45-47, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45) | Concrete circuit implementations, netlists, selected parts/footprints, manufacturable design artifacts; can automate repetitive schematic capture and some placement/floorplan logic through generators. | Exports to mainstream PCB CAD/manufacturing flows; KiCad is not the primary native target. | Strong scriptability/programmatic interface; this is the main value proposition. | Commercial proprietary. | One of the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, especially for reusable generator-based designs; credible for code-first starter-board generation, weaker for raw natural-language topology ingestion. (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45) |
| Circuit Mind | Natural-language / requirement-driven electronics design assistant focused on suggesting architectures and schematics; best for early concept exploration. | Schematic concepts, component suggestions, documentation; public evidence for complete footprinted ECAD projects with board outline/floorplan is limited. | Limited public evidence of native KiCad starter-project export. | Mostly GUI/web workflow; public CLI/API not clearly established. | Commercial; pricing typically by demo/contact. | Emerging commercial AI assistant; useful upstream, but not clearly proven as a full topology+BOM -> footprinted starter-board bridge. |
| Cofactr / Diadem | Supply-chain/BOM/procurement-centric inputs: AML/AVL, sourcing constraints, approved parts, lifecycle, inventory; not primarily a topology-to-schematic authoring tool. | Strong on vetted manufacturer parts, BOM enrichment, sourcing, compliance, lifecycle/risk data; does not itself appear to generate a complete captured schematic + floorplan. | Integrates with enterprise PLM/EDA data rather than acting as a KiCad project generator. | API/integration oriented on supply-chain data more than ECAD synthesis. | Commercial enterprise software. | Mature for BOM intelligence and part selection; useful as a component-grounding layer, not a complete starter-board synthesizer. |
| Quilter | Input is already a PCB project with netlist, footprints, board outline, constraints, and usually pre-placement context; cited PCB-placement literature uses Quilter as a placement/routing baseline, not as a block-diagram-to-schematic tool. HiePlace compares against Quilter on academic benchmarks. (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11) | Autonomous placement/routing assistance on an existing board design; does **not** fill the topology+BOM -> starter-board gap itself. (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 1-2) | Works on existing PCB CAD data; not presented in cited evidence as a schematic capture/export platform. | Commercial tool workflow; public general-purpose CLI/API not the main story in available evidence. | Commercial. | Strong downstream physical-design tool; explicitly depends on upstream starter-board artifacts already existing. (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 3-4) |
| DeepPCB / InstaDeep | Similar to Quilter: requires board-level physical-design inputs (placed/unplaced components with footprints, nets, constraints, board geometry). Research and benchmark context treats it as a placement/routing-stage method. | Placement/routing optimization, not schematic/BOM capture. | Consumes existing PCB design representations; not a schematic-capture export system. | Research/commercial-access dependent; not a public general-purpose CLI-first topology compiler. | Commercial/research-collaboration oriented. | Important downstream autonomous-layout reference, but not a solution to the intermediate topology->starter-board step. |
| pcbGPT | Natural-language hardware requirements; grounded by local KiCad libraries, exact component names, pin maps, footprint candidates, and datasheet-derived rules. Uses a Python circuit DSL instead of raw SKiDL. (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 4-6, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 6-8, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 8-10, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 1-2) | Editable KiCad project drafts, schematic artifacts, netlists, JSON/graph exports; focuses on schematic generation and draft project creation, not reliable autonomous placement/routing/manufacturing prep. Reported pass@1 0.90, pass@5 1.00 on its benchmark, but authors state it does not automate placement/routing. (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 6-8, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 8-10, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 1-2, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24) | KiCad-compatible projects (.kicad_sch/.kicad_pcb/.kicad_pro) and netlists. (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 1-2) | Research prototype with tool-augmented execution/validation loop; not yet a production public API/CLI platform. | Research code/prototype. | Among the most credible research systems for NL -> KiCad schematic drafts, but still weak on floorplan/outline and not production-ready. (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 2-4, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24) |
| PCBSchemaGen | Natural-language specifications plus datasheet-derived knowledge graph constraints; generates executable SKiDL code with verifier feedback. Covers digital/analog/power tasks. (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | Verified SKiDL code, KiCad schematics, and layout-ready KiCad artifacts; emphasizes structural correctness via KG + subgraph-isomorphism verification. Public evidence is stronger for schematic/netlist correctness than for automatic board outline/floorplan. (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | SKiDL -> KiCad workflow; explicitly aims at KiCad-ready files. (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | Research pipeline; open-sourced according to the paper, but not yet a polished commercial API product. (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | Research/open prototype. | Very promising as a topology/requirements -> verified KiCad schematic bridge; less mature on physical floorplanning and production workflow hardening. |
| SchGen | Natural-language user requests; finetuned 20B LLM generates semantic-grounded schematic-editing code rather than raw KiCad text. (luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 8-9, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 1-3, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 3-4, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 4-6) | Editable schematic code that executes to valid KiCad schematic files/netlists; benchmarked at 82% valid circuits and 60.5% expert functional correctness. Focus is schematic generation, not board-outline/floorplan automation. (luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 8-9, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 1-3, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 3-4) | KiCad target format via generated executable editing code. (luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 1-3, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 4-6) | Research model/code pipeline; not a commercial headless PCB compiler product. | Research/open prototype. | Strong research result on NL -> editable KiCad schematics; not yet a complete starter-board generator for autonomous routers. |
| Bottom line for this gap | Bare topology/block diagram alone is still rarely enough for robust automation. The most credible current bridges are: **commercial** code/reference-design systems like JITX/CELUS for constrained domains, and **research** systems like pcbGPT/PCBSchemaGen/SchGen for KiCad schematic generation. Quilter/DeepPCB remain downstream consumers, not gap-fillers. (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24) | A usable “starter board” for autonomous routers still usually needs human decisions on exact parts, footprints, power-tree safety, board outline, placement regions, keepouts, analog partitioning, and connector/mechanical details. HWE-Bench’s best full-correct rate of only 8.15% underscores the current gap. (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2) | KiCad-centered research is improving rapidly; production-grade end-to-end automation is not yet solved. | Headless/scriptable today: strongest in code-first/open flows, weaker in commercial GUI-first platforms. | Mixed commercial + research landscape. | As of 2025, the topology+BOM -> KiCad-starter-board gap is **partially fillable**, not fully solved. (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24) |


*Table: This table compares commercial platforms and recent research systems on the specific intermediate PCB-design gap: turning high-level requirements or topology into a schematic/netlist/BOM/footprinted starter board. It highlights which tools are real candidates for feeding downstream autonomous placement/routing engines and where the major limitations still are.*

### 2.1 CELUS Design Platform (CUBOs, Design Assistant)

CELUS operates primarily as a requirements-to-reference-design accelerator, mapping high-level functional requirements to pre-validated solution blocks ("CUBOs") drawn from its component library. Generative AI is applied to reference-design processing for embedded systems, where the platform classifies schematic elements and assembles designs from vetted building blocks (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2). The approach leverages iterative LLM prompting and maps high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs using modular reuse (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4). CELUS is strongest in the Altium ecosystem and does not prominently feature headless KiCad export or a public CLI. Pricing is enterprise SaaS. For the topology→starter-board gap, CELUS is most useful when the design maps to its known reference designs; it is weaker for novel or highly custom topologies.

### 2.2 Flux.ai

Flux.ai provides a browser-native ECAD environment with AI copilot features for schematic and PCB editing. It is best characterized as a collaborative design tool with AI assistance rather than a topology-to-board compiler. It does not prominently offer headless/CLI access or native KiCad round-trip export, making it less suitable for a version-controlled CI workflow.

### 2.3 JITX

JITX is among the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, using a Stanza-based hardware description language where generators can elaborate high-level parametric specifications into concrete circuits with selected parts and footprints. Related academic work on Polymorphic Blocks demonstrates how HDL-based systems can support block-level polymorphism (abstract-typed blocks refined into concrete implementations), electronics-model constraint propagation, and netlist export to layout tools (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 45-47, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45). JITX is strongly scriptable and code-first, making it one of the most credible options for automated starter-board generation. However, it is commercial/proprietary, and KiCad is not its primary export target.

### 2.4 Circuit Mind and Cofactr/Diadem

Circuit Mind provides AI-assisted architecture suggestions and schematic concepts but lacks strong public evidence for complete KiCad starter-project generation. Cofactr/Diadem is supply-chain and BOM-intelligence focused—valuable for grounding component selection in availability and lifecycle data but not itself a schematic-capture or floorplanning tool.

### 2.5 Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep: Input Requirements

HiePlace benchmarks explicitly compare against Quilter on academic PCB placement tasks, demonstrating that Quilter operates as a downstream placement/routing engine that consumes an already-prepared board design with components, footprints, nets, and board outline (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 3-4). Quilter achieved placement costs ranging from 19.46×10³ to 35.70×10³ on four academic benchmarks, with runtimes of 34–75 minutes, while HiePlace reduced costs by 76.7%–91.3% with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11). DeepPCB/InstaDeep similarly requires board-level physical-design inputs. Neither tool fills the topology→starter-board gap; they are explicitly consumers of the output that this gap analysis seeks to produce.

*Table: This table compares the main design-as-code and scriptable PCB frameworks relevant to generating footprinted, netlisted KiCad starter projects from higher-level descriptions. It highlights where automation is already strong—schematic/netlist/BOM generation—and where it remains weak, especially board outline and floorplanning.*

### 3.1 SKiDL (Python)

SKiDL is the most widely adopted open-source code-first schematic/netlist layer for KiCad automation. It serves as the backend representation for multiple LLM-driven systems: PCBSchemaGen generates executable SKiDL code from natural-language specifications and verifies it against a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2), while SchGen and HWE-Bench also reference SKiDL workflows (luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 8-9, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3). SKiDL generates netlists that can produce KiCad schematics, supports real parts and footprint assignment, and is fully scriptable/CI-ready. It does not, however, provide board-outline definition or automatic placement.

### 3.2 BHDL (Racket eDSL)

BHDL is a declarative, Racket-embedded DSL that uniquely combines schematic capture with automated placement. It models every circuit element as a function, supports hierarchical composition via wire/layout clauses, and includes an auto-placement engine consisting of a global analytical placer (based on RePlace, minimizing HPWL) and a simulated-annealing detailed placer (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6, li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5). BHDL exports to KiCad PCB format (exportable to Gerber), produces a BOM, and supports KiCad-format footprint libraries by default (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). The auto-placer is implemented in Julia for GPU acceleration (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). This makes BHDL the strongest research demonstration of a code→KiCad PCB + auto-placed layout pipeline, though it remains a research prototype rather than a production tool.

### 3.3 Polymorphic Blocks / EDG

Polymorphic Blocks extends hierarchical block diagrams with type polymorphism and electronics modeling. Users write Python HDL code that builds a hierarchical tree model; a compiler performs tree-walk lowering, applies refinements, invokes generators, and updates a constraint graph (ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 52-56, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 50-52). The system can export netlists with footprint annotations for import into KiCad (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45). It does not directly generate board outlines or perform automatic placement, leaving those steps downstream (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 2-2).

### 3.4 atopile

atopile is a fast-moving open-source hardware description language strongly oriented to Git/version-control and CI workflows. It generates netlists, schematic/PCB project artifacts, and BOMs via libraries, with KiCad as its primary target. Board outline and floorplan support exists but remains limited compared to schematic/netlist generation. atopile is one of the most promising tools for headless starter-board generation in a coding-agent workflow.

### 3.5 tscircuit (TypeScript/JavaScript)

tscircuit provides software-engineer-friendly programmatic circuit and PCB descriptions in TypeScript. It can emit schematic/PCB data structures, footprints, and web-native rendered artifacts. KiCad interoperability is improving but not yet canonical. It is highly scriptable and CI-friendly but still earlier-stage for production mixed-signal boards.

### 3.6 KiCad Python/IPC API + kicad-cli

KiCad's native Python API and CLI (kicad-cli) provide the production-grade backend for headless automation: DRC, ERC, plotting, export, library queries, and PCB manipulation. They do not constitute a high-level design language but are the essential execution layer for any code-first pipeline targeting KiCad output.

*Table: This table summarizes recent papers and benchmarks spanning PCB placement, IC/analog floorplanning, and LLM-based schematic generation. It is useful for seeing which methods actually produce placement or starter-board artifacts, what inputs they require, and how strong their reported results are.*

### 4.1 RL/ML-Based Placement and Floorplanning

**HiePlace** (Li et al., 2025, IEEE TCAD) is the current state of the art in PCB-specific placement. It uses hierarchical mathematical programming with dynamic programming for first-priority components and a relaxation algorithm to reduce Boolean variables. On 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic), it achieves an average 66% cost reduction and 7.7× speedup versus baselines. Versus Quilter specifically, cost reductions of 76.7%–91.3% are reported with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 11-12, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 3-4, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 1-2).

**Vassallo & Bajada** (DATE 2024) demonstrate end-to-end RL for PCB placement using TD3 and SAC agents, achieving 17% and 21% average post-routing wirelength reductions versus simulated annealing on unseen circuits, with convergence sometimes exceeding an order of magnitude faster (vassallo2024learningcircuitplacement pages 1-2).

**Vashisht et al.** (2020) combine cyclic RL with simulated annealing using sequence-pair representations, showing that RL-initialized SA consistently outperforms random initialization on ami49 and lattice benchmarks (vashisht2020placementinintegrated pages 5-7).

**Wang et al.** (2024) apply DRL with graph neural networks to chip floorplanning on ISPD 2005 benchmarks, achieving a 31.4% average HPWL reduction and 34.2% timing-violation reduction versus baselines including DREAMPlace (wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 7-12, wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 1-4, wang2024chipfloorplanningoptimization pages 12-15).

**Li et al.** (2025, PA-ICM) address double-layer PCB module placement with mixed-variable optimization, achieving automatic placement in minutes on industrial cases (li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 1-2, li2502adoublelayerplacement pages 7-8).

**Basso** (2026) combines R-GCN encoders with masked PPO for analog circuit floorplanning, reporting 67.3% layout-time reduction and 8.3% mean area reduction versus manual design on six industrial circuits (basso2026automatingthelayout pages 76-79, basso2026automatingthelayout pages 71-76).

### 4.2 LLM-Driven Schematic and Netlist Generation

**pcbGPT** (King et al., 2026) is a tool-augmented LLM synthesis agent that converts natural-language requirements into editable KiCad project drafts. It uses a custom Python circuit DSL, grounds generation in local KiCad libraries and datasheet-derived rules, and employs a generate–execute–repair validation loop. The best model achieves pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00 on its benchmark. Critically, the authors explicitly state it "does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation" (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 4-6, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 8-10, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 1-2, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24).

**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al., 2025) is a training-free framework that uses LLMs to propose SKiDL code, verified by a datasheet-driven knowledge graph and subgraph-isomorphism checker. It compresses datasheet context from ~16k to ~300 tokens per component and covers 23 benchmark tasks across digital, analog, and power domains, claiming significant accuracy improvements (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2).

**SchGen** (Luo et al., 2025) fine-tunes a 20B-parameter LLM using a semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) to generate editable KiCad schematics. It achieves 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert functional correctness, outperforming raw KiCad baselines (32% valid) and matching larger frontier models despite its smaller size (luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 8-9, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 1-3, luo2605schgenpcbschematic pages 3-4).

### 4.3 Benchmarks Revealing the Gap

**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al., 2025) is the most rigorous execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design. Using 300 tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets, it applies static rule checking plus LTspice dynamic simulation. The best-performing model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves only 8.15% end-to-end correct-design rate, with static pass rates around 71.84% and dynamic simulation pass rates around 58.65% (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2).

**PCB-Bench** (Li et al., 2026) provides a multimodal benchmark with ~3,700 text questions, ~500 image+text questions, and 170+ real PCB designs. Results show current LLMs struggle with spatial placement reasoning, rule adherence, and engineering artifacts, with no single model dominating all dimensions (li2026pcbbenchbenchmarkingllms pages 2-4, li2026pcbbenchbenchmarkingllms pages 8-11, li2026pcbbenchbenchmarkingllms pages 1-2).

**AMSnet-KG** (Shi et al., 2025) addresses the analog/mixed-signal domain with a knowledge-graph dataset of 894 circuits enabling LLM-based RAG for automated topology selection and Bayesian sizing. Case studies on op-amps and comparators demonstrate end-to-end design from specifications to sized netlists, with local annotations materially improving optimization convergence (shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 1-2, shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 9-11, shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 17-19).

---

## 5. Gap Assessment: Mixed-Signal Motor + Sensor Control Board

For a concrete low-cost, open-hardware board with an MCU driving stepper drivers, solenoids, servos, vibration motors, and an HX711/load-cell front end:

### 5.1 What Is Automatable Today

**Schematic capture** of well-documented subcircuits (MCU breakout, standard voltage regulators, connectors) can be partially automated via SKiDL scripts, pcbGPT, or PCBSchemaGen. pcbGPT achieves 0.90 pass@1 on schematic drafts, making it the strongest NL→KiCad schematic generator (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 1-2, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24). PCBSchemaGen's KG-verified SKiDL pipeline adds datasheet-grounded constraint checking (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3).

**Component selection** for standard parts (resistors, capacitors, common ICs) can be partially grounded via Cofactr/Diadem supply-chain intelligence or PCBSchemaGen's knowledge graph. Krishnamoorthy (2025) recommends mapping high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs and using iterative LLM prompting with temperature control to reduce generation errors (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2).

**Netlist and BOM generation** from code is mature via SKiDL or atopile pipelines feeding KiCad's CLI.

### 5.2 What Still Requires a Human

**Power-tree design and protection circuits** for motor drivers (high-current switching, flyback diodes, snubber networks for solenoids) require domain expertise that current LLMs handle poorly—HWE-Bench's 8.15% end-to-end correctness rate underscores this (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2).

**Analog front-end design** (HX711 + load cell, precision reference, EMI filtering) is particularly challenging. AMSnet-KG demonstrates that even for simpler analog circuits like op-amps, LLM-generated topologies sometimes fail to meet target specifications and require topology regeneration (shi2025amsnetkganetlist pages 13-15).

**Board outline and mechanical integration** (connector placement, mounting holes, enclosure constraints) are not automated by any current tool. This is a fundamental gap: no system generates a board outline from functional requirements.

**Floorplanning and placement regions** (separating power-switching sections from sensitive analog sections, keeping stepper-driver heat sinks away from the HX711, managing ground-plane partitioning) require mixed-signal domain knowledge that none of the current AI systems reliably encode.

**Design-rule specification** (impedance-controlled traces for motor PWM, guard rings for the load-cell front end, current-carrying trace widths) must be manually defined.

### 5.3 Headless/CI-Compatible Tool Chain

For a version-controlled, coding-agent workflow with no interactive GUI:

- **Best current stack**: atopile or SKiDL (source language) → KiCad Python API + kicad-cli (execution/export) → manual or scripted board outline + design rules → Quilter or FreeRouting (downstream placement/routing).
- **Most promising research addition**: PCBSchemaGen (open-source, LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with verification) or pcbGPT (NL→KiCad project drafts) as a schematic-generation front end.
- **BHDL** is the only system demonstrated to produce auto-placed KiCad PCBs from code, but it remains a research prototype (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6).
- **JITX** is the most mature commercial option for programmatic generation but is proprietary and not KiCad-native.

### 5.4 Realistic Assessment

For the described mixed-signal motor/sensor board, approximately 40–60% of the schematic capture could be accelerated by AI tools (standard subcircuits, MCU pin assignments, decoupling). The remaining 40–60% (power tree, analog front end, protection circuits, mixed-signal partitioning) requires human engineering review. Board outline, floorplan, and design rules remain essentially 100% manual. The complete "topology+BOM → KiCad starter board ready for Quilter" pipeline does not exist as a turnkey automated solution in 2025.

---

## 6. Conclusions and Recommendations

**Most credible gap-fillers for feeding Quilter/DeepPCB:**

1. **PCBSchemaGen** (open-source, training-free LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with KG+SI verification) is the most credible research system for producing verified KiCad schematics from natural-language specifications, with explicit grounding in real IC datasheets and pin constraints (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2).

2. **pcbGPT** achieves the highest reported schematic-synthesis accuracy (pass@1 = 0.90) and outputs native KiCad project files, but does not address placement/routing and remains a research prototype (king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 1-2, king2606pcbgptautomaticpcb pages 22-24).

3. **SKiDL + KiCad CLI** is the most mature, scriptable, CI-ready stack for code-first KiCad project generation, though it requires the human (or an upstream AI) to author the circuit description (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3).

4. **atopile** is the most promising emerging open-source HDL for version-controlled, Git-native PCB generation with KiCad as primary target.

5. **BHDL** uniquely demonstrates auto-placement integrated with code-first KiCad PCB generation, making it the closest existing system to a complete code→placed-board pipeline (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6).

6. **HiePlace** represents the state of the art in PCB-specific placement optimization and could serve as the placement stage between schematic generation and routing, though it is not publicly available as a packaged tool (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 3-4).

The fundamental bottleneck remains that no single system reliably converts a bare topology or block diagram into a complete, footprinted, outlined, design-rule-annotated KiCad project suitable for autonomous routing. The schematic/netlist generation layer is rapidly improving via LLM-based approaches, but board outline specification, mixed-signal floorplanning, and domain-specific design-rule encoding remain human-dependent. For a practical CI pipeline today, the recommended approach is to use a code-first framework (atopile or SKiDL) for the schematic/netlist layer, manually define the board outline and placement constraints, and then feed the resulting KiCad project to an autonomous router.

# Bridging the Topology+BOM → Starter Board Gap for Autonomous PCB Routers: A 2025 Survey

## 1. Introduction: The Intermediate Step Problem

Autonomous placement and routing engines such as Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep require, as their minimum input, a complete PCB project containing a captured schematic with a netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical footprints, a board outline, and design rules. They cannot start from a bare circuit topology or block diagram. This survey examines the tools, services, and research methods that attempt to bridge this "topology+BOM → starter board" gap as of 2025, covering commercial platforms (Section 2), design-as-code frameworks (Section 3), recent research on automated placement and LLM-driven schematic generation (Section 4), and an honest gap assessment for a representative mixed-signal board (Section 5).

---

## 2. Commercial and AI-Native Platforms

The following table summarizes the major commercial and emerging research platforms evaluated against the specific intermediate-step requirements:

| Tool/Platform | Input (what it accepts) | Output (schematic/netlist/footprints/BOM/outline/floorplan) | ECAD Export | Scriptability/API | Pricing/Licensing | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CELUS Design Platform / CUBOs / Design Assistant | High-level functional requirements, block-diagram-style intent, reference-design/library-driven selections; strongest when mapping requirements to known solution blocks rather than arbitrary bare topologies. Academic references describe automated reference-design processing and schematic generation around CELUS libraries. (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) | Claimed/marketed: generated schematic drafts, component selection/BOM, design documentation; practical floorplanning/outline generation appears limited and typically downstream in partner ECAD. Best viewed as requirements/block intent -> schematic/BOM accelerator, not a full autonomous starter-board generator. (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) | Altium-centric in practice; no strong public evidence of native KiCad export in the cited research context. | Primarily SaaS GUI/workspace; public headless API/CLI not prominent in available evidence. | Commercial enterprise SaaS; pricing not generally public. | Commercial and deployed, but evidence suggests strongest maturity in requirements-to-reference-design synthesis rather than headless topology->KiCad starter-board export. (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058) |
| Flux.ai | Usually starts from human-authored schematic/PCB or copilot-assisted editing in a browser ECAD; accepts natural-language assistance, component/library queries, and collaborative schematic edits more than pure block diagrams. | Browser-native schematic + PCB editing, shared libraries/BOM workflows; can help assign footprints and prepare PCB projects, but public evidence for fully automatic block-diagram -> complete starter board is limited. | Flux native; interoperability with standard ECAD formats exists, but KiCad-native round-trip/headless export is not its core story. | Web product; some integrations, but not a broadly documented public CLI-first workflow. | Commercial SaaS with team/business tiers; pricing changes over time. | Mature collaborative ECAD with AI copilot features, but not the most credible headless bridge for topology+BOM -> KiCad starter board. |

Related academic systems describe high-level HDL/generator flows that elaborate to concrete circuits and footprints. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) |
| Circuit Mind | Natural-language / requirement-driven electronics design assistant focused on suggesting architectures and schematics; best for early concept exploration. | Schematic concepts, component suggestions, documentation; public evidence for complete footprinted ECAD projects with board outline/floorplan is limited. | Limited public evidence of native KiCad starter-project export. | Mostly GUI/web workflow; public CLI/API not clearly established. | Commercial; pricing typically by demo/contact. | Emerging commercial AI assistant; useful upstream, but not clearly proven as a full topology+BOM -> footprinted starter-board bridge. |
| Cofactr / Diadem | Supply-chain/BOM/procurement-centric inputs: AML/AVL, sourcing constraints, approved parts, lifecycle, inventory; not primarily a topology-to-schematic authoring tool. | Strong on vetted manufacturer parts, BOM enrichment, sourcing, compliance, lifecycle/risk data; does not itself appear to generate a complete captured schematic + floorplan. | Integrates with enterprise PLM/EDA data rather than acting as a KiCad project generator. | API/integration oriented on supply-chain data more than ECAD synthesis. | Commercial enterprise software. | Mature for BOM intelligence and part selection; useful as a component-grounding layer, not a complete starter-board synthesizer. |
| Quilter | Input is already a PCB project with netlist, footprints, board outline, constraints, and usually pre-placement context; cited PCB-placement literature uses Quilter as a placement/routing baseline, not as a block-diagram-to-schematic tool. HiePlace compares against Quilter on academic benchmarks. (pqac-00000024) | Autonomous placement/routing assistance on an existing board design; does **not** fill the topology+BOM -> starter-board gap itself. (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000059) |
| PCBSchemaGen | Natural-language specifications plus datasheet-derived knowledge graph constraints; generates executable SKiDL code with verifier feedback. Covers digital/analog/power tasks. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047) | Verified SKiDL code, KiCad schematics, and layout-ready KiCad artifacts; emphasizes structural correctness via KG + subgraph-isomorphism verification. Public evidence is stronger for schematic/netlist correctness than for automatic board outline/floorplan. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000045) | SKiDL -> KiCad workflow; explicitly aims at KiCad-ready files. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009) | Research pipeline; open-sourced according to the paper, but not yet a polished commercial API product. (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000047) | Research/open prototype. | Very promising as a topology/requirements -> verified KiCad schematic bridge; less mature on physical floorplanning and production workflow hardening. |
| SchGen | Natural-language user requests; finetuned 20B LLM generates semantic-grounded schematic-editing code rather than raw KiCad text. (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000048, pqac-00000063) | Editable schematic code that executes to valid KiCad schematic files/netlists; benchmarked at 82% valid circuits and 60.5% expert functional correctness. Focus is schematic generation, not board-outline/floorplan automation. (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046) | KiCad target format via generated executable editing code. (pqac-00000043, pqac-00000048) | Research model/code pipeline; not a commercial headless PCB compiler product. | Research/open prototype. | Strong research result on NL -> editable KiCad schematics; not yet a complete starter-board generator for autonomous routers. |
| Bottom line for this gap | Bare topology/block diagram alone is still rarely enough for robust automation. The most credible current bridges are: **commercial** code/reference-design systems like JITX/CELUS for constrained domains, and **research** systems like pcbGPT/PCBSchemaGen/SchGen for KiCad schematic generation. Quilter/DeepPCB remain downstream consumers, not gap-fillers. (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000057, pqac-00000059) | A usable “starter board” for autonomous routers still usually needs human decisions on exact parts, footprints, power-tree safety, board outline, placement regions, keepouts, analog partitioning, and connector/mechanical details. HWE-Bench’s best full-correct rate of only 8.15% underscores the current gap. (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037) | KiCad-centered research is improving rapidly; production-grade end-to-end automation is not yet solved. | Headless/scriptable today: strongest in code-first/open flows, weaker in commercial GUI-first platforms. | Mixed commercial + research landscape. | As of 2025, the topology+BOM -> KiCad-starter-board gap is **partially fillable**, not fully solved. (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000059) |


*Table: This table compares commercial platforms and recent research systems on the specific intermediate PCB-design gap: turning high-level requirements or topology into a schematic/netlist/BOM/footprinted starter board. It highlights which tools are real candidates for feeding downstream autonomous placement/routing engines and where the major limitations still are.*

### 2.1 CELUS Design Platform (CUBOs, Design Assistant)

CELUS operates primarily as a requirements-to-reference-design accelerator, mapping high-level functional requirements to pre-validated solution blocks ("CUBOs") drawn from its component library. Generative AI is applied to reference-design processing for embedded systems, where the platform classifies schematic elements and assembles designs from vetted building blocks (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058). The approach leverages iterative LLM prompting and maps high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs using modular reuse (pqac-00000057). CELUS is strongest in the Altium ecosystem and does not prominently feature headless KiCad export or a public CLI. Pricing is enterprise SaaS. For the topology→starter-board gap, CELUS is most useful when the design maps to its known reference designs; it is weaker for novel or highly custom topologies.

### 2.2 Flux.ai

Flux.ai provides a browser-native ECAD environment with AI copilot features for schematic and PCB editing. It is best characterized as a collaborative design tool with AI assistance rather than a topology-to-board compiler. It does not prominently offer headless/CLI access or native KiCad round-trip export, making it less suitable for a version-controlled CI workflow.

### 2.3 JITX

JITX is among the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, using a Stanza-based hardware description language where generators can elaborate high-level parametric specifications into concrete circuits with selected parts and footprints. Related academic work on Polymorphic Blocks demonstrates how HDL-based systems can support block-level polymorphism (abstract-typed blocks refined into concrete implementations), electronics-model constraint propagation, and netlist export to layout tools (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000019). JITX is strongly scriptable and code-first, making it one of the most credible options for automated starter-board generation. However, it is commercial/proprietary, and KiCad is not its primary export target.

### 2.4 Circuit Mind and Cofactr/Diadem

Circuit Mind provides AI-assisted architecture suggestions and schematic concepts but lacks strong public evidence for complete KiCad starter-project generation. Cofactr/Diadem is supply-chain and BOM-intelligence focused—valuable for grounding component selection in availability and lifecycle data but not itself a schematic-capture or floorplanning tool.

### 2.5 Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep: Input Requirements

HiePlace benchmarks explicitly compare against Quilter on academic PCB placement tasks, demonstrating that Quilter operates as a downstream placement/routing engine that consumes an already-prepared board design with components, footprints, nets, and board outline (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025). Quilter achieved placement costs ranging from 19.46×10³ to 35.70×10³ on four academic benchmarks, with runtimes of 34–75 minutes, while HiePlace reduced costs by 76.7%–91.3% with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (pqac-00000024). DeepPCB/InstaDeep similarly requires board-level physical-design inputs. Neither tool fills the topology→starter-board gap; they are explicitly consumers of the output that this gap analysis seeks to produce.

---

## 3. Design-as-Code and Programmatic Frameworks

| Tool/Framework | Language | Input Description | Output (netlist/schematic/footprint/BOM/board outline/floorplan) | KiCad Export? | Auto-Placement/Floorplan? | Scriptable/CI-Ready? | License | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| atopile | atopile DSL + Python ecosystem | Textual hardware-description source capturing modules, constraints, interfaces, and component intent at higher abstraction than hand-drawn schematics; generally code-first rather than free-form block-diagram-first | Generates netlists, schematic/PCB project artifacts, footprints via libraries, BOMs; board outline/floorplan support exists but is limited compared with schematic/netlist generation and often still needs explicit designer intent | Yes, KiCad-centered in practice | Partial: can encode placement intent/constraints, but not a mature autonomous floorplanner; outline/floorplan usually still semi-manual | Yes; strongly oriented to Git/version control and automation workflows | Open source | Fast-moving open-source project; promising for headless starter-board generation, but less mature than KiCad itself |
| tscircuit | TypeScript/JavaScript | Programmatic circuit and PCB descriptions in TS/JS; strongest for software-engineer-friendly code-first designs and reusable generators/components | Can emit schematic/PCB data structures, footprints, BOM-style outputs, and web-native rendered PCB artifacts; KiCad interoperability is improving but not yet the canonical native target | Partial / indirect | Limited native auto-floorplanning; mainly parametric geometry and explicit placement authored in code | Yes; highly scriptable and CI-friendly | Open source | Emerging and developer-friendly, but still earlier-stage for production mixed-signal starter boards |
| SKiDL | Python | Python code describing parts, nets, buses, hierarchy, and parameters; ideal as an intermediate representation from higher-level generators or LLMs | Netlists directly; via downstream tools can generate/update KiCad schematics; supports real parts and footprints if assigned; BOM possible through Python processing; board outline/floorplan not native | Yes, through KiCad-oriented netlisting/workflows; heavily used in KiCad-centric research pipelines (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047) | No native floorplanning; leaves board outline and placement mostly manual/downstream | Yes; excellent for headless generation in CI | Open source | Mature and widely used as the most practical code-first schematic/netlist layer for KiCad automation |
| BHDL | Racket (embedded DSL) + Julia placer backend | Declarative PCB code with pin/part/wire/layout clauses; supports hierarchical composition, loops, parameterization, and layout combinators | Produces netlists, schematic representations, mapped footprints, BOM, KiCad PCB export, and auto-placed layouts using analytical + SA placers; can route via FreeRouting downstream (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005) | Yes, explicit KiCad PCB export (pqac-00000002) | Yes, unusually strong among research HDLs: includes relative layout combinators plus auto-placement (RePlace-style global placer + detailed SA) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) | Yes, code-first and notebook/REPL friendly; more research-prototype than turnkey CI product | Open source (pqac-00000000) | Research prototype but one of the strongest demonstrations of code->KiCad PCB + placement |
| Polymorphic Blocks / EDG | Python-embedded HDL | High-level block diagrams / abstract typed blocks refined into concrete implementations with propagated electrical constraints and generator libraries | Generates refined hierarchical circuit models, netlists, footprint annotations, and exports netlists for import into KiCad/layout tools; board layout and floorplan are mostly downstream/manual (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Yes, via netlist export/import workflow rather than native .kicad_pcb emission (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Limited: can reach layout-ready circuits conceptually, but not a full automatic board floorplanner in cited evidence (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000018) | Partly: underlying HDL/compiler is scriptable; user-facing refinement has GUI elements (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000019) | Research / open academic tooling | Important conceptual predecessor for high-level circuit synthesis; not a polished production KiCad compiler |
| KiCad Python / IPC API + kicad-cli | Python + CLI | Existing netlists/projects/scripts, custom generators, library queries, PCB edits, DRC/plot/export jobs; best used as a backend rather than a high-level language itself | Native KiCad projects, schematics/PCB edits, footprint assignment, plotting, ERC/DRC, BOM/plugin-driven outputs; board outlines and placement can be scripted but require user-authored logic | Native yes | Only what you script yourself; no built-in intelligent floorplanner | Yes; best native headless path for CI around KiCad | Open source | Production-grade backend automation layer, but not itself a high-level topology compiler |
| PCBmodE | Python + SVG | Geometry/code-driven PCB artwork and board-shape descriptions, usually emphasizing board form and traces over traditional schematic-first capture | Board outlines, copper geometry, stylized PCB layouts; not a full schematic/netlist/BOM/footprint capture system | No native full KiCad-project generation; more fabrication-geometry oriented | Can automate geometry/artistic layout, but not electrical floorplanning from topology | Yes | Open source | Niche/specialized; useful for geometry-first boards, not for general starter-board generation |
| Horizon EDA | Native application with code-friendly data model | Traditional schematic/PCB entry with scriptable/project-file friendliness; higher-level design capture is weaker than dedicated HDLs | Schematic, PCB, footprints, libraries, manufacturing outputs; can handle real parts and footprints natively; board outline manual; no known autonomous floorplanning focus | Not primarily KiCad-native; interoperability exists through standard EDA exchange rather than first-class KiCad export | Minimal built-in auto-floorplanning | Partial: data model is automation-friendly, but headless CI support is not as strong as SKiDL/KiCad CLI | Open source | Serious open-source EDA suite, but not a topology->starter-board compiler |
| Practical takeaway | Mixed | For fully headless, version-controlled generation of a footprinted KiCad starter board, the strongest stack today is usually **SKiDL or atopile as the source language**, plus **KiCad CLI/Python** as the execution/export backend; **BHDL** is the strongest research example of adding automated placement to that stack (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009) | Netlist/schematic/BOM generation is much more automated than board outline/floorplan generation; the latter usually still needs explicit constraints or human intent (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Yes, with the right stack | Partial overall | Yes, especially SKiDL/atopile + KiCad CLI | Mixed | As of 2025, code-first generation of a KiCad-ingestible starter board is feasible, but reliable automatic floorplanning remains the weak link |


*Table: This table compares the main design-as-code and scriptable PCB frameworks relevant to generating footprinted, netlisted KiCad starter projects from higher-level descriptions. It highlights where automation is already strong—schematic/netlist/BOM generation—and where it remains weak, especially board outline and floorplanning.*

### 3.1 SKiDL (Python)

SKiDL is the most widely adopted open-source code-first schematic/netlist layer for KiCad automation. It serves as the backend representation for multiple LLM-driven systems: PCBSchemaGen generates executable SKiDL code from natural-language specifications and verifies it against a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010), while SchGen and HWE-Bench also reference SKiDL workflows (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000044). SKiDL generates netlists that can produce KiCad schematics, supports real parts and footprint assignment, and is fully scriptable/CI-ready. It does not, however, provide board-outline definition or automatic placement.

### 3.2 BHDL (Racket eDSL)

BHDL is a declarative, Racket-embedded DSL that uniquely combines schematic capture with automated placement. It models every circuit element as a function, supports hierarchical composition via wire/layout clauses, and includes an auto-placement engine consisting of a global analytical placer (based on RePlace, minimizing HPWL) and a simulated-annealing detailed placer (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000004). BHDL exports to KiCad PCB format (exportable to Gerber), produces a BOM, and supports KiCad-format footprint libraries by default (pqac-00000002). The auto-placer is implemented in Julia for GPU acceleration (pqac-00000002). This makes BHDL the strongest research demonstration of a code→KiCad PCB + auto-placed layout pipeline, though it remains a research prototype rather than a production tool.

### 3.3 Polymorphic Blocks / EDG

Polymorphic Blocks extends hierarchical block diagrams with type polymorphism and electronics modeling. Users write Python HDL code that builds a hierarchical tree model; a compiler performs tree-walk lowering, applies refinements, invokes generators, and updates a constraint graph (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000007). The system can export netlists with footprint annotations for import into KiCad (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019). It does not directly generate board outlines or perform automatic placement, leaving those steps downstream (pqac-00000018).

### 3.4 atopile

atopile is a fast-moving open-source hardware description language strongly oriented to Git/version-control and CI workflows. It generates netlists, schematic/PCB project artifacts, and BOMs via libraries, with KiCad as its primary target. Board outline and floorplan support exists but remains limited compared to schematic/netlist generation. atopile is one of the most promising tools for headless starter-board generation in a coding-agent workflow.

### 3.5 tscircuit (TypeScript/JavaScript)

tscircuit provides software-engineer-friendly programmatic circuit and PCB descriptions in TypeScript. It can emit schematic/PCB data structures, footprints, and web-native rendered artifacts. KiCad interoperability is improving but not yet canonical. It is highly scriptable and CI-friendly but still earlier-stage for production mixed-signal boards.

### 3.6 KiCad Python/IPC API + kicad-cli

KiCad's native Python API and CLI (kicad-cli) provide the production-grade backend for headless automation: DRC, ERC, plotting, export, library queries, and PCB manipulation. They do not constitute a high-level design language but are the essential execution layer for any code-first pipeline targeting KiCad output.

---

## 4. Recent Research on Automated Placement, Floorplanning, and LLM-Driven Schematic Generation (2020–2025)

| Paper (authors, year) | Approach | Domain | Input | Output | Key Results / Metrics | Benchmark |

*Table: This table summarizes recent papers and benchmarks spanning PCB placement, IC/analog floorplanning, and LLM-based schematic generation. It is useful for seeing which methods actually produce placement or starter-board artifacts, what inputs they require, and how strong their reported results are.*

### 4.1 RL/ML-Based Placement and Floorplanning

**HiePlace** (Li et al., 2025, IEEE TCAD) is the current state of the art in PCB-specific placement. It uses hierarchical mathematical programming with dynamic programming for first-priority components and a relaxation algorithm to reduce Boolean variables. On 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic), it achieves an average 66% cost reduction and 7.7× speedup versus baselines. Versus Quilter specifically, cost reductions of 76.7%–91.3% are reported with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000078).

**Vassallo & Bajada** (DATE 2024) demonstrate end-to-end RL for PCB placement using TD3 and SAC agents, achieving 17% and 21% average post-routing wirelength reductions versus simulated annealing on unseen circuits, with convergence sometimes exceeding an order of magnitude faster (pqac-00000020).

**Vashisht et al.** (2020) combine cyclic RL with simulated annealing using sequence-pair representations, showing that RL-initialized SA consistently outperforms random initialization on ami49 and lattice benchmarks (pqac-00000022).

**Wang et al.** (2024) apply DRL with graph neural networks to chip floorplanning on ISPD 2005 benchmarks, achieving a 31.4% average HPWL reduction and 34.2% timing-violation reduction versus baselines including DREAMPlace (pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051, pqac-00000052).

**Li et al.** (2025, PA-ICM) address double-layer PCB module placement with mixed-variable optimization, achieving automatic placement in minutes on industrial cases (pqac-00000072, pqac-00000076).

**Basso** (2026) combines R-GCN encoders with masked PPO for analog circuit floorplanning, reporting 67.3% layout-time reduction and 8.3% mean area reduction versus manual design on six industrial circuits (pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054).

### 4.2 LLM-Driven Schematic and Netlist Generation

**pcbGPT** (King et al., 2026) is a tool-augmented LLM synthesis agent that converts natural-language requirements into editable KiCad project drafts. It uses a custom Python circuit DSL, grounds generation in local KiCad libraries and datasheet-derived rules, and employs a generate–execute–repair validation loop. The best model achieves pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00 on its benchmark. Critically, the authors explicitly state it "does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation" (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059).

**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al., 2025) is a training-free framework that uses LLMs to propose SKiDL code, verified by a datasheet-driven knowledge graph and subgraph-isomorphism checker. It compresses datasheet context from ~16k to ~300 tokens per component and covers 23 benchmark tasks across digital, analog, and power domains, claiming significant accuracy improvements (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000047).

**SchGen** (Luo et al., 2025) fine-tunes a 20B-parameter LLM using a semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) to generate editable KiCad schematics. It achieves 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert functional correctness, outperforming raw KiCad baselines (32% valid) and matching larger frontier models despite its smaller size (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000063).

### 4.3 Benchmarks Revealing the Gap

**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al., 2025) is the most rigorous execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design. Using 300 tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets, it applies static rule checking plus LTspice dynamic simulation. The best-performing model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves only 8.15% end-to-end correct-design rate, with static pass rates around 71.84% and dynamic simulation pass rates around 58.65% (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037).

**PCB-Bench** (Li et al., 2026) provides a multimodal benchmark with ~3,700 text questions, ~500 image+text questions, and 170+ real PCB designs. Results show current LLMs struggle with spatial placement reasoning, rule adherence, and engineering artifacts, with no single model dominating all dimensions (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031).

**AMSnet-KG** (Shi et al., 2025) addresses the analog/mixed-signal domain with a knowledge-graph dataset of 894 circuits enabling LLM-based RAG for automated topology selection and Bayesian sizing. Case studies on op-amps and comparators demonstrate end-to-end design from specifications to sized netlists, with local annotations materially improving optimization convergence (pqac-00000065, pqac-00000066, pqac-00000068).

---

## 5. Gap Assessment: Mixed-Signal Motor + Sensor Control Board

For a concrete low-cost, open-hardware board with an MCU driving stepper drivers, solenoids, servos, vibration motors, and an HX711/load-cell front end:

### 5.1 What Is Automatable Today

**Schematic capture** of well-documented subcircuits (MCU breakout, standard voltage regulators, connectors) can be partially automated via SKiDL scripts, pcbGPT, or PCBSchemaGen. pcbGPT achieves 0.90 pass@1 on schematic drafts, making it the strongest NL→KiCad schematic generator (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059). PCBSchemaGen's KG-verified SKiDL pipeline adds datasheet-grounded constraint checking (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009).

**Component selection** for standard parts (resistors, capacitors, common ICs) can be partially grounded via Cofactr/Diadem supply-chain intelligence or PCBSchemaGen's knowledge graph. Krishnamoorthy (2025) recommends mapping high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs and using iterative LLM prompting with temperature control to reduce generation errors (pqac-00000057, pqac-00000058).

**Netlist and BOM generation** from code is mature via SKiDL or atopile pipelines feeding KiCad's CLI.

### 5.2 What Still Requires a Human

**Power-tree design and protection circuits** for motor drivers (high-current switching, flyback diodes, snubber networks for solenoids) require domain expertise that current LLMs handle poorly—HWE-Bench's 8.15% end-to-end correctness rate underscores this (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000037).

**Analog front-end design** (HX711 + load cell, precision reference, EMI filtering) is particularly challenging. AMSnet-KG demonstrates that even for simpler analog circuits like op-amps, LLM-generated topologies sometimes fail to meet target specifications and require topology regeneration (pqac-00000069).

**Board outline and mechanical integration** (connector placement, mounting holes, enclosure constraints) are not automated by any current tool. This is a fundamental gap: no system generates a board outline from functional requirements.

**Floorplanning and placement regions** (separating power-switching sections from sensitive analog sections, keeping stepper-driver heat sinks away from the HX711, managing ground-plane partitioning) require mixed-signal domain knowledge that none of the current AI systems reliably encode.

**Design-rule specification** (impedance-controlled traces for motor PWM, guard rings for the load-cell front end, current-carrying trace widths) must be manually defined.

### 5.3 Headless/CI-Compatible Tool Chain

For a version-controlled, coding-agent workflow with no interactive GUI:

- **Best current stack**: atopile or SKiDL (source language) → KiCad Python API + kicad-cli (execution/export) → manual or scripted board outline + design rules → Quilter or FreeRouting (downstream placement/routing).
- **Most promising research addition**: PCBSchemaGen (open-source, LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with verification) or pcbGPT (NL→KiCad project drafts) as a schematic-generation front end.
- **BHDL** is the only system demonstrated to produce auto-placed KiCad PCBs from code, but it remains a research prototype (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).
- **JITX** is the most mature commercial option for programmatic generation but is proprietary and not KiCad-native.

### 5.4 Realistic Assessment

For the described mixed-signal motor/sensor board, approximately 40–60% of the schematic capture could be accelerated by AI tools (standard subcircuits, MCU pin assignments, decoupling). The remaining 40–60% (power tree, analog front end, protection circuits, mixed-signal partitioning) requires human engineering review. Board outline, floorplan, and design rules remain essentially 100% manual. The complete "topology+BOM → KiCad starter board ready for Quilter" pipeline does not exist as a turnkey automated solution in 2025.

---

## 6. Conclusions and Recommendations

**Most credible gap-fillers for feeding Quilter/DeepPCB:**

1. **PCBSchemaGen** (open-source, training-free LLM→SKiDL→KiCad with KG+SI verification) is the most credible research system for producing verified KiCad schematics from natural-language specifications, with explicit grounding in real IC datasheets and pin constraints (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000047).

2. **pcbGPT** achieves the highest reported schematic-synthesis accuracy (pass@1 = 0.90) and outputs native KiCad project files, but does not address placement/routing and remains a research prototype (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000059).

3. **SKiDL + KiCad CLI** is the most mature, scriptable, CI-ready stack for code-first KiCad project generation, though it requires the human (or an upstream AI) to author the circuit description (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000044).

4. **atopile** is the most promising emerging open-source HDL for version-controlled, Git-native PCB generation with KiCad as primary target.

5. **BHDL** uniquely demonstrates auto-placement integrated with code-first KiCad PCB generation, making it the closest existing system to a complete code→placed-board pipeline (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002).

6. **HiePlace** represents the state of the art in PCB-specific placement optimization and could serve as the placement stage between schematic generation and routing, though it is not publicly available as a packaged tool (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025).

The fundamental bottleneck remains that no single system reliably converts a bare topology or block diagram into a complete, footprinted, outlined, design-rule-annotated KiCad project suitable for autonomous routing. The schematic/netlist generation layer is rapidly improving via LLM-based approaches, but board outline specification, mixed-signal floorplanning, and domain-specific design-rule encoding remain human-dependent. For a practical CI pipeline today, the recommended approach is to use a code-first framework (atopile or SKiDL) for the schematic/netlist layer, manually define the board outline and placement constraints, and then feed the resulting KiCad project to an autonomous router.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes BHDL as an embedded circuit DSL that uses structural/compositional syntax ("series and parallel syntax") and supports "in-place part creation" (anonymous components, loops and conditionals) so users can programmatically generate repeated circuitry. BHDL provides formal reduction semantics for nets and composition (e.g., "The nets(xi.R, xj.L) means a netlist consisting of two pins" and net unions like "y.nets ⇝N1 ∪N2"), indicating it derives netlists from circuit clauses. Layout is expressed with hierarchical "layout combinators" such as hstack/vstack and spatial operators ("at deﬁnes the offset (x, y) between two parts.") and example layouts appear as nested forms like "(hstack (vstack btn1 btn2 btn3) esp01)". BHDL embeds in a host language ("The BHDL circuit is a ﬁrst-class value inside the host language"), allowing parameterized variants (examples show different behavior for "Debug" vs "Release"). Part/footprint management uses a "footprint" clause and "import" to bind package names and pin orders to BHDL parts. For placement, the system applies a simple padding heuristic ("padding parameter that enlarge components’ footprints") and a detailed placer using simulated annealing ("We also implement a simulated annealing (SA) detailed placer"), implying outputs include netlists, mapped footprints/pads, and auto-placed layouts derived from code.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a generator-style hardware description language (HDL) implemented as a Python-embedded domain-specific language that authors parameterized blocks (templates/classes) programmatically. It separates interface from implementation by translating high-level inputs into internal parameters: examples include an LED-resistor generator that computes a resistor value from input voltage and an RC filter block that computes R and C from cutoff frequency and impedance. The system includes an electronics model (ports, links, signal types) and a library of primitives and higher-level blocks derived from parts tables (E24 series, capacitors, inductors, etc.). Port parameters propagate through links to form aggregate net properties (e.g., total current, acceptable voltage range), and links can assert correctness constraints. Library blocks produced by the HDL provide design automation and can be dropped into system-level descriptions. From this, inputs to the HDL are high-level block parameters, class/port/link definitions, and design constraints; outputs are instantiated, parameterized block implementations and library components (with selected part values and propagated net parameters) that enable downstream artifacts such as schematic-level blocks and machine-readable component selections.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

contexts.context: From the excerpt, BHDL is a domain-specific, embedded programming language for describing PCB circuits and their physical layouts. Users write BHDL code that declares parts (including anonymous in-place parts), pins, and connections using compositional wire/connection primitives: net (list-of-pins producing a conventional netlist), series (connect parts in series by left/right pins), parallel (connect parts in parallel), and bus (connect corresponding pins across parts, with placeholders supported). These syntactic constructs have formal semantics that generate the connectivity information (i.e., netlists) and can be nested to express complex topologies concisely. For physical placement, BHDL supplies relative layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) and a hierarchical layout model: sub-circuits can be laid out and then composed into larger layouts using spatial relations like “left of” and “above.” The language therefore takes as input textual BHDL descriptions (parts, pins, connection expressions, and layout combinators) and produces structured circuit connectivity (netlist/schematic representations) and hierarchical, auto-placed layouts derived from the layout combinators. The excerpt does not list exact exported file formats or downstream EDA artifacts.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

The series syntax is used to specify parts connected in series
The parallel syntax is used to specify parts connected in parallel
The bus syntax is used to connect corresponding pins of two or more entities
The series syntax expects a list of parts, and the semantics is to connect the left and right pins of each component in a series

contexts.context: The paper describes a Python-embedded hardware description language (HDL) implemented as a library of base classes (Block, Port, GeneratorBlock, etc.) with mypy types. Users write Python HDL code which builds a hierarchical tree model of blocks and ports. The compiler performs a tree-walk lowering pass: it applies refinements (replacing abstract blocks with chosen concrete subclasses), invokes generators (which receive concrete parameter bounds/values and instantiate internal sub-blocks), and updates a constraint graph that records assignments and bounds. Generators must run once without ordering guarantees so they must accept worst-case parameter bounds. After the model is fully lowered, a netlisting phase walks the tree to produce a netlist. The system can export a netlist file (components + connectivity) for import into KiCad; it does not perform board layout or automatic placement/routing itself. It does, however, generate deterministic component names from HDL variable names to allow netlist updates to an in-progress layout. A visualization/refinement GUI shows an automatically laid-out block diagram, solved parameter values, and lets users select refinements interactively.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

snippets: The user-facing HDL is implemented as a library of base classes in Python, with mypy static type annotations allowing the user HDL to be type checked.
The user HDL code invokes hardware construction methods (like Block and Port) which builds up the hierarchy block model as a tree data structure.

The hardware compiler takes the “high-level” model, as in fig. 3.2a, and incrementally “lowers” the model by adding detail and expanding sub-elements until getting to the lowest form, as in fig. 3.2b.
Refinement:  if there is a refinement selected for the type or particular block, the block is replaced with the refinement.
Generation:  if the block is a generator, the generator is provided with the concrete values of any accessible parameters, then invoked to define the block’s internal elements.

Generators run once and not in any specific order, so all referenced parameters must have at least worst-case bounds, and the generator must be written to produce a working implementation for that entire range.
Constraints of the form “a == something” are recorded as assignments to a, and constraints of the form “a subset-of something” are recorded as bounds to a.
Netlisting is handled as a compiler phase after the design has been fully lowered

Our system can export this as a netlist file describing components and their connectivity, which can then be imported into KiCad’s [29] board layout tool. Otherwise, we currently do not address board layout.
we enable netlist updates to a work-in-progress layout by generating deterministic component names using HDL variable names.
we also provide a visualization and refinement GUI.
visualizes the HDL with an automatically laid out block diagram and provides insight into the system’s reasoning though inspection of solved values.

contexts.context: BHDL is a declarative, embedded domain-specific language for PCB design implemented on Racket with REPL-driven and Jupyter-based incremental development. It provides programmatic constructs for circuits, pins, and multiple footprints; an import clause lets users bring in footprints (including from Internet URLs) and rename them. Footprint management supports KiCAD-format libraries by default and allows specifying default and alternative packages (examples show selecting a 0603 footprint and multiple footprints for an ATtiny25). For physical layout, BHDL includes an auto-placement engine: a global analytical placer based on RePlace that minimizes HPWL and a simulated-annealing detailed placer (with an added rotation dimension) to legalize overlaps. The auto-placer is implemented in Julia for easier debugging and GPU acceleration. The system compiles/exports designs to KiCAD PCB format (exportable to Gerber) and produces a Bill of Materials. The workflow is incremental: circuit definitions are evaluated interactively rather than repeatedly compiling to KiCAD output, enabling visualization of rendered placements and iterative tuning.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes BHDL (Board HDL) as a declarative, simple, modular, and expressive embedded domain-specific language for PCB design implemented as an eDSL in Racket. BHDL models every circuit element (even a resistor) as a function to enable hierarchical, modular composition. It exposes a concise syntax for nets, series/parallel/bus connections, and leverages host-language features (loops, conditionals, libraries) for programmability. BHDL supports physical layout co-design directly in code via relative layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) and also integrates adapted VLSI auto-placers to auto-place free parts. The development model is interactive/REPL-style and integrated with Jupyter notebooks. The framework generates placed PCB boards from BHDL programs and can export boards for routing with the FreeRouting tool to produce final manufacturable PCBs. The project is open source (https://bhdl.org).
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?
