snippets: To facilitate a more effective PCB design workﬂow, we propose BHDL (standing for Board HDL), a declarative, simple, modular, and expressive HDL and system, in this paper.
BHDL is designed as an embedded Domain Speciﬁc Language (eDSL) inside the modern and highly-regarded programming language Racket [10]–[12].
Treating all circuits, even a resistor, as functions, BHDL naturally supports modularized development that builds a complex design up from smaller designs hierarchically.
As an embedded Domain Speciﬁc Language (eDSL), BHDL allows users to leverage the  full feature of the host web-based, REPL-style development and generates auto-placed PCBs.
BHDL provides a simple and intuitive syntax for expressing circuit connections naturally.
BHDL supports the co-design of the physical layout directly in the language.
BHDL provides simple and relative layout combinators namely hstack, vstack, rotate and at.
we also adapt state-of-the-art VLSI auto-placers [13], [14] to ﬁnd appropriate locations for free parts, with simple and effective improvements for PCBs.
The development model of BHDL is interactive and incremental, follows the Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL) [15] or interactive scripting model.
BHDL is shipped with the Jupyter [16] notebook environment
Our framework generates placed PCB board. The board can then be routed using the open-source FreeRouting [17] tool to produce the ﬁnal PCB for manufacturing.
Our framework is open source at https://bhdl.org

contexts.context: BHDL is an embedded, Lisp-family (Racket) programming language for PCB designs. Designs are written as circuit definitions composed of four clauses: pin (declares interface signals like I/O, power, ground), part (instantiates other circuits as functions and supports parametrization such as footprints and resistor values), wire (describes connectivity), and layout (describes placement/arrangement). Circuits are instantiated by calling the circuit name like a function and binding it to a variable. The wire clause provides net/series/parallel/bus constructs; the net construct explicitly declares a conventional netlist by listing pins to be connected. The layout clause includes primitives such as hstack, vstack, rotate, and at, enabling programmatic placement and arrangement of parts (i.e., auto-placed layouts). From BHDL source (circuit code plus parameters), the system can produce corresponding schematic representations, generate netlists (from net clauses), and drive layout/placement using the layout clause primitives. Inputs are BHDL code (circuit definitions, parameters/arguments, and part instances); outputs are schematics, netlists, and layouts/placements derived from that code.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

snippets: BHDL is based on Racket which roots back to LISP, BHDL uses nested pairs of brackets to control the structure.
A circuit definition consists of 4 clauses: pin, part, wire, layout.
The pin syntax defines the interface signals, such as I/Os, power, and ground, of a circuit so it can be hooked with other circuits to form a bigger design.
To instantiate a circuit instance, call the circuit function with or without arguments and parameters, and then bind it with a variable.
BHDL extends it by allowing parametrizations with keywords and default values for tasks unique to PCB design such as selecting footprints and set resistances
The wire syntax in BHDL supports four kinds of connection clauses: net, series, parallel, and bus
net: The very basic one, the net syntax, takes a list of pins to declare a conventional “netlist” connecting all the given pins together.
Part 4: layout clause
l ::= (hstack alopt l ...) (horizontal layout) | (vstack alopt l ...) (vertical layout) | (rotate l degree) (rotation) | (at l1 x y l2) (overlapping)

contexts.context: The excerpt describes an HCL-based design flow (Polymorphic Circuit Blocks) where designers write system-architecture-level descriptions (block diagrams) in a hardware description language. The HCL uses subcircuit generator libraries: blocks can be defined as executable generators that translate high-level specifications into concrete implementations (for example computing resistor values from input voltage). The language supports polymorphism and ambiguity (generic parts vs. specific part numbers) so designs can be refined progressively. HCL designs are elaborated into a hierarchical block-graph, expanded with community libraries, and refined interactively in a GUI; parameters are automatically propagated and checked for consistency. The primary output described is a netlist file that can be exported and imported into conventional board layout tools. The system is presented as an end-to-end authoring approach but the excerpt notes the GUI refinement and export-to-netlist step rather than fully automated placement/routing; actual auto-placed PCB layouts are not explicitly described in this passage.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

snippets: supports the system architecture level of abstraction, typically block diagrams of high-level components
enables generators that automatically customize the implementation of those libraries, for example sizing components by higher-level parameters
allows ambiguity in those block diagrams, for example with a generic resistor instead of a specific part number, at both the system architecture and library level

Defining blocks as generators – executable code that translates high-level specifications into an implementation, e.g.  a LED-resistor subcircuit that calculates resistance from input voltage – also helps separates interface from implementation and enables relative novices to leverage the knowledge of experts.

In the Polymorphic Circuit Blocks approach (purple box), board designers start by writing their system architecture in a hardware description language (HCL), which is then elaborated into a hierarchy block graph model and expanded using community libraries. That graph is then refined through interactive choices in a GUI and automatically propagated parameters are checked to ensure system correctness. The result can be exported to a netlist file, which can then be imported into a board design tool for layout.
We implement this new model of circuit design in Polymorphic Circuit Blocks, an end-to-end system for authoring block diagrams.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a hardware construction language (HCL) / hardware description layer used to programmatically define hierarchical, polymorphic circuit blocks for PCB/electronics design. The model uses a class hierarchy so designers can instantiate abstract or concrete blocks (superclasses and subclasses) and delay precise specification. Blocks expose ports with physical properties (e.g., voltage_out, current_limit, current_draw, voltage_limit) and links aggregate connected-port parameters; parameters "flow" through connections and links produce aggregate net properties and assertions to check compatibility. The system defines signal and analog port types and multi-wire protocol bundles. A library of primitives (resistors, capacitors, inductors, diodes, transistors) and higher-level blocks (pull-ups, decoupling caps, RC filters, resistive dividers) translate port parameters to component parameters and perform calculations (e.g., RC cutoff → R/C values, E24 selection for resistor pairs). Example HCL code shows block instantiation and connect operations. The generator HCL interface "translates high-level inputs into internal parameters," enabling automated part-parameter generation and design-checking; these intermediate parameters (component values, net voltages/currents, assertions) are the outputs of the language's synthesis step.
contexts.question: What is BHDL programming language for PCB design, and how does it generate schematics, netlists, auto-placed layouts from code? What are the inputs and outputs?

snippets: Class Hierarchy
Our class hierarchy, borrowing inheritance concepts from object-oriented programming, defines how parts are functionally similar and can be used in place of one another.
This allows using blocks that are abstract – generic and without implementation – and delaying the precise specification until later.
Ports are defined with their physical properties: voltage output and current limits for sources, and current draw and voltage limits for sinks.
These parameters "flow" through connected ports to links, which create aggregate parameters of connected ports such as the total current drawn and acceptable voltage range.
Links also define assertions to check correctness properties.
We also define signal types, including digital ports modeling high and low voltage thresholds and analog ports modeling input and output impedances.
we built a library of common blocks.

These primitives are defined with untyped passive ports, and are wrapped in higher-level library blocks (e.g., pull-up resistors for digital lines and decoupling capacitors for power lines) that translate port parameters to component parameters (e.g., pin voltage to rated voltage on a decoupling capacitor).

For example, a low-pass resistor-capacitor (RC) filter block calculates the resistance and capacitance based on a cutoff frequency and impedance specification, while a resistive divider block finds a pair of resistor values in the E24 series meeting the target ratio and output impedance.
Figure 5.8: Example code defining the Blinky circuit Block.
class Blinky(Block):
self.mcu = self.Block(Nucleo_F303k8())
self.led = self.Block(IndicatorLed())
self.connect(self.mcu.gnd, self.led.gnd)
self.connect(self.mcu.digital[0], self.led.io)
Hardware Description Language

we provide a generator HCL interface for authoring blocks. This programmatic construction of blocks captures the design methodology to construct a family of subcircuits, and separates interface from implementation by translating high-level inputs into internal parameters.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen as an LLM-based framework that translates natural-language PCB schematic specifications into SKiDL code. The authors choose SKiDL because Python-based hardware description languages provide high information density and align with LLM pretraining. The pipeline uses carefully designed prompts, a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) that encodes essential device rules and constraints (compressing typical ~16k-token datasheets down to ~300 tokens per component), and a multi-stage verification loop combining electrical design rules and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) topology checks. Pin semantics are abstracted via a labeling function mapping pins to one of 36 roles to decouple verification from vendor naming. Correctly generated SKiDL produces KiCAD schematic files and KiCAD can then generate PCB layout files ready for routing. The system provides verification feedback to the LLM for iterative refinement. The excerpt does not mention pcbGPT or describe its approach, so comparisons or pcbGPT-specific details are not present here.
contexts.question: What are PCBSchemaGen and pcbGPT approaches for LLM-based schematic generation from natural language? How do they use SKiDL and KiCad to produce netlists and schematics?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT’s approach to LLM-based schematic generation. pcbGPT uses a custom Python-embedded circuit DSL as an intermediate representation between natural-language-driven synthesis and KiCad export, modeling Circuit, Component, Net, and Pin objects. Python was chosen because LLMs handle Python well. Connectivity is expressed with operator overloading (the & operator merges nets and attaches pins), and example code shows construction via circuit = Circuit() and add_component/add_net calls. The team explicitly notes SKiDL exists but built their own AI-oriented DSL because SKiDL lacked features needed for synthesis-and-evaluation. The DSL enforces useful semantics for synthesis: pin error handling, normalized value formats, footprint validation, optional components, FlexiblePin mappings, and evaluation-oriented export capabilities. pcbGPT performs two-stage validation: deterministic execution and rule checking (including KiCad/ERC checks) for syntactic validity, plus probabilistic semantic validation (datasheet-derived checks, plausibility, correct component usage). When validations pass, designs are compiled into KiCad project drafts and export artifacts (JSON, graphs, netlists, KiCad projects). A web UI supports interactive sessions with generated schematics and iterative edits.
contexts.question: What are PCBSchemaGen and pcbGPT approaches for LLM-based schematic generation from natural language? How do they use SKiDL and KiCad to produce netlists and schematics?

snippets: To address this limitation, we developed a Python-embedded circuit DSL that serves as an intermediate representation between language-driven synthesis and KiCad export.
The DSL models an explicit object graph of Circuit, Component, Net, and Pin objects.
We chose Python as the host language because it is widely used and because large language models are comparatively strong at understanding and generating Python code.
Connectivity is expressed through operator overloading. Specifically, the bitwise-and operator & is used to merge nets and attach pins
circuit = Circuit()

While existing systems such as SKiDL [30] also allow PCB circuits to be expressed as Python code, they do not provide several features required for our synthesis-and-evaluation setting, which motivated the development of our own AI-oriented DSL:
When all checks pass, the pipeline compiles the resulting design into a KiCad project draft and associated export artifacts for downstream use.
Our DSL supports export to JSON, graphs, netlists, and KiCad projects for both circuit capture and automated benchmark evaluation.

Deterministic execution and rule checking establish syntactic validity, while probabilistic semantic validation assesses whether the generated design is plausible with respect to component usage, support circuitry, and overall design intent.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen as a training-free, LLM-driven pipeline that generates executable SKiDL code from natural-language specifications. The system uses domain-specific prompting (including Chain-of-Thought and In-context Learning) to have an LLM propose SKiDL designs, then applies an iterative feedback loop where a verifier returns interpretable messages to refine generated code. The verifier is multi-phase and KG & SI driven: PCBSchemaGen constructs a knowledge graph from real-world IC datasheets capturing pin-role semantics, multiple constraint types, and topological constraints; this anchors generation to physical IC package and pin rules. Generated SKiDL artifacts include Part declarations referencing KiCad footprints and values (example lines show use of 'from skidl import *', Part(..., footprint="KiCAD:TPS54302"), and an ERC() check). The paper also reports a 23-task benchmark across digital, analog, and power domains and claims end-to-end capability from natural language to KiCad PCB layout-ready files. contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT, an LLM-driven system for automatic schematic generation from natural-language requirements. pcbGPT focuses on early schematic-level design (not placement/routing) and outputs editable KiCad project drafts. Its pipeline has three main components: a synthesis agent, component tools that ground generation in the local KiCad environment (searching symbols in the KiCad library), and a validation stage. The system retrieves datasheet information via a custom extraction approach to inform component integration, and uses a Python-based circuit DSL to represent circuits for synthesis and evaluation. Validation executes the generated representation to run deterministic structural checks followed by semantic review; identified errors feed back to the synthesis agent in a generate–execute–repair loop. The system supports fully automatic generation and human-in-the-loop refinement through a web interface that lets users iteratively refine requirements, inspect intermediate representations, and synchronize with KiCad workflows. contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT, a system that converts conversational natural-language hardware requirements into editable KiCad-compatible schematic drafts. pcbGPT represents circuits using a Python DSL (examples shown: net_3v3 = Net("3v3"), imu = Component("BMI160"), net_3v3 & imu.pins[5]) and produces KiCad project artifacts (.kicad_sch, .kicad_pcb, .kicad_pro, "Generated KiCad Project"). The approach is tool-augmented: it combines component-library search, datasheet-grounded design knowledge, execution-based checking, deterministic structural and semantic validation, and an interactive web workflow for iterative refinement and synchronization with KiCad. The paper reports high generation accuracy on benchmark tasks (best model pass@1 0.90, pass@5 1.00) and frames outputs as reviewable first drafts rather than fully automated replacements for expert designers.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen as a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses a large language model (LLM) as a proposal generator to produce SKiDL Python-style code representing PCB schematics. PCBSchemaGen translates schematic generation into SKiDL code generation, then verifies correctness using a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (KG) and a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) based verifier that encodes pin-role semantics and topological constraints. The design is decoupled: an LLM proposes SKiDL code (with domain-specific prompts, Chain-of-Thought, and in-context learning), and a rules-based verifier checks conformance to real IC package and pin constraints, avoiding large-scale training. The project is open-sourced on GitHub. The excerpt does not mention pcbGPT or details about how KiCad is used to produce netlists/schematics; it focuses on PCBSchemaGen’s SKiDL-centric, verifier-backed, training-free approach to turn natural-language guided proposals into verified SKiDL code which can be translated into netlists/schematic artifacts.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT: it frames schematic generation as a code/program-synthesis task that produces KiCad-compatible artefacts via a Python-based circuit DSL. pcbGPT uses a tool-augmented synthesis agent that takes a natural-language request and emits an executable schematic description (a Python-based representation). The pipeline has three parts: (1) a synthesis agent that plans the design and issues tool calls rather than relying purely on model memory, (2) component tools that ground the agent in local KiCad libraries, exact component names, pin maps, footprint candidates, and datasheet-derived rules, and (3) a validation stage that runs syntax, structural, and semantic checks. The system iterates in a generate–execute–repair loop: propose a circuit, run validation to obtain concrete failure signals or critiques, and revise until valid or iteration limit reached. The paper positions this approach relative to code-first tools (e.g., SkiDL) but emphasizes compilation to editable KiCad schematics. Note: the excerpt does not describe PCBSchemaGen; no details about PCBSchemaGen's use of SKiDL or KiCad are present here.

snippets: This motivates the use of programmatic circuit design as the intermediate layer in our pipeline, since board-level hardware description languages and code-first PCB workflows already provide textual representations that compile into conventional design artefacts.
Our system differs by treating board-level schematic generation as a tool-grounded program synthesis task in a circuit DSL that compiles directly into editable KiCad artefacts.
The objective of pcbGPT is to translate a natural-language circuit specification into a KiCad-compatible schematic that can be inspected, edited, and reused in a standard PCB design workflow.

We treat the problem as a code generation task which we solve using three system components: a synthesis agent that plans and assembles the design, a set of component tools that ground the agent in the local KiCad libraries and component datasheets, and a validation stage that executes, checks, and critiques candidate designs.
The synthesis agent is responsible for converting the user prompt into an executable schematic description.

The component tools provide the grounding required for this process by connecting the language model to the exact component names, pin maps, footprint candidates, and datasheet-derived integration rules available in the local design environment.

The validation stage then checks whether the generated design is syntactically executable, compatible with the DSL and KiCad environment, and structurally and semantically plausible with respect to the original design intent.
Together, these three parts form an iterative generate, execute, repair loop.

Its input is a natural-language request such as a regulator stage, sensor interface, or complete embedded subsystem, and its output is a circuit expressed in our Python-based schematic representation.
First, it identifies the functional blocks implied by the prompt and selects concrete candidate components.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT, an LLM-driven synthesis agent that converts natural-language prompts into a Python circuit-DSL and uses tool augmentation and a local KiCad installation to ground design choices. The system uses a component search tool to find valid KiCad symbols and footprints and a component information tool that fetches and summarizes datasheets (rendering pages, extracting text, producing page-local schematic-design summaries, merging and caching them). Generated Python circuit-DSL code enters a validation pipeline: the code is executed to instantiate the design in the local environment, the system checks syntactic validity and conformance with the circuit representation and KiCad, runs ERC (electrical rules check), and then a validation agent inspects structural and semantic correctness against requirements and datasheet summaries. pcbGPT employs a generate–execute–repair loop where validation feedback (e.g., missing support circuitry, illegal pin references) is returned to the synthesis agent to iteratively refine the design. Final outputs include a KiCad project draft, netlist, and PDF artifacts for inspection.

contexts.context: The excerpt does not mention PCBSchemaGen or pcbGPT by name, but it describes relevant prior approaches and the SchGen alternative. Code-based PCB design tools like SKiDL are used to produce netlists via executable Python code, but such approaches "rely on manual coding and skips the schematic design step to generate netlists," meaning they typically do not produce editable, human-readable schematic images. Some iterative frameworks (e.g., Zou et al.) rely on SKiDL "schematic graph rendering to avoid spatial reasoning issues," which limits scalability for larger schematics. The paper chooses KiCad as the open-source schematic format ("we use the KiCad schematic format because it is open source"). SchGen instead introduces a semantic-grounded code representation that "encodes editing primitives and pin-level semantics," enabling an LLM to generate editable schematics from natural-language prompts and executable code. The authors also build a dataset by converting open-source designs into executable code via a human–agent pipeline. Together this implies a common pattern: SKiDL/code-first methods produce netlists but not visual schematics, while SchGen targets both semantics and editable schematic generation using KiCad-compatible representations.
contexts.question: What are PCBSchemaGen and pcbGPT approaches for LLM-based schematic generation from natural language? How do they use SKiDL and KiCad to produce netlists and schematics?

snippets: Recent research explored code-based PCB design, such as SKiDL [2025], but it still relies on manual coding and skips the schematic design step to generate netlists.
we use the KiCad schematic format because it is open source.
Our code representation encodes editing primitives and pin-level semantics, transforming geometry prediction to a semantics-driven task amenable to LLM training.
SchGen, the first LLM that generates editable PCB schematics from user prompts.
We construct a large-scale dataset of PCB schematics paired with user prompts via a human–agent collaborative pipeline that converts open-source designs into executable code representations.

Zou et al. [2026] provides a feedback-based iterative design framework for the PCB schematic design task; however, it is evaluated on only 23 tasks and relies on SKiDL schematic graph rendering to avoid spatial reasoning issues, which does not scale well to larger schematics.

contexts.context: Polymorphic Blocks is an HDL-driven system that lets designers specify system architecture at multiple abstraction levels (system, subcircuit, component) and then elaborates that HDL into a hierarchical block-graph model expanded by community/subcircuit libraries. The approach extends block diagrams with polymorphism and abstract-typed blocks (for example, a generic resistor super-type) and includes electronics modeling (currents/voltages). Users refine the generated graph interactively in a GUI; parameters are automatically propagated and checked for correctness. The system supports associating footprints (example annotation shown: footprint(res0603)) and can export the elaborated design as a netlist file which can then be imported into traditional board design/layout tools. The excerpt does not state that Polymorphic Blocks directly exports KiCad project files or board outlines, nor does it describe automated placement/routing or direct generation of board outlines — it only explicitly mentions netlist export and footprint annotations.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and approaches of Polymorphic Blocks, JITX, and atopile for programmatic PCB design? Do they export KiCad files, generate netlists, handle footprints and board outlines?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks (PolyBlocks) as an end-to-end system that moves from high-level hierarchical block diagrams to layout-ready circuits. Users author designs in a hardware description language (HDL) augmented by subcircuit generator libraries; generators are executable code that translate high-level specs into implementations (for example computing a resistor value for an LED). The system supports block-level polymorphism (refining abstract blocks with compatible concrete subtypes) to balance high-level architecture and low-level control. An underlying electronics model enforces constraints such as operating voltages and currents, and a graphical visualization and refinement interface enables interactive exploration of refinements. The design aims to be responsive by using a lighter-weight automated reasoning/propagation approach rather than heavyweight synthesis, emphasizing partnership with the designer. The excerpt highlights that the combination of HDL, electronics model, and UI distinguishes PolyBlocks from other textual PCBs HDLs. The excerpt does not state specifics about exporting formats or artifacts (e.g., KiCad files, netlists, footprints, or board outlines), nor does it mention JITX or atopile.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks (PolyBlocks) as a programmatic, mixed-abstraction PCB design approach where designs are represented as block diagrams that can be abstract, concrete, or a mix. The system provides active assistance: suggestions, error checks, and salient information. It emphasizes parametric components and code generators to promote reuse and automation; an HDL is used to write generators, but generated blocks can also be manipulated via a graphical, schematic-like interface. PolyBlocks can export a netlist to board layout tools, similar to conventional schematic capture. The system relies on libraries of parts and subcircuits (current library coverage is incomplete), and uses foundational abstractions like hierarchy blocks, links, and parameters. Limitations noted include a simplified electronics model (e.g., lacking support for multiple grounds) and the need for better integration between HDL, refinements, and graphical visualizations. Usability points include refinements stored separately from HDL, calls for tighter HDL/diagram update loops, and suggestions for non-fatal checks and improved automatic layouts and naming.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and approaches of Polymorphic Blocks, JITX, and atopile for programmatic PCB design? Do they export KiCad files, generate netlists, handle footprints and board outlines?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks as an end-to-end system for authoring hierarchical block diagrams for board-level design. Users write designs in a hardware description language (HDL) augmented by subcircuit generator libraries; generators are executable code that translate high-level specifications into implementations and can compute parameters (e.g., a LED-resistor subcircuit that calculates resistance). The system supports block-level polymorphism—refining blocks with compatible subtypes to swap, e.g., an abstract voltage converter for a specific buck converter—so high-level architecture and low-level control coexist. An underlying electronics model performs constraint checks (operating voltages, currents) while a graphical visualization and refinement interface lets users iteratively obtain a layout-ready circuit. The paper emphasizes separation of interface and implementation, reuse via generator libraries, and distinguishes this approach from purely textual PCB HDLs and schematic-only tools. The excerpt does not mention practical export details (KiCad file export), netlist generation, footprint assignment, or board outline/placement/routing behaviors, so those capabilities are not reported here.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and approaches of Polymorphic Blocks, JITX, and atopile for programmatic PCB design? Do they export KiCad files, generate netlists, handle footprints and board outlines?

snippets: We implement this new model of circuit design in Polymorphic Blocks, an end-to-end system for authoring block diagrams.
users write designs in a hardware description language (HDL) with the aid of subcircuit generator libraries, then interactively explore refinements to obtain a layout-ready circuit.
An underlying electronics model checks designs using constraints such as operating voltages and currents.

Writing these blocks as generators – executable code to translate high-level specifications into an implementation, e.g. a LED-resistor subcircuit that calculates resistance from input voltage – separates interface from implementation

block-level polymorphism – refining blocks with compatible subtypes, e.g., substituting a specific buck converter in place of an abstract voltage converter – balances high-level design with fine-grained control.

This combination of HDL, electronics model, and user interface distinguishes our work from related work on purely textual PCB HDL efforts [3, 25] and high-level design tools that don’t also allow lower-level control [2].

contexts.context: Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-embedded hardware description language (HCL) with generator-based blocks (generators run during a compiler "lowering" pass) and a visualization/refinement GUI. It is implemented as a Python library (with mypy annotations) and is open-source on GitHub. The compiler incrementally lowers hierarchical designs and then performs a netlisting phase that "builds up and writes out the index of footprints, pins, and connections." The system can export a netlist suitable for import into KiCad, and supports updating an in-progress KiCad layout by generating deterministic component names from HCL variable names (to keep component identity stable across edits). However, Polymorphic Blocks explicitly does not address board layout itself; board layout/outline generation is not handled by the system. Additional implementation notes include use of a tree-walk lowering process and the requirement that generator code provide worst-case bounds for parameters since generators run without a specific ordering.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and approaches of Polymorphic Blocks, JITX, and atopile for programmatic PCB design? Do they export KiCad files, generate netlists, handle footprints and board outlines?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes EDG as a proof-of-concept automated reasoning tool that lets a user provide a high-level control-logic specification and produces a finished schematic, demonstrating that much of embedded design can be automated. EDG handles a range of inputs—from minimal synthesis specs to partial or complete designs—and produces outputs that remain correct even when available parts change. Building on EDG, Polymorphic Blocks is presented as a user-facing tool: designers write system architecture in an HDL, which is elaborated into a hierarchical block-graph model and augmented with community libraries. That graph is refined via an interactive GUI with automatic parameter propagation and correctness checks. The workflow supports exporting a netlist file for import into board-layout tools. The figure text also shows explicit footprint annotations (e.g., "footprint(res0603)") in the model. The excerpt does not mention KiCad-specific exports, explicit board-outline generation, or detailed handling of PCB footprints beyond the presence of footprint annotations and netlist export capability.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the Polymorphic Blocks system: the authors implemented an HDL and compiler built on a hierarchy/block-diagram abstraction that provides design automation and can produce hardware-proven boards. Designers compose systems from high-level blocks; experienced engineers implement those blocks as reusable generators (executable code). The system generates complex subcircuits from high-level specifications and is demonstrated with working examples. The approach is library-driven — reuse depends on having good, complete libraries — and the current library is useful but incomplete, with complex-part details often only in PDF datasheets. While writing generators requires the HDL, the generated blocks and design model can be exposed in a graphical, schematic-like interface to lower the need for programming. The electronics model worked for intermediate-level projects but has known limitations (only a few signal interfaces, no multiple-ground support). The authors note the need for improved datasheet parsing, larger community libraries, GUI integration, and further user studies as future work.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and approaches of Polymorphic Blocks, JITX, and atopile for programmatic PCB design? Do they export KiCad files, generate netlists, handle footprints and board outlines?

contexts.context: This excerpt (Vassallo & Bajada, DATE 2024) describes an end-to-end RL approach to PCB component placement that frames placement as an MDP and trains agents (TD3 and SAC). On unseen circuits, their policies outperform state-of-the-art simulated annealing baselines, producing average post-routing wirelength reductions of 17% (TD3) and 21% (SAC). The authors report emergent collaborative/competitive behaviours and substantially faster placement convergence in some cases, “sometimes exceeding an order of magnitude.” Their state representation uses image-processing: grayscale layered images of the netlist (0.5 mm resolution) plus per-component masks for local overlap and line-of-sight detection. They provide code, datasets, and pre-generated results on GitHub. The excerpt contains no performance numbers, benchmarks, or success rates for HiePlace or Li’s double-layer placement algorithm; additional sources are needed to compare those methods and to obtain their reported metrics and benchmarks.
contexts.question: What are the recent results in deep reinforcement learning for PCB component placement, and what benchmarks or success rates are reported? Include HiePlace, Vassallo learning circuit placement, Li double-layer placement algorithm.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports results from Vassallo & Bajada (2024) on RL-based PCB component placement. They formulated iterative placement as an MDP, trained policies with SAC and TD3, and used an adaptive reward signal. A highlighted configuration (n=2, m=6, p=2) trained with SAC "outperformed SA-PCB on all unseen layouts, resulting in an average wirelength reduction of 21.1%", while TD3 on the same configuration gave a 17.4% improvement. The authors note that "Configurations 1-4 tend to meet and exceed the performance of SA-PCB" and that learned policies show a good understanding of task dynamics. The baseline used throughout is SA-PCB (Simulated Annealing-based Placement For PCB Layout), and the primary reported metric is post-routing wirelength. The paper also discusses policy behaviour (Fig. 3) and ablation-style experiments; overall the results indicate competitive performance versus simulated annealing and that experience-guided actions yield faster/better placements compared to blind attempts.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that several reinforcement-learning-based placement models outperform the ground truth (GT) on the TEWL metric for most PCB test cases, with the exception of some complex boards (U20 and U26). Among the evaluated agents, A2C is described as the best-performing ML model overall, even though it produces more routing conflicts and worse TEWL than DQN on the most complex cases. A net-aware variant, DQNnet, that encodes net information shows "significant improvements across all the PCBs in terms of TEWL" compared to vanilla DQN and reduces overlapping passive components, though it slightly increases routing conflicts. The paper frames the contribution as a "novel RL approach for PCB placement with component centric layout." The excerpt cites related works (e.g., Mirhoseini 2020; Vassallo & Bajada 2024) but does not provide numeric benchmarks, success rates, or any information about HiePlace or Li's double-layer placement algorithm. Thus, while it gives qualitative comparative findings (TEWL improvements, conflict trade-offs, and DQNnet gains), it lacks explicit numeric success rates or benchmark comparisons for HiePlace, Vassallo’s results, or Li’s algorithm.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a component-centric, discrete-action RL approach for PCB component placement where a main component is fixed at the center and passive components are positioned near power pins to reduce wirelength and avoid overlaps. The PCB is modeled as a set of discrete candidate locations around the main part, which reduces search space and allows RL agents to place components into valid positions. The authors implemented and compared multiple methods including Deep Q-Network (DQN), Actor-Critic, and Simulated Annealing. Evaluation on over nine real-world PCBs of varying complexity reportedly shows the best proposed method "approaches near human-like placements in terms of wirelength and feasibility." The paper also discusses common PCB placement challenges for RL: supporting single- and double-sided boards, handling diverse component sizes, ensuring non-overlap, and balancing wirelength/congestion/feasibility in rewards. The excerpt does not mention HiePlace, Vassallo (learning circuit placement), Li (double-layer placement algorithm), nor does it provide numeric benchmarks or success rates for those works, so no specific benchmark figures for them are available in this excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt references recent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) work for chip placement (Mirhoseini and Goldie et al.'s "Chip Placement with Deep Reinforcement Learning") and positions that approach as a foundation for applying DRL + Graph Neural Networks to PCB component placement. The thesis extends those methods toward PCB use, focusing on a constrained subproblem called ring placements and reports encouraging results for an end-to-end DRL system on that subset. It also explicitly notes limitations and scaling challenges that must be addressed before production deployment. The text emphasizes the inherent difficulty of placement because routing is NP-Complete, that PCB placement remains computationally prohibitive, and that most auto-placement relies on heuristics and DRCs. It also cites AI-driven routing efforts (e.g., InstaDeep) but states current AI routing works mainly on simple 2-layer boards with fewer than 150 connection pairs. The excerpt does not provide numerical benchmarks, success rates, or specific results for HiePlace, Vassallo, or Li's algorithms, so it is insufficient by itself to answer the question about reported benchmarks and success rates for those methods.

contexts.context: This excerpt reports results from a paper that combines deep reinforcement learning (RL) and simulated annealing (SA) in a cyclic framework for placement (Sequence Pair representation). Training/testing settings: ami49 was trained for 10 epochs with the agent taking 200 RL steps followed by 5000 SA steps; lattice experiments ran 15 epochs with 200 RL steps and 5000 SA steps per epoch. Runtime: each training epoch averages 17 seconds; after training, RL+SA takes ~17 seconds to run 5000 steps to find the final solution, while pure SA takes ~16 seconds for 5000 steps. Results (averages over 10 runs) show RL-initialized SA outperforms random initialization (pure SA) on lattice benchmarks: for 100 blocks RL init cost 3,386 µm vs random 3,532 µm; for 225 blocks 14,348 vs 14,844. On ami49 (10-run averages), RL init yields lower area costs: with fixed blocks 49 mm2 vs 53 mm2; without fixed blocks 43 mm2 vs 46 mm2. The paper reports smaller standard deviations for RL initialization, and concludes the cyclic RL+SA framework provides better initializations and lower-cost placements than pure SA.

contexts.context: The HiePlace input description includes an irregular fixed outline, a set of modules M (containing chips U and components C), per-component pins P with fixed coordinates, a netlist N, preplaced components with fixed corner coordinates, obstacle blocks, and spacing constraints between components and the outline. The desired output is a detailed placement that places chips and components within the outline. Objectives are to minimize weighted wirelength and white-space ratio, reduce the number of misaligned areas (alignment constraint), and minimize net crossings for high-priority components; same-layer chip-to-component connections are preferred to reduce drilling and improve routability. Preprocessing handles irregular outlines ("To fill the irregular outline, we employ five blocks") and stacked/irregular components via a subdivision strategy and a cohesion constraint to keep subcomponents' relative coordinates. Methodologically, HiePlace adopts an MP-based approach converting constraints to mathematical formulas and introduces a novel grouping algorithm that clusters highly correlated components to improve alignment and reduce solution space/runtime. The excerpt reports qualitative improvements (better alignment, improved routability, minimal change to overall wirelength) and shows example results (Fig. 2(d)), but it does not provide numeric benchmarks or detailed quantitative results in the provided pages.
contexts.question: HiePlace hierarchical PCB placement algorithm: what inputs does it accept, what are the reported results and benchmarks?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HiePlace as an MP-based, hierarchical PCB placement framework targeting PCB-specific constraints and metrics (noting first-priority components and core chips). It introduces two acceleration techniques: DP for first-priority component placement and a relaxation algorithm that reduces Boolean variables and the solution space. Routability was compared against Floorplet using metrics such as estimated HPWL for first-priority components/core chips, misaligned regions (MC), and net crossings; the experiments reported use the first 10 PCB designs with feasible placements. Reported results include an average reduction of 16% in wirelength and 46% in misaligned regions versus the baseline, plus significant runtime improvements and lower costs. HiePlace also reportedly optimized PCB-specific design metrics. The paper notes remaining limitations: routability evaluation (currently treated as a superposition of indicators like net intersection and alignment) and lack of double-sided layout support, with plans to extend the model and include quick routing feasibility checks and interlayer cost handling in future work.
contexts.question: HiePlace hierarchical PCB placement algorithm: what inputs does it accept, what are the reported results and benchmarks?

snippets: In this section, we compare our approach with Floorplet on   key metrics affecting routability.
We compared the solution on the first 10 PCB designs, which have feasible placement results.
our method achieves an average reduction of 16% in wirelength   and 46% in misaligned regions while effectively minimizing the impact of line crossings.

Our framework introduced two innovative acceleration techniques: DP for first-priority component placement and a relaxation algorithm that minimizes Boolean variables while effectively reducing the solution space.
Experimental results demonstrated significant improvements in both runtime and wirelength reduction compared to existing methods.
The most critical among these are routability evaluation and double-sided layout.
HiePlace also successfully optimized PCB-speciﬁc design metrics, validating its effectiveness in automating PCB placement while satisfying complex design requirements.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the HiePlace implementation and experimental setup. The implementation is in C++ and the MP-based subproblem is solved via Gurobi. The authors evaluate on 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industry, 4 academic), with density defined as sum of component areas over the irregular outline area; PCB8 (141 components) is used for convergence studies. Reported metrics include HPWLall (half-perimeter wirelength), Area Ratio (computed by a sweepline algorithm), Cost (defined by equation (17)), alignment-path count, and runtime. Key empirical findings: HiePlace converges rapidly (near-optimal within ~10 min), scales to large cases (most significant cases finish in <2 h), and outperforms prior MP-based and Quilter baselines. On average it reduces cost by 66% and 18% versus two baselines while using only 13% of their runtime. Versus Quilter on four academic benchmarks, cost reductions range 76.7%–91.3% (e.g., bm4 from 19.46×10^3 to 3.58×10^3) and runtime drops 99.4%–99.9% (Quilter: 34–75 min; HiePlace: a few seconds). Improvements in routability, fewer net crossings, and better alignment are also reported.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt documents inputs and the algorithmic flow of HiePlace but contains no experimental numbers or benchmarks. Explicitly, Algorithm 1 lists its inputs as “a set of components C, a set of pins P, an set of net N.” The method begins with preprocessing to handle irregular outlines by using a rectangle bounding box and treating whitespace as pre-placed components. The placement model enforces overlap and boundary constraints using Boolean variables pi,j and qi,j (e.g., xci + wci ≤ xcj + W · pi,j + qi,j and pi,j, qi,j ∈ {0,1}). To reduce MP solver complexity, the authors “propose a hierarchical approach that reduces the reliance on Boolean variables and narrows the solution space.” The algorithm is multi-stage ("The algorithm has five steps, as shown in Fig. 6"): component grouping via a component graph Gc, DP for first-priority/core components, relaxation to narrow solution space, an optimized MP-based placement for remaining components, and a final fast greedy fine-tuning. Grouping formulas (Chnew ≡ Chorigin × Vcnt + sp × (Vcnt −1)) and grouping/spacing rules are provided. The excerpt does not include reported results, performance metrics, or benchmark comparisons.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HiePlace as an MP-based placement framework built specifically for PCB placement, intended to handle PCB-specific requirements that differ from VLSI. It explicitly accepts and accounts for diverse constraints in PCB designs, mentioning "priorities, irregularities, and alignment" as example inputs/constraints the method considers. To accelerate the otherwise time-consuming MP approach, HiePlace uses two techniques: an initial-stage dynamic programming method to "prioritize the placement of core components," and a relaxation algorithm to "minimize the number of boolean variables," thereby narrowing the solution space. The paper compares HiePlace against analytical placers (illustrated in Fig. 2) and reports experimental improvements of "7.7× speed up and 66% cost reduction." The excerpt discusses PCB-specific issues such as irregular board outlines and component placement patterns, and shows that HiePlace can consider alignment and component priorities in placement results. The excerpt does not list specific benchmark suite names, dataset sizes, or detailed metrics beyond the summarized speedup and cost reduction.

contexts.context: PCB-Bench evaluates LLMs/MLLMs on three complementary PCB tasks and modalities: (1) large-scale text-to-text QA and choice questions (about 1,800 free-form QAs with corresponding CQs, ~3,700 questions total) to test generative and factual reasoning; (2) image-and-text multimodal reasoning (about 500 questions) pairing natural language prompts with PCB placement images; and (3) real-world PCB design comprehension using over 170 complete designs (schematic diagrams, design intents, component libraries, placement and routing files) for schematic-to-description tasks. The benchmark explicitly measures generative output with BERTScore and SBERT similarity and factual accuracy for choice questions (accuracy). The authors report that current LLMs struggle with real-world engineering constraints, producing errors in rule adherence, logical consistency, and component semantics, and that performance varies across models and task types. PCB-Bench also provides a curated dataset of over 170 real-world PCB designs and standardized protocols (task formats, metrics, prompt designs) to support reproducible evaluation of foundation models on PCB placement and routing.
contexts.question: PCB-Bench benchmark for LLMs in PCB placement and routing: what tasks does it evaluate, what results are reported?

contexts.context: PCB-Bench is a multimodal benchmark for evaluating LLMs on PCB placement and routing tasks, combining images, text, and design artifacts. The paper defines multiple tasks including structured classification/answering metrics (CBC, CBFB, BWEI, QAP/QAR) and Task 3: PCB Design Understanding, which measures "how well models interpret PCB designs from editor screenshots through free-form functional and structural descriptions." Evaluation uses semantic metrics (BERTScore, SBERT) and structured metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1). Reported results show model trade-offs: GPT-5 delivers the most balanced performance (e.g., CBC/CBFB/BWEI line: "GPT-5 83.26 75.67 90.90 ..." and Task 3 BERTScore/F1: "GPT-5 0.8156 0.6134 0.8493 0.8691 0.8591"). GPT-5-nano scores highly on semantic similarity but is mid-tier in accuracy; Gemini-2.5-Pro attains top CBFB accuracy and perfect BWEI in the reported table. The study finds no single model dominates all dimensions and notes a clear trade-off between structured accuracy and semantic coherence. Overall leaders include Gemini-2.5, GPT-4o/5, Claude, and Qwen-VL-Max. The authors conclude that larger and domain-adapted models (or EDA integration) and a dedicated PCB evaluation system are needed since current LLMs remain insufficient for realistic open-ended PCB placement/routing.
contexts.question: PCB-Bench benchmark for LLMs in PCB placement and routing: what tasks does it evaluate, what results are reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCB-Bench's evaluation tasks but does not report model performance results. PCB-Bench defines three task settings: (1) Text-to-Text QA and CQ: over 1,800 text QA instances with matching choice questions (≈3,700 total questions), covering placement and routing topics and difficulty levels. (2) Image-and-Text Multimodal QA and CQ: multimodal instances combining PCB layout images and text prompts; the paper describes a multimodal dataset of about 500 image+text instances (300 single-choice, 100 cloze, 100 open-ended) and a task set of roughly 400 choice/cloze questions plus 100 QA-form questions. These target visual-semantic subtasks such as component recognition, functional block identification, trace type detection, signal layer classification, and via presence checking. (3) PCB Design Understanding: visual-only interpretation using 174 real-world PCB designs/screenshots (extracted from EDA tools) where models must generate free-form descriptions of board function and structure. The excerpt outlines dataset construction and task objectives but contains no numerical or comparative results for model performance.

snippets: Task 1: Text-to-Text QA and CQ.

This task is constructed from 1,800+ text-based QA instances derived from realistic PCB placement and routing contexts. For each QA instance, we additionally provide a corresponding choice question, resulting in a total of approximately 3,700 questions.
Task 2: Image-and-Text Multimodal QA and CQ.
It includes approximately 400 questions, presented in either choice question or cloze-style (fill-in-the-blank) formats, and 100 QA-form questions.
including component recognition, functional block identification, trace type detection, signal layer classification, and via presence checking.

We construct a multimodal dataset of about 500 instances that combine PCB layout images with textual prompts. Specifically, it includes 300 single-choice questions, 100 cloze-style fill-in-the-blank questions, and 100 open-ended QA instances.
Task 3: PCB Design Understanding.
This task focuses on high-level interpretation of professional PCB placement artifacts through visual input alone.
We collect 174 complete PCB designs (Figure 2) from JL-CPCB1.
Each screenshot is used as a standalone input, without accompanying textual descriptions or schematic information.
Together, these tasks provide a comprehensive and multimodal framework for evaluating the capabilities of foundation models on practical, structured engineering problems.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents PCB-Bench’s evaluation setup and reported results for LLMs on PCB placement (and parts of routing). It explicitly lists task 1 as Placement with both Macro-Level and Micro-Level evaluations: Macro-Level includes question-answer evaluations reported as choice-question accuracy (percentage) and open-ended QA scored by BERTScore and SBERT; Micro-Level is reported as choice-question accuracy. Subtopics evaluated within placement include Module Placement, Power/Ground Planning, Signal/EMI/EMC, DFM, and Practical Concerns. The paper provides fine-grained per-model scores in tables and figures (Table 5, Table 6, Table 7, Figure 8, Figure 9), comparing many state-of-the-art LLMs (examples shown include GPT-4o, GPT-5, Claude-Opus-4.1, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-Chat-V3.1-671B, LLaMA-4-Maverick-400B, InternVL3-78B, MythoMax-L2-13B, Rocinante-12B, Ministral-3B, Qwen variants). Metrics reported are choice-question accuracy (%) and open-ended QA scores (BERTScore and SBERT), enabling granular comparison of strengths/weaknesses across PCB design subtopics.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCB-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs on printed circuit board placement and routing. It defines three task settings: (1) text-based reasoning (~3,700 expert-annotated instances, >1,800 QA pairs) focused on component placement, routing strategies, and design-rule compliance; (2) multimodal image–text reasoning (~500 problems) requiring joint interpretation of PCB visuals and specifications, including component identification, function recognition, and visual trace reasoning; and (3) real-world design comprehension (over 170 complete PCB projects) involving schematics, placement files, and design documentation. The benchmark includes structured evaluation protocols to assess both generative and discriminative capabilities and supports multi-modal inputs (RGB images, text, schematic diagrams, and design files). The authors run extensive comparisons across state-of-the-art LLMs/MLLMs and report that current models show substantial gaps: they struggle with spatial placement reasoning, following domain-specific constraints, and interpreting professional engineering artifacts. The paper positions PCB-Bench as filling an evaluation gap in EDA and provides data and code publicly at the project repository.
contexts.question: PCB-Bench benchmark for LLMs in PCB placement and routing: what tasks does it evaluate, what results are reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HWE-Bench evaluation tasks, metrics, and key results for LLMs applied to board-level schematic design. Tasks include static rule checking (topology and label-matching), dynamic behavior (SPICE-style) simulation, and an overall correct-design metric. Results are reported across multiple application domains (Aerospace, Educational, Automotive, Telecom, IoT, Consumer, Healthcare, Industrial). Aggregate metrics: static rule-checking average pass rate ≈ 71.84% (Table III), dynamic simulation average pass rate ≈ 58.65% (Table IV), and very low overall correct-design rates per model (Table V shows values like 5.12%, 8.15%, 6.58%, 5.16%, 4.4%, 5.03%). The paper notes models often score higher on “spice simulation pass rate” than on strict static checks because they create functionally equivalent connections that pass simulation but violate rigid physical labeling or exclusivity constraints. Identified failure modes include entity role confusion, label conflicts, and pin multiplexing mistakes (microcontroller pins assigned incompatible simultaneous roles). HWE-Bench applied signal-chain constraints via prompt engineering (an internal signal chain inference mechanism) to reduce label mapping errors and resolve role conflicts in case studies.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HWE-Bench as a board-level circuit design benchmark based on 300 real-world open-source projects across 8 domains, with 2,914 collected IC datasheets. Task definition: given semi-structured functional requirements and IC datasheets, an LLM must generate a complete circuit schematic netlist that satisfies the project tests. The benchmark uses a two-step data collection/refinement pipeline and guides models through a four-stage constrained generative pipeline: partitioning into functional submodules (each capturing core functionality, interactions, and I/O), component allocation, module-level local network table generation, and system-level global network integration. Metrics and evaluation: HWE-Bench moves beyond static text metrics and uses an execution-driven notion of ‘functional correctness’ by combining static electrical rule checking (rigorous pin-connection checks via graph topology algorithms) with dynamic physical verification (integrating generated netlists into LTspice for physical-level and transient timing simulations). snippets: HWE-Bench is a board-level circuit design benchmark built based on real-world open-source hardware projects.
The task is according to given circuit functional requirements and a series of integrated circuit (IC) datasheets, to generate a complete circuit schematic netlist, and pass relevant tests.

By seamlessly integrating expert-level static electrical rule checking with dynamic physical simulations, HWE-Bench establishes a rigorous, execution-driven metric for ‘functional correctness’ in the physical world.

It not only conducts rigorous static pin connection checks using modern graph topology algorithms to verify physical constraints [16], but also seamlessly integrates the generated system-level netlists into industry-standard LTspice simulation environments for dynamic physical level and transient timing tests.
To find high-quality task instances at scale, we use a 2-step pipeline as follows.
A total of 2914 datasheets were obtained, and their distribution is presented in Table II.
This eventually resulted in 300 projects covering 8 areas, each with documentation of functional specifications, bill of materials, and reference designs.
the LLM is guided through a four-stage constrained generative pipeline.

Each module definition is mandated to capture three key aspects: (1) its core functionality, (2) its interaction with other modules in the signal chain, and (3) the format of its input/output interfaces.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the HWE-Bench-style benchmark used to evaluate LLMs for board-level PCB schematic design. The benchmark contains 23 unique tasks built from real-world IC components and datasheets, covering analog, digital, power, and mixed circuits. Task difficulty is controlled by circuit node and net counts, spanning designs with roughly 4 component pins up to over 100 component pins. Evaluation and metrics emphasize topology correctness and interpretable verification: topology is represented as a bipartite graph and checked via subgraph isomorphism using the VF2 algorithm. The verification framework produces different feedback/message granularities used as metrics or signals: detailed and specific error messages (syntax, ERC, subgraph verification with precise pin/net/part-value errors), concise error messages (type only), and binary pass/fail. The experimental setup applies prompt-engineering strategies (ICL, CoT), multi-stage verification with feedback loops, and a subcircuit library reuse mechanism; ablation studies reportedly show that retrieval/reuse improves success rates. The excerpt does not include numeric performance values or tables—results are said to be summarized in Table 1 and Section 4.2, but those numeric results are not present in the provided text.

contexts.context: The provided pages describe HWE-Bench's task set, pipeline, and evaluation approach. The benchmark contains 300 standardized circuit design tasks plus a library of 2,914 cleaned datasheets. Tasks are decomposed into submodules (component assignment, module-level local netlist generation, then system-level global netlist integration). The model is constrained to output structured JSON/ plain-text netlists; automated parsers validate formats and convert outputs. Verification is dual-stage: static rule checking (rule-based label matching/topological and protocol checks) and dynamic SPICE transient simulation using human-constructed ground-truth SPICE circuits wrapped in Python testbenches. The core metric is the aggregate pass rate across static and dynamic checkpoints. Success is defined as avoiding physical damage and keeping node voltages/currents within predefined safety thresholds under specified excitations. snippets: Ultimately, we curated a high-quality structured dataset comprising 300 standardized circuit design tasks and 2,914 cleaned, structured electronic component datasheets.

Decomposed task setting. Complex system-level designs are decomposed into functionally distinct submodules, guiding the model to execute the entire design pipeline step-by-step, from functional partitioning to global netlist generation.
Component assignment. Leveraging the structured datasheet library provided by HWE-Bench, the model selects and allocates appropriate electronic components for each submodule [26].
Module schematic. Based on the internal signal chain of each submodule, along with the pin definitions of the selected electronic components, the model constructs the corresponding local netlist.
System schematic. This stage severely tests the model’s system-level physical intuition, requiring it to correctly handle the signal logic relationships and power networks between submodules.
Static checking utilizes rule-based label matching to evaluate the structural correctness of the system-level netlist.
Dynamic simulation validates the operational behavior of the circuit.
The LLMs-generated netlists are subsequently converted into SPICE format and injected into this Python testbench for automated transient simulation.
To ensure rigorous evaluation, human experts pre-construct a functionally correct ground-truth SPICE circuit, which is then encapsulated into a Python-driven test script.
A design is deemed successful if it avoids physical damage and maintains all key node voltages and currents within predefined safety thresholds under specified excitations.
The core evaluation metric is the aggregate pass rate across these static and dynamic checkpoints
Dual-stage verification system. A dual-stage verification framework is proposed, integrating static electrical rule checking and dynamic SPICE transient simulation.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench is an execution-driven benchmark for LLMs on board-level schematic design. The benchmark contains 300 board-level design tasks across 8 application domains and includes a knowledge base of 2,914 real IC datasheets. For each task, models must generate a schematic from scratch using only the provided functional requirements and component datasheets. Evaluation is two-stage: static electrical rule checking of the produced netlists, followed by dynamic verification via integration into an industry-standard LTspice simulator to check transient behaviors. The authors standardize tasks with machine-checkable goals and pre-parsed datasheet representations and provide a test pipeline designed to emulate human engineering workflows. They evaluated several state-of-the-art LLMs (DeepSeek-v3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Qwen3-Max, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok-4). Results show limited physical reasoning: while models show some engineering/documentation understanding, the top-performing model achieved an overall pass rate of only 8.15%, indicating substantial gaps in physical intuition and practical board-level design capability.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that HWE-Bench is an evaluation framework covering 300 real-world board-level schematic design tasks across 8 application domains and referencing 2,914 integrated-circuit datasheets. The benchmark applies a design-constraint abstraction that groups MCU physical resources into functional groups (e.g., I²C, SPI) plus a general GPIO pool to enforce interface protocol contracts. For static verification HWE-Bench uses a functional group–based rule verification method and a ‘pin-locking’ mechanism that marks pins as ‘locked’ when a connection passes the functional-group attribute check; subsequent reuse triggers LOCK CONFLICT warnings to catch resource contention and exclusivity violations. In addition to static checks, the framework integrates dynamic validation via SPICE simulation. The reported results (in this excerpt) emphasize that these combined static and dynamic evaluations reveal critical limitations in current LLMs’ physical reasoning for schematic design. The excerpt does not provide numeric metric values or per-model performance numbers; it describes tasks, verification methods, and qualitative outcomes (identified limitations and conflict detection).

snippets: This paper introduces the HWE-Bench evaluation frame- work, which encompasses 300 real-world design tasks span- ning 8  application domains and  2,914  integrated  circuit datasheets.

We constructed a design constraint model based on func- tion al groups, partitioning all physical resources of the MCU into dedicated bus groups (e.g., I²C, SPI) and a general GPIO resource pool based on their underlying electrical attributes [26].
Whenever a connec- tion in the netlist passes the functional group attribute check, the corresponding physical pin is automatically marked by the system as ‘locked’
Integrated with dynamic validation via SPICE simulation, our evaluation results identify critical limitations in the physical reasoning capabilities of existing models.

Through  the adoption  of a  ‘pin-locking’ mechanism, we effectively mitigate logical conflicts and resource contention that arise when large language models(LLMs) process MCU pin multiplexing scenarios.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench evaluates LLMs on board-level schematic design using a generative pipeline that mimics human engineering and breaks design into sub-tasks (e.g., module partitioning). The benchmark uses a three-tier verification hierarchy: static rule checks (topology/ pin-interface consistency), dynamic SPICE transient simulation driven via LTspice, and a global correctness metric requiring full system-level functionality. It also compiles generated netlists into SPICE scripts and applies electrical metric monitoring across the simulation lifecycle (probe voltages/currents must meet thresholds). The authors tested six LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.5, DeepSeek-v3, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-max, Grok-4) with standardized sampling (T=0.1, top-p=0.95). Results: static checks show an overall average pass rate of 71.84% (Claude Sonnet 4.5: 77.73%; DeepSeek-v3: 74.78%). Dynamic simulation pass rates are lower (Claude Sonnet 4.5: 67.92%; Qwen3-Max: 60.21%; Gemini 3.1 Pro: 60.00%). Global correctness (end-to-end functional designs) is very low: Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 8.15%, GPT-5.2 at 6.58%, others ~4–5%, indicating substantial gaps in physical reasoning and cross-module coherence.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen's semantic-grounded code representation approach for PCB schematic generation. The system is training-free: an LLM (with domain-specific prompting, CoT and ICL) acts as a proposal generator to synthesize executable SKiDL code without fine-tuning. Generation is anchored by a multi-phase verifier driven by a knowledge graph (KG) built from open-source real-world IC datasheets (pin-role semantics, multiple constraint types, and topological constraints) and SI-driven checks; this verifier returns interpretable error messages to enable iterative feedback. Inputs include natural-language specifications and domain knowledge from IC datasheets (the KG), and outputs are executable SKiDL code and downstream KiCAD PCB layout files (netlist/layout-ready artifacts) using real-world IC packages. For evaluation, the authors curated a benchmark of 23 unique circuits spanning digital, analog, and power domains, each tied to real IC components. The paper claims end-to-end capability from natural language to KiCAD-ready files and emphasizes reproducibility and practicality, but the excerpt contains no detailed quantitative metrics or comparative results.

PCB schematic tasks Benchmarking: To our knowledge, we curated a first benchmark for evaluate the PCB generative ability of LLMs.

contexts.context: The paper frames PCB schematic generation as tool-grounded program synthesis in a Python-based circuit DSL that compiles into editable KiCad artefacts. The pipeline has three core parts: (1) a tool-augmented synthesis agent that accepts a natural-language request (examples: regulator stage, sensor interface, or complete embedded subsystem) and produces a circuit in the DSL, (2) component tools that ground the agent by searching local KiCad libraries and retrieving datasheet-derived component information (pin maps, footprints, integration rules), and (3) a validation stage that executes the DSL, checks syntactic correctness, and performs structural and semantic validation (rather than relying on full electrical simulation). These parts operate in an iterative generate–execute–repair loop: the agent issues tool calls to find symbols and component knowledge, assembles a candidate schematic, the validator returns concrete failure signals or critiques, and the agent revises the design. The excerpt documents inputs (natural-language requirements, KiCad libraries, datasheets), the output (KiCad-compatible schematic / Python DSL representation), grounding strategies, and the validation-driven iterative workflow. The excerpt does not include explicit benchmark descriptions or quantitative results.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT, an LLM-driven pipeline for automatic PCB schematic synthesis from natural-language requirements. The approach uses three building blocks: a synthesis agent, component tools, and a validation stage. The synthesis agent interprets user requirements, searches the local KiCad library for symbols, retrieves and extracts datasheet knowledge, and proposes candidate circuit designs. Generated designs are subjected to deterministic structural checks and a semantic review (an iterative generate–execute–repair loop) rather than full electrical simulation. A Python-based circuit DSL is used as the programmatic intermediate representation and to export KiCad-compatible artifacts. Inputs are natural-language descriptions or existing KiCad projects; outputs are editable KiCad project drafts (schematics) intended for inspection and refinement by hardware-literate users. Evaluation is performed via a set of reference circuits and expert review, including an empirical benchmark on 20 representative embedded/IoT/wearable tasks with automated comparisons to reference implementations and expert assessment. The excerpt describes methodology and evaluation design but does not provide quantitative success rates or detailed numerical results.

snippets: pcbGPT: an LLM-driven system for automated printed circuit board (PCB) schematic generation from natural language specifications.
Inputs to the system can be either natural-language descriptions of desired functionality or existing KiCad projects that the system can extend or modify based on user instructions.
The output of the system is an editable KiCad project draft containing the generated schematic, which can be opened, inspected, modified, and used as a starting point for downstream EDA workflows.
Our approach consists of three building blocks: a synthesis agent, a set of component tools, and a validation stage.
the synthesis agent interprets the user requirements and proposes candidate circuit designs by orchestrating the component tools

ground this process in the local KiCad environment by allowing the synthesis agent to search for suitable symbols, retrieve datasheets, and extract component-specific design knowledge needed for correct integration.
forming an iterative generate, execute, and repair loop that refines the design until it passes all checks.
deterministic structural checks before applying semantic review to assess whether the circuit plausibly satisfies the design intent.
A Python-based circuit DSL for schematic synthesis and evaluation, supporting structured circuit construction, optional components, flexible pin assignments, and export to KiCad-compatible artifacts.
We evaluate our system with a series of reference circuits and expert review.

an empirical evaluation of schematic generation on 20 representative embedded, IoT, and wearable circuit design tasks, using reference implementations and interface constraints for automated comparison alongside expert review of generated circuits.

contexts.context: The paper presents SchGen, a PCB schematic generation system that finetunes a 20B-parameter base LLM (GPT-oss-20b) using a semantic-grounded code representation called Code-L1. Code-L1 encodes schematics with features such as relative coordinates and pin-name connectivity; ablations (Code-L2 without relative coordinates and Code-L3 without relative coordinates and pin-name connectivity) isolate the contribution of these choices. Training uses example code, uniform prompts, and provided Python APIs; all models are evaluated in a single-run setting on identical test inputs. Outputs are generated schematic code/netlists that are measured by structural and functional metrics: valid circuit pass ratio, spatial violations (overlaps), netlist accuracy (Jaccard, precision, recall), symbol/pin connection errors, and expert functional verification. The authors avoid SPICE simulation due to mixed-domain system complexity. SchGen outperforms ablations and larger frontier models (GPT-5.2, GPT-o4mini, Grok-4) on the benchmark: e.g., SchGen achieves 82.00% valid circuits, netlist Jaccard 49.08, precision 54.87, recall 52.80, and 60.5% expert functional correctness. On an unseen GitHub dataset SchGen generalizes with 65.59% valid circuits and Jaccard 40.65. The results highlight the importance of the Code-L1 representation and chain-of-thought for generation quality.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes pcbGPT’s evaluation and comparator design rather than SchGen. pcbGPT uses datasheet-grounded reference implementations with optional parts and a FlexiblePin mechanism to allow interchangeable pin mappings when permitted. Generated schematics are compared to reference circuits using a deterministic comparator in three stages: component matching, net matching, and score computation. Component matching is two-pass (required then optional), only considers components with identical KiCad library and symbol names, computes connection-sensitive weights from pin-level neighborhood signatures, and resolves ambiguous assignments with the Hungarian algorithm. Optional reference components/nets that are not matched are ignored for scoring. Endpoint normalization handles symmetric two-pin passives and expands flexible reference pins. The comparator computes three subscores — component (Dice coefficient), attribute (value agreement), and connectivity (endpoint overlap) — combined using weights (α,β,γ)=(0.4,0.2,0.4). The system supports fully automatic synthesis from natural-language prompts and an interactive human-in-the-loop workflow. The excerpt does not provide explicit benchmark names or quantitative results for performance.

contexts.context: SchGen is a finetuned LLM (GPT-oss-20b) that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests by using a semantic-grounded code representation. The approach replaces verbose, geometry-heavy schematic files with a compact set of editing primitives (add symbol, connect pins, query pin locations) and APIs that encode spatial relations via local coordinates and relative offsets and connect nets by semantic pin names (e.g., VCC, TXD). Inputs are user textual functional requests; outputs are executable Python-like code representations of schematics that can be converted to netlists and downstream PCB layout/fabrication files. To address data scarcity, the authors build a large dataset via a human–agent pipeline that converts open-source PCB designs into their representation and synthesizes prompts. Evaluation covers design validity, spatial layout quality, netlist accuracy, and expert verification. On these benchmarks, their Code-L1 representation yields an 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness versus a 32% valid rate from raw KiCad file baselines.
contexts.question: SchGen PCB schematic generation with semantic-grounded code representations: what is the approach, inputs, outputs, benchmarks and results?
