contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-driven pipeline that translates natural-language PCB schematic descriptions into executable SKiDL code and downstream KiCAD schematic/layout files. Inputs are natural-language task descriptions (digital, analog, power, or mixed). SKiDL was chosen as the semantic-grounded code representation because Python-based hardware description languages provide high information density. The approach emphasizes prompt design to improve LLM comprehension, circuit connectivity awareness, and adaptability. For correctness, the system constructs an IC datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) encoding device rules and constraints, and uses a multi-stage verification stack including electrical design rules and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) topology checks; verification feedback is returned to the LLM for iterative refinement. The KG compresses typical datasheet token footprints (≈16k tokens) to about 300 tokens per component, a 30–70× context reduction while preserving functional/electrical constraints. The method also defines semantic pin roles (mapping pins to one of 36 roles) to decouple verification from vendor pin naming. The excerpt notes there is currently no scalable functional/electrical performance verifier, so datasheet-grounded structural verification is used as a practical abstraction. No numeric layout-generation accuracy benchmarks or end-to-end task metrics are provided in the excerpt.
contexts.question: SchGen PCB schematic generation with semantic-grounded code representations: what is the approach, inputs, outputs, benchmarks and results?

snippets: We proposed a sophisticated prompt design methodology to enhance the LLM’s comprehension proficiency, circuit connectivity awareness, and learning adaptability.

To improve generation accuracy, we proposed a multi-stage verification framework based on IC datasheet driven Knowledge Graph (KG), electrical design rules, and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) checking for topology verification.

We built KG by encoding essential device rules and constraints in a structured form, reducing the token footprint per IC component from approximately 16k tokens (typical datasheet length) to average 300 token, achieving a 30 to 70 times reduction in context usage while preserving functional and electrical constraints.
However, We select SKiDL as the target representation because Python-based hardware description languages offer the highest information density.
User inputs natural language description of PCB schematic design task, it can be digital, analog or power task, or mix of them.

no scalable functional or electrical performance verifier currently exists, making datasheet-grounded structural verification a necessary and practical abstraction.

contexts.context: SchGen is an LLM system for PCB schematic generation that is finetuned from a 20B-parameter model (GPT-oss-20b). The approach centers on a semantic-grounded code representation (Code-L1) that encodes schematic editing primitives, relative placement, and pin-level connectivity instead of raw KiCad text or raster images. Inputs are natural-language user prompts; outputs are editable schematic code representations that can be materialized into KiCad schematics and netlists. The authors built a dataset (1390 schematic types) from open-source resources via a human–agent pipeline, using KiCad as the target format. Evaluation uses metrics for design validity, spatial layout quality, netlist accuracy, and expert verification. Results show the Code-L1 representation yields an 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness, versus only 32% valid circuits using raw KiCad baseline. SchGen matches or exceeds larger frontier LLMs (e.g., matching GPT-5.2 netlist accuracy) and achieves the highest performance despite its smaller 20B size, with demonstrated generalization to out-of-distribution GitHub projects.

snippets: SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations
Built on our representation and dataset, we finetune GPT-oss-20b to obtain SchGen.
We evaluate SchGen performance on a comprehensive set of metrics spanning design validity, spatial layout quality, netlist accuracy, and expert verification.
our proposed Code-L1 representation achieves 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness
compared to only 32% valid circuits from the raw KiCad file baseline.
prompted with the same APIs, including GPT-5.2, SchGen achieves the highest performance despite having only 20B parameters.
We present the new task of PCB schematic generation from natural language and develop SchGen, the first LLM that generates editable PCB schematics from user prompts.

We propose a semantic-grounded code representation that abstracts schematic design into structured editing operations with relative placement and pin-level connectivity to facilitate semantics-driven matching.
We construct a large-scale dataset of PCB schematics paired with user prompts via a human–agent collaborative pipeline that converts open-source designs into executable code representations.
Throughout this paper, we use the KiCad schematic format because it is open source.

A schematic contains three main item types: (1) component symbols representing physical circuit components such as chips, resistors, capacitors, (2) power symbols representing rails such as VCC for power source, GND for ground, (3) net labels that represent named net connections.
The schematic design workflow typically starts by selecting the required symbols, then placing them to form a readable layout, and finally wiring the pins to realize the intended circuit.
we have constructed a dataset of 1390 different schematic types using the open-source online design resources on Spark-fun [SparkFun Electronics, 2025] under the CC BY-SA 4.0 license, as a reference.

contexts.context: The paper proposes PCBSchemaGen, a training-free, constraint-guided pipeline for generating PCB schematics by combining LLM code proposals with formal verification. The approach uses an LLM as a proposal generator for SKiDL (Python-style) code via domain-specific prompts and reasoning techniques (CoT, ICL). Generated proposals are validated by a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) and a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) verifier that encodes pin-role semantics and topological/pin-package constraints derived from real IC datasheets. Inputs to the system include IC datasheets, constraint specifications/knowledge graph, and task prompts; outputs are SKiDL code representations of schematics (i.e., netlists/code that can be captured as schematics). The authors evaluate the system on an extensive benchmark of 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains. Results reported show that PCBSchemaGen "significantly improves design accuracy and computational efficiency." All code, KG, and benchmarks are open-sourced at the linked repository for reproducibility. This makes the method semantically grounded (datasheet/KG) and verifiable (SI-based checking) while avoiding large-scale model fine-tuning.

contexts.context: SchGen represents PCB schematics as executable, semantic-grounded code rather than raw KiCad text or images. The approach abstracts schematic creation as a sequence of editing APIs (add_schematic_symbol, add_label, get_pin_location, connect_pins, write_out_all_wires) that capture placement, labeling, pin queries and connectivity. To reduce LLM difficulty with spatial reasoning, placements use local (relative) coordinates anchored to central symbols or pins rather than absolute coordinates. Executing the generated code produces valid KiCad schematic files. Dataset construction uses an LLM sketching + human correction pipeline from online open-source PCB designs and trains SchGen on user prompts paired with these code representations. Representations are benchmarked using Minimum Description Length (MDL), Lempel–Ziv complexity (LZ Norm), and validation loss (Val Loss). The proposed Code-L1 (with relative coordinates and pin-name-based connectivity) achieves the lowest MDL/LZ/Val Loss compared to variants that remove relative coordinates (Code-L2) or pin-name connectivity (Code-L3), with example reported values for Code-L1 around 2.19/2.31 (MDL), 1.36/1.42 (LZ Norm), and 1.29/0.25 (Val Loss), indicating a more structured, learnable representation and better generation-to-file validity.

contexts.context: The paper describes a DRL-based chip floorplanning approach that uses a graph neural network (GNN) for state representation. The GNN encodes node features (module dimensions, current position, pin count), edge features (connection strength, criticality), and global features (utilization, total length, timing information). The agent is trained with a composite reward R combining HPWL, area utilization, timing violations, and an exploration bonus: R_t = -α * HPWL_t - β * Area_t - γ * Timing_Violations_t + δ * Exploration_Bonus_t. To mitigate sparse rewards, an exploration bonus based on reconstruction error L_Rec = ||f(B̂ ) - f(B)||^2 is used. Training employs Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with curriculum learning and embedded expert heuristics (e.g., masking actions to place macros at margins). Experiments use ISPD 2005 benchmarks (circuit characteristics listed) and are run on a cluster with NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs using PyTorch 1.9.0 and PyTorch Geometric. Reported results include Table 4 per-circuit HPWL improvements (e.g., adaptec1: 84,905,888 vs DREAMPlace 128,927,038, 34.14% improvement) and Table 6 averages where "Our Method" shows lower Avg. HPWL (145,010,125) and moderate runtime (5.8 hours) versus baselines (DREAMPlace Avg. HPWL 211,470,939).

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a chip floorplanning DRL approach that uses a trained R-GCN as a circuit encoder for the RL policy (removing final FC layers to reuse embeddings) and trains the agent with a masked PPO to enforce positional constraints. The layout is discretized to a 32×32 grid and the agent chooses a block lower-left cell plus one of 3 shapes, yielding an action space of size 3×32×32=3072; placements are ordered heuristically by decreasing block size. Positional masking uses three masks (one per shape) formed by combining occupancy and symmetry/alignment constraint matrices; additional grid-based masks include partial placement, wire-increase (fw), and a dead-space mask fds computed as the incremental empty-space increase. The 6 masks are concatenated into a 6×32×32 tensor and fed to a CNN (conv layers then an FC producing a 512-dimensional embedding), which is concatenated with R-GCN embeddings for the value and policy networks. The policy has an FC then 3 deconvolution layers to produce action probabilities. Reward shaping gives intermediate reward rt = −(∆ds + ∆HPWL) and a final reward combining area, HPWL and aspect-ratio error with weights α,β,γ=(1,5,5). The excerpt does not report benchmark datasets, numeric results, or comparisons.

contexts.context: The paper proposes a chip floorplanning method that combines deep reinforcement learning (DRL) with graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach represents the chip netlist with GNNs to capture topological information and uses a three-head network (policy, cost, and reconstruction heads) to improve feature extraction and decision making. A multi-objective reward function balances wire-length minimization (HPWL), area utilization, and timing constraints; an exploration bonus derived from reconstruction error is added to mitigate sparse rewards. Training strategies aim to improve scalability and generalization. The method was evaluated on ISPD 2005 benchmarks and reported state-of-the-art performance: an average 31.4% reduction in half-perimeter wire length (HPWL) and a 34.2% reduction in breach time versus the best baseline. The authors also report experiments assessing scalability and robustness across different circuits and perturbations.

snippets: This paper presents a new method for chip floorplanning optimization using deep learning (DRL) combined with graph neural networks (GNNs).
Three-head network architecture, including a policy network, cost network, and reconstruction head, is introduced to improve feature extraction and overall performance.
GNNs are employed for state representation and feature extraction, enabling the capture of intricate topological information from chip netlists.
A carefully designed reward function incorporating wire length minimization, area utilization, and timing constraint satisfaction guides the DRL agent toward high-quality floorplan solutions.
An exploration bonus based on reconstruction error addresses the sparse reward problem.
Extensive testing of the ISPD 2005 benchmarks demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed approach, consistently operating on a state-of-the-art basis.
Significant improvements include an average 31.4% reduction in half-perimeter wire length (HPWL) and a 34.2% reduction in breach time compared to the best baseline performance.
The process scalability and robustness are evaluated, showing performance in various circuits and different perturbations.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a chip placement/floorplanning method that cyclically combines reinforcement learning (RL) and simulated annealing (SA). Placements are represented as Sequence Pairs in a custom environment; the RL agent generates a Sequence Pair which is then used to initialize SA. Training/testing protocol: for ami49 the framework is trained for 10 epochs, with the agent taking 200 steps followed by 5000 SA steps; for lattice experiments they run 15 epochs with the same per-epoch step counts. Runtime: each training epoch averages 17 seconds, RL+SA initialization plus 5000 SA steps averages another 17 seconds, while pure SA for 5000 steps averages 16 seconds. Benchmarks and results reported: lattice problems of various sizes (100, 225, 400, 625 blocks) where RL initialization yields lower average cost than random initialization (e.g., 100 blocks: 3,386 vs 3,532). ami49 benchmark with/without fixed blocks also shows improvement (w/ fixed: 49 vs 53; w/o fixed: 43 vs 46). Overall, preliminary results show the RL-initialized SA outperforms pure SA with random starts on these benchmarks.

contexts.context: The paper proposes a DRL-based chip floorplanning method that uses graph neural networks (GNNs) for state representation and feature extraction. The agent architecture is a three-head network (policy, value, reconstruction) and uses a reward that combines wire-length minimization, area utilization, and timing constraint satisfaction; an exploration bonus based on reconstruction error is added to mitigate sparse rewards. Experiments are run on the ISPD 2005 benchmark suite with a case study on the adaptec3 circuit. Quantitative results (Table 6) show the proposed method achieving average HPWL = 145,010,125, density = 0.92, runtime ~5.8 hours, and timing violations = 127, outperforming baselines DREAMPlace, RePlAce, and DeepPlace (e.g., DREAMPlace HPWL = 211,470,939). The paper reports an average 31.4% HPWL reduction and 34.2% reduction in timing violations versus the best baseline. Scalability analysis (Table 7) gives runtimes and relative HPWL improvements across scales: Small (<100k modules) 1.2h / 28.5% improvement; Medium 4.7h / 23.7%; Large 8.9h / 19.2%; Very Large (>1M) 15.6h / 15.8%. Limitations include substantial training compute requirements and a fixed action space that may restrict placement flexibility.

contexts.context: The paper proposes a chip floorplanning approach that combines a pre-trained relational GCN (R-GCN) encoder with a reinforcement learning (RL) policy (masked PPO). The R-GCN is trained in a supervised fashion to predict placement rewards from circuit graphs: it uses node features (area, transistor params, pin counts, functional one-hot), models multiple relation types (connectivity, alignment, symmetry), and the architecture consists of 4 R-GCN layers, node mean aggregation, then 5 FC layers. The R-GCN training dataset contains 21,600 floorplans and rewards generated by SA/GA/PSO optimizers; the supervised loss is MSE. The RL agent state combines the R-GCN graph embedding, 32-dimensional instance embeddings, and pixel-level CNN maps on a 32×32 grid (including reward masks fds, fw and positional masks fp ∈{0,1}3×32×32). Actions choose one of three precomputed shapes and a grid cell for the block's lower-left corner; rewards are negative increases in proxy wirelength/empty space and an end-of-episode negative weighted sum of area, half-perimeter wirelength, and aspect-ratio discrepancy. The trained pipeline is integrated into ANAGEN and is reported to outperform traditional metaheuristics and prior RL methods on area, proxy wirelength, speed and cost metrics, shortening design time while matching or surpassing manual layouts. Numeric benchmark values are not included in the excerpt.

snippets: In particular, we train an R-GCN model to predict rewards for circuit placements.
The R-GCN model encodes the structural and topological information of the circuit and structures contained in it, providing the RL agent with an enriched state representation.
The agent then selects shapes for circuit components and places them onto a discretized grid, which serves as a representation of the layout space.

States: The state st combines detailed information encoded by the R-GCN model, including the circuit graph g, current instance nk 32-dimensional embeddings, and local feature maps extracted at the pixel level by a CNN.
32×32 grid representation fg ∈{0, 1}32×32,

Actions: The action at at time step t consists in selecting one of three possible shapes for the current block bt, which have been precomputed using the technique described in Section 5.3.1, and determining the grid cell to place its lower left corner.

Rewards: We define a partial reward rt as the negative increase of proxy wirelength and empty space in the floorplan after at. The end of episode reward is instead equal to the negative weighted sum of floorplan’s area, half-perimeter wirelength and, if specified, discrepancy w.r.t. the target aspect ratio.

Figure 6.3 shows the R-GCN model architecture, which consists of 4 R-GCN layers followed by a node mean aggregation block to produce the whole graph embedding. This is then fed into 5 FC layers to predict the reward value.

The R-GCN training dataset comprises 21600 floorplans and corresponding reward values, generated in 40 hours by optimizing placement w.r.t. area and proxy wirelength metrics using a mixture of SA, GA, and PSO.
Finally, the supervised model is trained to minimize the mean squared error between the ground truth and predicted reward associated to the input circuit graph.
we decided to train the RL agent using a masked version of PPO in order to properly apply the positional mask.

We validate our novel method on circuits of increasing complexity, showing that our approach outperforms traditional metaheuristics and existing RL-based methods in terms of area, proxy wirelength, speed and cost metrics.
Our enhanced procedural layout generation pipeline proves to consistently shorten design timeframes while matching and even surpassing the quality of manually designed layouts.

contexts.context: The paper describes a chip floorplanning method that combines a relational graph convolutional network (R-GCN) with deep reinforcement learning. The R-GCN is trained to predict placement rewards and to encode circuit, device, and geometric constraints; its output is used as an encoder for an RL agent. The RL agent additionally consumes spatial, pixel-level encodings from a CNN and learns an absolute-placement policy that selects component shapes and positions while enforcing no-overlap, symmetry, alignment, and other constraints. The approach improves generalization across different circuit topologies compared to earlier methods that required a separate model per component count. Evaluations were performed on six industrial circuits; the pipeline "surpassed established floorplanning techniques in terms of speed, area and half-perimeter wire length." Reported results include an overall layout time reduction of 67.3% and an 8.3% mean area reduction versus manual layout. In a case study (OTA), automated tooling reduced template generation and refinement times by ~99.9% and ~95.8% respectively, and produced 13.8% smaller layout area and 14.9% shorter wirelength than manual design.

contexts.context: The paper models chip floorplanning as a sequential decision process (an MDP (S,A,P,R)) where states encode current placements and metrics, actions place a module at (x,y), and transitions are deterministic. The DRL architecture uses a shared GNN encoder to process the chip netlist graph (nodes = modules, edges = connections), producing learned node representations that feed three heads: a policy network (π(a|s)) for action probabilities, a value network (V(s)), and a reconstruction head that recovers a visual placement representation. GNN input features include node features (module dimensions, current position, pin count), edge features (connection strength, criticality), and global features (utilization, total length, timing information). The reward is a composite function targeting wire-length minimization, area utilization, and timing constraint satisfaction. The excerpt describes the model design, state/action definitions, network architecture, and feature/reward design but does not include experimental benchmarks, datasets, or quantitative results in the provided pages.

snippets: The chip floorplanning optimization problem can be formalized as a sequential decision-making process, where the goal is to find an optimal arrangement of modules on the chip canvas.
The problem can be represented as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), defined by the tuple (S, A, P, R)
State (S)            The current placement of modules, utilization, length
Action (A)            Place module mi at position (x, y)
Transition (P)         Deterministic based on action
The proposed deep reinforcement learning network architecture for chip floorplanning optimization consists of three main components: a policy network, a value network, and a reconstruction head.
The architecture comprises three main branches: the policy network, value network, and reconstruction head.
The input features are processed through a shared graph neural network (GNN) encoder, followed by separate fully connected layers for each branch.
We employ a graph neural network (GNN) to capture the structural information of the chip netlist and extract relevant features for decision-making.
The chip netlist is represented as a graph G = (V, E), where V is the set of nodes representing modules and E is the edges representing connections between modules
Node Features           Module dimensions, current position, pin count
Edge Features            Connection strength, criticality
Global Features             Utilization, total length, timing information
The reward function is designed to guide the DRL agent toward optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously.
We define a composite reward function incorporating wire length minimization, area utilization, and timing constraint satisfaction.

contexts.context: The paper reports applied methods, findings, and recommendations for using generative AI (LLMs) in platform hardware/PCB schematic generation. Methods include controlling LLM sampling via the temperature parameter (trade-off: diversity vs. precision) and iterative LLM prompting—pointing out errors in regeneration reduces generation error. At the functional-to-component level, generated Verilog or high-level representations are mapped to vendor parts using BOM preferences; a modular mapping approach is recommended to enable reuse across platforms. For schematic placement, the workflow differs from layout: placement emphasizes visual understandability over pure wirelength minimization. The process relies on component dimension and pin-location data, begins by placing the most-connected component, aligns connected pins (with rotations/mirroring as needed), and uses offpage connectors when multiple pages are required. Generated designs are made editable for user corrections and can be exported/imported into PCB layout editors. Overall recommendations: tune temperature for desired diversity/precision, use iterative prompts to correct LLM outputs, map high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs, adopt modular reuse, provide accurate component/pin metadata, and produce editable outputs for downstream layout tools.

contexts.context: This pcbGPT study evaluates a generative-AI pipeline that translates natural-language requirements into KiCad-editable schematics using large code-capable models (e.g., gpt-5.3-codex). Empirically, the system attains high synthetic metrics (With gpt-5.3-codex, the system reaches an overall pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00) and can produce reviewable first-draft schematics that serve as useful starting points for development. Major failure modes are localized: supporting circuitry, values, interface details, and local topology. The authors position the tool as design acceleration rather than autonomous replacement, emphasizing an interactive web UI to keep humans in the loop for iterative inspection and refinement. Important system limitations include focus on schematic generation only (It does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation...), validation that ensures executability and plausibility but not electrical correctness, and exclusion of layout-dependent concerns (signal integrity, EMI, thermal behavior, etc.). Recommended next steps are model adaptation to improve electrical decision reliability, expanding the benchmark to larger and more diverse circuits, and studying interactive workflows and user-efficiency gains via user studies.

snippets: With gpt-5.3-codex, the system reaches an overall pass@1 of 0.90 and pass@5 of 1.00
The remaining errors are concentrated in supporting circuitry, values, interface details, and local topology
The strongest practical interpretation is therefore not fully autonomous design, but design acceleration.
The interactive web interface is an important systems feature because it connects the automatic generation system to how hardware design is actually carried out in practice.
pcbGPT can generate schematics that form a useful starting point for further development and iteration, even though performance still varies substantially with task complexity and model choice.

It does not automate placement, routing, manufacturing preparation, or fabrication-specific optimization, and it does not reason directly about layout-dependent effects that often determine whether a design remains robust beyond the schematic stage.
The validation pipeline establishes executability, rule-level consistency, and schematic-level plausibility, but it is not a guarantee of electrical correctness.

Effects such as signal integrity, EMI, thermal behavior, tolerance stacking, startup transients, and other layout- or operating-condition-sensitive issues remain outside the scope of the current system.

First, a natural next step is to investigate how model adaptation on schematic-generation tasks, component-integration patterns, and datasheet-grounded circuit knowledge affects reliability on the detailed electrical decisions that still cause many failures.

Second, the benchmark should be expanded to cover a broader range of circuit classes and larger multi-subsystem designs, enabling more extensive evaluation of both automatic generation quality and valid design variation.

Third, the interactive workflow should be studied directly with a focus on expert efficiency gains, for example through user studies that measure whether natural-language prompting, schematic inspection, manual editing, and project synchronization reduce the time and effort required for hardware-literate users to reach a correct design.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes SchGen, a 20B-parameter LLM designed for PCB schematic generation. Methodologically, the authors introduce a semantic-grounded code representation that converts schematics into a structured sequence of interpretable editing Python code, enabling the model to capture spatial reasoning and functional intent. They assembled training data via an agent–human collaborative pipeline and evaluated generalization on a held-out KiCad dataset of 988 unseen samples from 20 GitHub projects. Findings show SchGen substantially outperforms ablation variants and general-purpose models on valid circuit generation, spatial arrangement, and netlist accuracy, and its performance on unseen designs is reported as comparable to GPT-5.2 when GPT-5.2 is prompted with their code representations — despite SchGen being much smaller (20B params). Limitations and recommendations include: current focus on structured SparkFun schematics and lack of modeling for advanced PCB constraints; scaling to larger/complex PCBs is challenging due to dataset and model capability limits; and future work should target larger datasets, stronger models, more efficient pipelines, and maintaining human oversight to ensure robustness and safety.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses how AI/ML—and specifically generative AI—is increasingly integrated into electronic and SoC design flows, with applications relevant to platform hardware and PCB automation. It notes successful combinations of open-source EDA frameworks (e.g., OpenLane/OpenROAD) with ML to achieve earlier design optimization at RTL. Generative AI roles include architectural space exploration, optimized place-and-route, code optimization, autocompletion, and partial automatic code generation. ML techniques are used for combinational circuit generation, accurate power modelling, and pre-synthesis HDL quality verification. Practical enablers include FPGAs that can run full operating systems for edge platforms and the use of GPUs to accelerate ML workloads. From these points, useful methodological guidance for a study like Krishnamoorthy 2025 would be: integrate generative-AI modules into existing EDA flows (targeting RTL and place-and-route stages), leverage open-source toolchains (OpenLane/OpenROAD) for reproducibility, employ ML-based power and quality models to guide optimization, and use GPU-accelerated training/inference. Recommendations also include validating generated outputs via standard EDA verification (HDL checks, synthesis, power analysis), and considering system-level constraints (edge computing, FPGAs, Industry 4.0) when automating platform hardware/PCB designs.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses LLMs to generate SKiDL-format PCB schematics and a rule-based verification stage to enforce real-world IC package and pin constraints. Methodologically, it (1) frames schematic generation as LLM-driven code synthesis (proposal generator for SKiDL), (2) builds a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (KG) and uses Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) matching as a verifier to encode pin-role semantics and topological constraints, and (3) adopts a decoupled architecture so generation and verification are separate. The authors evaluate the system on 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains and report that PCBSchemaGen “significantly improves” design accuracy and computational efficiency. Recommended design choices implicit in the paper include leveraging domain-specific prompts, chain-of-thought and in-context learning for the LLM, using datasheet-derived KG constraints rather than purely simulation-based verification (e.g., SPICE), and open-sourcing code, KG, and benchmarks to support reproducibility. These points emphasize constraint-aware, interpretable verification and training-free LLM-assisted code generation for practical PCB schematic automation.

snippets: We introduce PCBSchemaGen, the first training-free framework for PCB schematic design that comprises LLM agent and Constraint-guided synthesis.
We built a datasheet-driven knowledge graph (KG) and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) based verifier to ensure the generated schematic correctness.
Training-free code generation paradigm: We em- ploy an LLM as a proposal generator for SKiDL code.
Since PCB schematics can be translated as SKiDL Python style code (Vandenbout, 2025) , our key ap- proach is to leverage LLM to be proposal SKiDL generator for code generation.
Results demonstrate that PCBSchemaGen significantly improves de- sign accuracy and computational efficiency.
This decou- pled architecture not only avoids reliance on large-scale training data, but also ensures the interpretability, reliabil- ity and reproducibility of verification through rules.

contexts.context: This paper introduces SchGen, an LLM system for generating editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests. It targets two key challenges: efficient schematic representation and data scarcity. The authors propose a semantic-grounded code representation that replaces verbose geometry-heavy formats with a compact set of editing primitives (add symbols, connect pins, query pin locations). Spatial reasoning is handled via local coordinate systems using relative offsets from anchor components, and wiring is specified by semantic pin names (e.g., VCC, TXD) rather than absolute coordinates. To create training data, they build an agentic pipeline that converts open-source PCB designs into editable KiCad schematics, synthesizes user prompts, and emits executable Python code. They finetune GPT-oss-20b on this dataset to produce SchGen. Empirically, the Code-L1 representation yields substantial gains: 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness versus 32% valid circuits from raw KiCad-file baselines, and the model outperforms alternative representations and larger general-purpose LLMs on connectivity and functional metrics. The work emphasizes that careful representation design is critical for applying generative models to complex hardware design tasks.

snippets: SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations
SchGen, the first large language model that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests.
We address two critical challenges: (i) efficient representation.
(ii) data scarcity.
semantic-grounded code representation
We define a compact set of editing primitives mirroring how experts draw schematics
local coordinate systems with relative offsets from anchor components
connect wires by semantic pin names (e.g., VCC, TXD) rather than absolute coordinates
we develop an agentic pipeline that replicates open-source PCB designs as editable KiCad schematics, synthesizes user requests, and converts each design into executable Python code.
Built on our representation and dataset, we finetune GPT-oss-20b to obtain SchGen.

Results confirm that our proposed Code-L1 representation achieves 82% valid circuit rate and 60.5% expert-verified functional correctness, compared to only 32% valid circuits from the raw KiCad file baseline.
Our results highlight the critical role of representation design in enabling generative models for complex hardware design tasks.

contexts.context: The paper reports that generative AI can produce structured artifacts (code, HDL) useful for platform hardware/PCB design, and that these techniques have already reduced design time and improved system optimization. Key methods discussed include structured-text generation with LLMs (zero-shot, few-shot, fine-tuning), industry uses of reinforcement learning and high-level command-driven generation, and the use of Verilog HDL as a practical representation for schematics. The authors found few-shot approaches effective when paired with domain descriptions and best-known methods (BKMs) to set context for LLMs. They note challenges specific to platform hardware: strict functional correctness, optimization needs, domain-specific constraints, and the necessity of verification and design reviews. Recommendations and directions include creating machine-consumable data and component libraries, using Verilog-level primitives to represent schematics, leveraging agentic/collaborative AI workflows for generation, critiquing and regeneration, and accounting for continual change requests to improve board designer productivity and reduce re-spins.

contexts.context: AMSnet-KG constructs a netlist dataset by extracting schematic images from textbooks and papers, then automatically detecting components, symbols, and nets to generate full SPICE netlists. The pipeline uses template matching to bootstrap labels, then trains deep-learning models in a semi-supervised loop to label remaining schematics. Orientation labels are recorded for each component orientation to infer connectivity and to order terminals correctly for SPICE (e.g., MOSFET drain/gate/source/body). Symbol pin functions are determined by marking pin numbers and using MLLM/GPT-4 for identification. Net detection assumes solid-line wires and that intersecting wires without a junction are not connected; grouping grows from component bounding-box pixels to neighboring wire pixels to form nets. For even-component intersections, a 2D convolution finds high-density wire pixels as intersection points which are added and the grouping repeated. The authors report 97% recall for component and symbol detection and 96% accuracy for net labeling. Exceptions and erroneous results are manually corrected to ensure data quality. The dataset supports LLM-based AMS circuit auto-design using knowledge-graph RAG.

contexts.context: The paper presents AMSnet-KG, a curated dataset of AMS circuit schematics and SPICE netlists organized as a knowledge graph with detailed functional and performance annotations. The authors use this KG to enable a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workflow for LLM-based automated AMS circuit design. The proposed pipeline: (1) formulate a design strategy from specifications (architecture and component counts), (2) retrieve matched circuit components from the KG and assemble a complete topology, (3) perform transistor sizing via Bayesian optimization, and (4) simulate the resulting netlist and feed simulation results back to the LLM for topology refinement. The approach is motivated by a scarcity of public AMS netlist datasets and attendant LLM hallucination problems; AMSnet-KG aims to mitigate this by providing high-quality, annotated examples. The paper reports case studies on operational amplifier and comparator design that verify end-to-end automatic design from specifications to netlists with minimal human effort. The excerpt does not provide quantitative performance metrics in these pages; the dataset will be open-sourced after publication.

snippets: To address this issue, this paper introduces AMSnet-KG, a dataset encompassing various AMS circuit schematics and netlists.
We construct a knowledge graph with annotations on detailed functional and performance characteristics.
Facilitated by AMSnet-KG, we propose an[cs.AI] automated AMS circuit generation framework that utilizes the comprehensive knowledge embedded in LLMs.
We first formulate a design strategy (e.g., circuit architecture using a number of circuit components) based on required specifications.
Next, matched circuit components are retrieved and assembled into a complete topology, and transistor sizing is obtained through Bayesian optimization.
Simulation results of the netlist are fed back to the LLM for further topology refinement, ensuring the circuit design specifications are met.
We perform case studies of operational amplifier and comparator design to verify the automatic design flow from specifications to netlists with minimal human effort.
However, the absence of high-quality datasets has led to issues such as model hallucination, which undermines the robustness of automatically generated circuit designs.
The dataset used in this paper will be open-sourced upon publishing of this paper.
Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for Electronic Design Automation (EDA) applications,

contexts.context: The paper presents AMSnet-KG, a knowledge-graph-centered dataset built from schematics, netlists, and structured annotations (JSON) to enable LLM-driven analog/mixed-signal (AMS) circuit auto-design. AMSnet-KG models entities as strings or circuit objects (including schematic, netlist, image attributes, and local annotations) and uses string relations to link them. The dataset contains 894 circuits with schematics, component bounding boxes, and netlists, and a focused subset covering OPAMPs, comparators, bandgaps, LDOs, ADCs and related testbenches. The proposed AMSgen pipeline uses KG-RAG: LLMs (prompted using in-context learning and chain-of-thought) produce design strategies and analyses, which are formatted into relation triplets. Those triplets are used to query the AMSnet-KG (e.g., via Cypher/Neo4j) to retrieve matching circuits and testbenches; retrieved components are assembled and simulated. The pipeline covers topology selection, automated transistor sizing, and topology regeneration if sizing fails, enabling an end-to-end flow from performance specifications to fully sized transistor-level netlists.
contexts.question: AMSnet-KG dataset for LLM-based analog mixed-signal circuit auto-design using knowledge graph RAG: what is the approach and results?

snippets: We take one step further and produce JSON files with LLM to introduce more structure into our annotation data.
In this work, we collect all schematics, netlists, and annotation data to create the AMSnet-KG dataset.
AMSnet-KG defines an entity as either a string or a circuit.
AMSnet currently contains 894 circuits with schematic diagrams, component bounding boxes, and netlists.
AMSnet-KG selects application-specific circuits and further include local and global annotations. It currently contains OPAMPs, comparators, bandgaps, LDOs, and ADCs, as well as related testbenches.
The data support from AMSnet-KG enables us to fully automate AMS circuit design, from performance specification to fully sized netlists.
Given a set of performance metrics, we obtain a design strategy from the LLM which outlines the circuit architecture as a set of circuit components.
We design a comprehensive prompt engineering framework, incorporating in-context learning (ICL) [8, 14] and chain of thought (CoT) [32], for generating circuit topology design strategy,

After receiving the LLM’s analysis and response regarding the design specifications, we format the LLM’s responses into relation query triplets to facilitate the retrieval of relevant circuits from AMSnet-KG, as illustrated in Fig. 12.

we perform searches within the knowledge graph using matching queries, such as those formulated in the Cypher language for use in Neo4j as follows:
  MATCH (node:circuit{input:’Differential’}) RETURN node ORDER BY ...

contexts.context: The paper presents AMSnet-KG, a knowledge-graph dataset of AMS netlists annotated with architectural descriptions, pin functions, and expert insights to support LLM-based analog/mixed-signal (AMS) auto-design. Motivation: LLMs can describe high-level design strategies but often fail at producing correct transistor-level netlists. The proposed AMSgen framework leverages LLMs to generate high-level architectures, then uses KG-based retrieval (KG-RAG) to find matching circuit components from AMSnet-KG and assemble complete topologies. Local annotations index pin functions and expert rules to guide automatic transistor sizing via Bayesian optimization, reducing the search space. If a generated design misses targets, AMSgen converts the design and measured performance into few-shot examples and iterates with the LLM to refine strategies. The reported application used 28nm technology and achieved various target performance specifications with minimal human effort. Key contributions are the AMSnet-KG dataset for retrieval and the AMSgen end-to-end flow combining LLM strategy generation, KG retrieval/assembly, automated sizing, and iterative LLM feedback.
contexts.question: AMSnet-KG dataset for LLM-based analog mixed-signal circuit auto-design using knowledge graph RAG: what is the approach and results?

snippets: AMSnet-KG dataset includes (a) circuit netlists, (b) corresponding annotations, and (c) an overall knowledge graph encompassing all circuits.
Although the state-of-the-art LLMs make mistakes in directly generating netlists, they have been trained with extensive AMS circuit knowledge in human languages.
Given design specifications, AMSgen starts by generating high-level design strategies, describing the circuit architecture using a number of circuit components.
Matching circuit components are efficiently retrieved from AMSnet-KG and assembled into complete a topology for simulation.
Afterward, guided by the local annotations to reduce the search space, AMSgen automatically optimizes transistor sizing through Bayesian optimization (BO).

if the generated design does not meet performance specifications, AMSgen formats the current design and achieved performance into additional fewshot examples, and returns to the LLM-based strategy generation step.
We construct AMSnet-KG, a comprehensive dataset thoroughly annotating AMS netlists with architectural description, pin functions, and expert insights.
We propose AMSgen, an automatic AMS circuit generation framework that produces fully sized circuits from performance specifications.
We apply AMSnet-KG through AMSgen and design circuits under a 28nm technology, achieving various desired performance specifications with minimal human effort.

contexts.context: The paper introduces AMSnet-KG, a knowledge-graph-formatted netlist dataset for analog/mixed-signal (AMS) circuit design that includes schematics, netlists, and manual annotations. The proposed workflow couples large language models (LLMs) with knowledge-graph retrieval-augmented generation (KG-RAG). Given performance specifications, an LLM generates a design strategy (e.g., topology) which is converted into relation-query triplets to retrieve relevant components and testbenches from the KG. Retrieved components are assembled into a circuit and parameter sizing is performed via Bayesian optimization using a formulated FoM. If the assembled design does not meet specs or is too large, the strategy and topology are iteratively refined. Two case studies (OPAMP and a strong-arm latch comparator) demonstrate the approach; results show that local annotations in the KG materially improve optimization convergence and final performance. For the comparator case, Bayesian optimization over a reduced set of free parameters (22 parameters reduced to 10 by symmetry) produced substantially better FoM and metrics when local annotations were used (FoM 2.89 vs 1.61; offset 3.50×10−5 V vs 7.98×10−4 V; delay 1.19×10−11 s vs 5.81×10−10 s; power 8.84×10−5 W vs 3.42×10−4 W). The authors plan to expand AMSnet-KG with more parameters, topologies, and improved performance modeling/sizing methods.
contexts.question: AMSnet-KG dataset for LLM-based analog mixed-signal circuit auto-design using knowledge graph RAG: what is the approach and results?

snippets: In this paper, we introduce a new high-quality dataset for AMS circuits, titled AMSnet-KG, which includes schematics, netlists, and manual annotations, all presented as a knowledge graph.
We also propose an AMS circuit topology design process based on LLM and KG-RAG.
This process begins from input performance specifications into LLM to obtain a design strategy (e.g., circuit architecture), which is then transformed into relation query triplets.
Relevant circuit components and testbenches are then retrieved from the knowledge graph.
After assembling a complete circuit using retrieved components, parameter sizing is finalized using Bayesian optimization based on design constraints.
If the resulting design fails to meet specifications (or costs too much circuit area to meet specifications), the design strategy is adjusted and the topology is refined.
We have experimented two case studies and obtained desired OPAMP and Comparator designs.
Again similar to the OPAMP case study, having local annotations significantly improves convergence time as well as final performance.
parameterized by a length and a width for a total of 22 parameters. After considering symmetry, the number of free parameters drop to 10.
Bayesian optimization trajectory for strong-arm latch comparator
Table 2. Designs optimized by Bayesian optimization

contexts.context: The paper demonstrates an LLM + knowledge-graph RAG workflow (AMSnet-KG) for AMS circuit auto-design. The LLM generates a design strategy (example: two-stage OPAMP with differential first stage, current-mirror load, common-source second stage, and Miller compensation). That strategy is converted into relation-query triplets which query AMSnet-KG to retrieve matching component netlists (e.g., differential pair, PMOS current mirror, common-source stage, bias, Miller RC). Local annotations on components are used to assemble the full netlist and to match testbench circuits. SPICE simulations (Cadence Spectre) are invoked programmatically to evaluate metrics. Sizing uses Bayesian optimization on a FoM combining normalized gain, CMRR, PSRR, log(GBW), and a phase-margin penalty around 60°; initial min/max values are taken from 100 simulations. Local annotations let symmetric transistors share (L,W), reducing free parameters from 19 to 15, which accelerates convergence and yields better final FoM and metrics (comparison table shows improved DM gain, GBW, CMRR, PSRR when using annotations). However, the optimized sizing for the initial topology still did not reach the target gain (66.21 dB vs 80 dB), motivating topology regeneration.

contexts.context: The AMSnet-KG work presents Amsnet-kg, a netlist dataset intended to enable LLM-based analog/mixed-signal (AMS) circuit auto-design using a knowledge-graph (KG) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach. The pipeline extracts relationships from detailed circuit descriptions and encodes them as triplets (e.g., component types, roles, locations, functions) to populate a KG that can be queried by LLMs. The paper shows few-shot prompt examples where performance specifications (28nm process constraints) are analyzed and mapped to topology design recommendations: an op-amp example (DC Gain > 80dB, GBW > 10MHz, Phase Margin > 60°, CMRR/PSRR > 80dB) and a comparator example (propagation delay <1ns, offset <100µV, 1GHz sampling, <100µW power). LLM responses produce structured design strategies (input stages, gain/latch stages, compensation), concrete circuit-structure descriptions, and triplet outputs for KG ingestion. The excerpt demonstrates methodology and qualitative outputs (design strategies and KG triplets) but does not provide quantitative evaluation metrics or final performance results within these pages. Paper metadata: May 2025, peer-reviewed, 19 citations, DOI:10.1145/3736166.

contexts.context: AMSnet-KG constructs a netlist knowledge graph for LLM-based AMS circuit auto-design using a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) style pipeline. The authors extract relation triplets (standardized by few-shot examples) via an LLM and perform Cypher queries in Neo4j to retrieve circuit components. Retrieved components are assembled into full netlists by matching preserved net functions and net names (e.g., Vout to Vin). Sizing is formulated as a constrained black-box optimization solved with Bayesian optimization (BO), where local annotations in AMSnet-KG provide equality/ratio constraints to reduce the parameter search space (Algorithm 1 describes constraint-augmented sizing). If sizing cannot meet specs, topology regeneration uses the LLM again, now seeded with previous design performance as quantitative context to propose new topologies. Implementation details: YOLO-V8 for object detection during dataset construction, Neo4j for the KG, GPT-4 for queries, experiments on SMIC 28nm (device limits given), Optuna for BO, and a simulation budget limited to 2000. A case study: for an OPAMP spec the LLM proposed a two-stage opamp (differential first stage with current mirror load, CS second stage, Miller compensation) and the strategy was converted to query triplets to retrieve circuits. The provided excerpt documents the approach and experimental setup but does not include final numeric performance results within these pages.

we formulate sizing as a black-box optimization problem and resolve it through Bayesian optimization (BO) with local annotations retrieved from AMSnet-KG.

Algorithm 1: Constraint-Augmented Sizing for Analog Circuit Design

In the case where an initial topology design does not satisfy the desired metrics after a set amount of sizing effort (or it costs too much circuit area for the desired results), we consider this a topology issue and begin regeneration.

With the previous design strategy proposed by the LLM and the optimal performance during simulation, we reverse the cause-effect and pretend that we initially wanted to achieve the performance metrics, and then correctly obtained the previous design.
We use YOLO-V8 for object detection during AMSnet-KG construction, Neo4j for knowledge graph implementation, and GPT-4 for various inquiries throughout dataset construction and circuit design.

We use Bayesian optimization (BO) implemented by the Optuna [2] library, and limit our total simulation count to 2000.

contexts.context: The paper describes AMSnet-KG: a knowledge-graph-backed netlist dataset and pipeline to support LLM-based AMS circuit auto-design (RAG). The approach collects large quantities of schematics and descriptions from textbooks and papers, uses a semi-supervised pipeline that annotates bounding boxes on a subset of pages and trains an object detector to extract schematic images, and applies template matching (annotating a single copy per component type) to identify components. A connectivity-detection algorithm converts detected components and nets into SPICE-style netlists. The extracted schematics, generated netlists, and detailed annotations are organized into the AMSnet-KG knowledge graph to enable retrieval for LLMs. Methodologically, the authors emphasize using LLMs for high-level design strategy (e.g., topology category) selection rather than direct netlist generation to improve robustness, and they introduce a universal format to store expert insights for transistor sizing. The excerpt documents dataset construction and workflow but does not provide quantitative evaluation metrics or experimental results in the provided pages.

contexts.context: The Hieplace approach is a hierarchical PCB placement pipeline that first preprocesses input to handle PCB irregularities and reduce solution space. It converts the 2-D chip outline into a 1-D segment for placing first-priority components, then applies a reordering (Algorithm 1) that prioritizes minimizing net crossings (considered more important than wirelength) and secondarily reduces HPWL. Components are selected for a constrained first-row layout by formulating selection as a 0/1 knapsack problem; component value is computed as val(i) = hic × 1 −wlic/lenchip, favoring larger components with shorter HPWL. Algorithm 2 performs DP-based selection; unselected components are interpolated with the x-coordinate of their succeeding ordered component. After priority placement, a relaxation/position-solving stage places grouped modules under boundary constraints, solving an objective (minimizing inter-module connection cost) with SLSQP. Reported qualitative outcomes include improved routability, reduced wirelength and crossings, better alignment for irregular boards, and runtime benefits from dimensional reduction and staged processing. Quantitative metrics are not provided in the excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HiePlace, an MP-based hierarchical PCB placement framework by Li et al. tailored to PCB-specific constraints (irregular outlines, priorities, alignment). To reduce the heavy computation of direct MP solutions, HiePlace applies two acceleration techniques: (1) a dynamic programming stage that prioritizes placement of core (large) components to reduce the solution space and improve placement quality; and (2) a relaxation algorithm that reduces the number of boolean variables, further narrowing the search space. The approach explicitly considers PCB-specific features such as irregular components, component priorities, and alignment. Reported experimental results for HiePlace show substantial improvements: a 7.7× speedup and a 66% reduction in cost (objective) compared to baseline methods. The work emphasizes that conventional VLSI placers do not match PCB needs, motivating this MP-based hierarchical approach with specialized accelerations.

contexts.context: The paper presents a double-layer PCB placement algorithm for IC-based modules that combines a mixed-variable model with a flexible legalization procedure. During legalization the algorithm removes overlaps by iteratively moving components (up/down/left/right) using an intermediate “middle” component and traversing neighboring components to eliminate overlaps. Top-layer legalization uses a partition-based approach; the basic idea is consistent with bottom-layer legalization but the top-layer is legalized based on region partitions and directional elimination within each partition. The authors propose two different legalization strategies to handle the differing placement principles of the two layers. Experimental validation used four industrial PCB cases (single- and two-layer instances) and fixed-outline floorplanning benchmarks; implementations were in C++ on an AMD Ryzen 7 5800H laptop. Numerically, the proposed method generally runs faster than Parquet 4.5 and IARFP and performs well on medium-to-large circuits (ami49, n100, n200, n300), though it underperforms the compared methods on the small-scale circuit ami33.

contexts.context: The paper proposes PA-ICM, an automatic double-layer placement algorithm for IC-based functional modules on PCB. The placement is modeled as a mixed-variable optimization problem with a top-layer centralized placement subproblem and a bottom-layer pin-oriented placement subproblem. The approach is two-stage: a global-placement stage that permits partial overlap and concurrently optimizes module rotation angles and positions via a hybrid algorithm (DEA-PPM + CSA), and a legalization stage that eliminates overlaps and enforces diverse space rules. The method uses centralized strategies for top-layer placement and a pin-oriented global placement based on bin clustering for bottom-layer optimization. A cluster-based optimization strategy (cluster-wise initialization, batch optimization, and corresponding legalization) further improves performance. Numerical comparisons report that the proposed mixed-variable optimization scheme achieves optimized total wirelength. Experimental results on several industrial PCB cases are reported (details not provided in the excerpt).

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Li et al.'s two-layer PCB placement approach: they transform constrained placement into unconstrained weighted objectives combining HPWL (wirelength), overlap penalty (square root of overlapping area) and boundary-violation penalty for the top layer (min f = α1 W + β1 sqrt(D) + γ1 B) and a simpler objective for the bottom layer (min f = α2 W + β2 sqrt(D)). Wirelength uses HPWL formula; overlaps are computed from per-axis pairwise overlap terms Oi,j(x,r) and Oi,j(y,r) with component effective widths/heights ˆwi,ˆhi depending on orientation. Optimization uses two main methods: (1) a Conjugate Sub-Gradient Algorithm (CSA) to handle analytic non-smooth optimization of positions, and (2) a Distributional Evolutionary Algorithm based on a Population of Probability Model (DEA-PPM) to search orientation combinations. An initial placement phase (Algorithm 3 TopInitialization) computes top-layer area, approximates the contour, and seeds component coordinates before global placement refinement. The excerpt does not include numerical experiments, benchmarks, or performance results, so no quantitative results or comparisons are available in these pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the approach of Li et al.'s double-layer PCB placement algorithm rather than presenting numerical results. The method treats top- and bottom-layer components differently. For the top layer it enforces a centralized layout around an IC: an artificial external contour is built, netlist connections to IC pins are extracted, each component is attached to its nearest associated IC-pin (with special handling for 'close-to-pin' nets which are initialized directly), and coordinates are legalized so components lie outside the IC. For the bottom layer the algorithm clusters IC pins using DBSCAN to form groups; components are assigned to pins (randomly for initial attachment), noise pin points are handled by attaching nearby components or placing some components directly on those pins, and components bound to close-to-pin nets are excluded from subsequent global placement. Component clustering is performed in initial placement, then global placement and legalization run per-cluster independently and are finally integrated; legalization aims to eliminate overlaps and reduce wirelength and constraint violations. The excerpt does not include quantitative experimental results or performance metrics.

contexts.context: The paper formulates the two-layer (top and bottom) PCB module placement as a mixed-variable optimization problem and develops tailored strategies for global optimization and legalization for both layers. The method yields legal placements for top- and bottom-layer components: centrally located parts keep small offsets and relative positions to neighbors, close-to-pin components remain adjacent to their assigned pins, and bottom-layer parts are placed within feasible regions restricted by IC outlines. The algorithm handles unbalanced component distributions and produces legal placements with various component-space settings; it also produces few crossed jumper wires for top-layer connections. Experiments (ten independent runs) report HPWL and runtime statistics (examples: Case1 bottom: 9267.355 HPWL, Case2 top: 4667.297 HPWL) and show the algorithm achieves automatic placement in a couple of minutes with small standard deviations, indicating stability. Numerical comparisons claim the mixed-variable optimization outperforms metaheuristic methods based on representative structure codes. A noted limitation is that the density-based clustering step can concentrate IC-pin points into one cluster, making global placement more challenging; future work targets large-scale cases and integration into multi-module PCB design.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: Survey, as of 2025, the tools, services, and research methods that use generative AI / automation to perform the INTERMEDIATE step of the PCB design pipeline: taking a high-level circuit TOPOLOGY or block diagram, a list of COMPONENTS (or functional requirements/specifications), and producing a basic 'starter' board design that a downstream autonomous placement/routing engine (such as Quilter or DeepPCB / InstaDeep) can ingest. Concretely, the target output of this intermediate step is a captured SCHEMATIC with a complete netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical FOOTPRINTS, a Bill of Materials, and ideally a preliminary board OUTLINE and component FLOORPLAN, exported as native KiCad (or Altium/EAGLE) files. Because autonomous routers explicitly cannot start from a bare topology (they require footprints, a board outline, and design rules), this 'topology+BOM -> starter board' gap is the focus.

Cover specifically:
(a) commercial / AI-native platforms that claim to bridge concept or block diagram to schematic + floorplan, e.g. CELUS (CELUS Design Platform, CUBOs, Design Assistant), Flux.ai (AI schematic copilot), JITX (programmatic design / algorithmic floorplanning), Circuit Mind, Cofactr/Diadem, Quilter and DeepPCB's own input requirements and any pre-layout helpers; for each: what input it accepts (topology/block diagram vs. full schematic), what it outputs (schematic, netlist, footprints, BOM, board outline, floorplan), which ECAD formats it exports (KiCad?), whether it has a public API / CLI / scriptable interface vs. GUI/login-only, pricing/free-tier, and maturity.
(b) design-as-code / programmatic frameworks that can generate a footprinted, netlisted KiCad project from a higher-level description (atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, Horizon EDA, KiCad's Python / IPC API + kicad-cli, PCBmodE), and how much of board-outline/floorplan generation they automate vs. leave manual.
(c) recent (2020-2025) peer-reviewed / arXiv research on automatically going from netlist or topology to an initial PLACEMENT / FLOORPLAN / board outline (analog/PCB placement, constructive floorplanning, ML/RL placement, LLM-driven schematic-to-layout, force-directed or partitioning-based initial placement), including benchmarks and reported success rates.
(d) honest assessment of the gap: for a low-cost, open-hardware, mixed-signal motor + sensor control board (microcontroller driving stepper drivers, solenoids, servos, vibration motors, and an HX711/load-cell front end), how automatable is this intermediate step today, what still requires a human, and which tools are realistically usable from a headless, version-controlled, CI / coding-agent workflow (no interactive GUI).

For each tool/method give: what it is, the concrete input->output, ECAD/KiCad interoperability, scriptability/API availability, licensing/cost, maturity, and cited evidence (vendor documentation and ~8-15 recent peer-reviewed or arXiv papers). Conclude with which tools most credibly fill the topology+BOM -> KiCad-starter-board gap and feed cleanly into Quilter/DeepPCB.

# Bridging the Topology+BOM → Starter Board Gap for Autonomous PCB Routers: A 2025 Survey

## 1. Introduction: The Intermediate Step Problem

Autonomous placement and routing engines such as Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep require, as their minimum input, a complete PCB project containing a captured schematic with a netlist, real manufacturer parts with assigned physical footprints, a board outline, and design rules. They cannot start from a bare circuit topology or block diagram. This survey examines the tools, services, and research methods that attempt to bridge this "topology+BOM → starter board" gap as of 2025, covering commercial platforms (Section 2), design-as-code frameworks (Section 3), recent research on automated placement and LLM-driven schematic generation (Section 4), and an honest gap assessment for a representative mixed-signal board (Section 5).

---

## 2. Commercial and AI-Native Platforms

The following table summarizes the major commercial and emerging research platforms evaluated against the specific intermediate-step requirements:

| Tool/Platform | Input (what it accepts) | Output (schematic/netlist/footprints/BOM/outline/floorplan) | ECAD Export | Scriptability/API | Pricing/Licensing | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CELUS Design Platform / CUBOs / Design Assistant | High-level functional requirements, block-diagram-style intent, reference-design/library-driven selections; strongest when mapping requirements to known solution blocks rather than arbitrary bare topologies. Academic references describe automated reference-design processing and schematic generation around CELUS libraries. (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2) | Claimed/marketed: generated schematic drafts, component selection/BOM, design documentation; practical floorplanning/outline generation appears limited and typically downstream in partner ECAD. Best viewed as requirements/block intent -> schematic/BOM accelerator, not a full autonomous starter-board generator. (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2) | Altium-centric in practice; no strong public evidence of native KiCad export in the cited research context. | Primarily SaaS GUI/workspace; public headless API/CLI not prominent in available evidence. | Commercial enterprise SaaS; pricing not generally public. | Commercial and deployed, but evidence suggests strongest maturity in requirements-to-reference-design synthesis rather than headless topology->KiCad starter-board export. (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2) |
| Flux.ai | Usually starts from human-authored schematic/PCB or copilot-assisted editing in a browser ECAD; accepts natural-language assistance, component/library queries, and collaborative schematic edits more than pure block diagrams. | Browser-native schematic + PCB editing, shared libraries/BOM workflows; can help assign footprints and prepare PCB projects, but public evidence for fully automatic block-diagram -> complete starter board is limited. | Flux native; interoperability with standard ECAD formats exists, but KiCad-native round-trip/headless export is not its core story. | Web product; some integrations, but not a broadly documented public CLI-first workflow. | Commercial SaaS with team/business tiers; pricing changes over time. | Mature collaborative ECAD with AI copilot features, but not the most credible headless bridge for topology+BOM -> KiCad starter board. |
| JITX | Code/programmatic hardware specification using Stanza-based generators; best input is parameterized circuit/programmatic intent, not an informal block diagram. Related academic systems describe high-level HDL/generator flows that elaborate to concrete circuits and footprints. (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 45-47, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45) | Concrete circuit implementations, netlists, selected parts/footprints, manufacturable design artifacts; can automate repetitive schematic capture and some placement/floorplan logic through generators. | Exports to mainstream PCB CAD/manufacturing flows; KiCad is not the primary native target. | Strong scriptability/programmatic interface; this is the main value proposition. | Commercial proprietary. | One of the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, especially for reusable generator-based designs; credible for code-first starter-board generation, weaker for raw natural-language topology ingestion. (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45) |
| Circuit Mind | Natural-language / requirement-driven electronics design assistant focused on suggesting architectures and schematics; best for early concept exploration. | Schematic concepts, component suggestions, documentation; public evidence for complete footprinted ECAD projects with board outline/floorplan is limited. | Limited public evidence of native KiCad starter-project export. | Mostly GUI/web workflow; public CLI/API not clearly established. | Commercial; pricing typically by demo/contact. | Emerging commercial AI assistant; useful upstream, but not clearly proven as a full topology+BOM -> footprinted starter-board bridge. |
| Cofactr / Diadem | Supply-chain/BOM/procurement-centric inputs: AML/AVL, sourcing constraints, approved parts, lifecycle, inventory; not primarily a topology-to-schematic authoring tool. | Strong on vetted manufacturer parts, BOM enrichment, sourcing, compliance, lifecycle/risk data; does not itself appear to generate a complete captured schematic + floorplan. | Integrates with enterprise PLM/EDA data rather than acting as a KiCad project generator. | API/integration oriented on supply-chain data more than ECAD synthesis. | Commercial enterprise software. | Mature for BOM intelligence and part selection; useful as a component-grounding layer, not a complete starter-board synthesizer. |
| Quilter | Input is already a PCB project with netlist, footprints, board outline, constraints, and usually pre-placement context; cited PCB-placement literature uses Quilter as a placement/routing baseline, not as a block-diagram-to-schematic tool. HiePlace compares against Quilter on academic benchmarks. *Table: This table compares commercial platforms and recent research systems on the specific intermediate PCB-design gap: turning high-level requirements or topology into a schematic/netlist/BOM/footprinted starter board. It highlights which tools are real candidates for feeding downstream autonomous placement/routing engines and where the major limitations still are.*

### 2.1 CELUS Design Platform (CUBOs, Design Assistant)

CELUS operates primarily as a requirements-to-reference-design accelerator, mapping high-level functional requirements to pre-validated solution blocks ("CUBOs") drawn from its component library. Generative AI is applied to reference-design processing for embedded systems, where the platform classifies schematic elements and assembles designs from vetted building blocks (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2). The approach leverages iterative LLM prompting and maps high-level functions to vendor-preferred BOMs using modular reuse (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4). CELUS is strongest in the Altium ecosystem and does not prominently feature headless KiCad export or a public CLI. Pricing is enterprise SaaS. For the topology→starter-board gap, CELUS is most useful when the design maps to its known reference designs; it is weaker for novel or highly custom topologies.

### 2.2 Flux.ai

Flux.ai provides a browser-native ECAD environment with AI copilot features for schematic and PCB editing. It is best characterized as a collaborative design tool with AI assistance rather than a topology-to-board compiler. It does not prominently offer headless/CLI access or native KiCad round-trip export, making it less suitable for a version-controlled CI workflow.

### 2.3 JITX

JITX is among the most mature programmatic PCB design systems, using a Stanza-based hardware description language where generators can elaborate high-level parametric specifications into concrete circuits with selected parts and footprints. Related academic work on Polymorphic Blocks demonstrates how HDL-based systems can support block-level polymorphism (abstract-typed blocks refined into concrete implementations), electronics-model constraint propagation, and netlist export to layout tools (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 1-2, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 45-47, ramesh2023betterembeddeddesign pages 42-45). JITX is strongly scriptable and code-first, making it one of the most credible options for automated starter-board generation. However, it is commercial/proprietary, and KiCad is not its primary export target.

### 2.4 Circuit Mind and Cofactr/Diadem

Circuit Mind provides AI-assisted architecture suggestions and schematic concepts but lacks strong public evidence for complete KiCad starter-project generation. Cofactr/Diadem is supply-chain and BOM-intelligence focused—valuable for grounding component selection in availability and lifecycle data but not itself a schematic-capture or floorplanning tool.

### 2.5 Quilter and DeepPCB/InstaDeep: Input Requirements

HiePlace benchmarks explicitly compare against Quilter on academic PCB placement tasks, demonstrating that Quilter operates as a downstream placement/routing engine that consumes an already-prepared board design with components, footprints, nets, and board outline (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 3-4). Quilter achieved placement costs ranging from 19.46×10³ to 35.70×10³ on four academic benchmarks, with runtimes of 34–75 minutes, while HiePlace reduced costs by 76.7%–91.3% with 99.4%–99.9% runtime reduction (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11). DeepPCB/InstaDeep similarly requires board-level physical-design inputs. Neither tool fills the topology→starter-board gap; they are explicitly consumers of the output that this gap analysis seeks to produce.
